blocks.text: Elements of Design

A client has asked your agency to redesign their corporate website — the brief says it looks 'messy and unprofessional.' Before you open Figma or Adobe XD, you need a shared vocabulary. The five elements of design are the raw material every visual communicator works with: line, shape, space, texture, and colour. Year 12 builds on Year 11 knowledge by focusing on how these elements are deliberately combined and why their relationship to design principles determines whether a product succeeds or fails.
Line

Lines do far more than divide space — they direct attention, imply motion, and communicate emotion before a viewer reads a single word. Thin hairlines suggest precision and elegance (common in luxury branding); thick bold rules convey strength; diagonal lines create energy and urgency. In UI design, horizontal rules separate content sections while subtle vertical lines create column structure in data tables. Every line you place is a decision.

blocks.definition: A line that does not physically exist but is created by the eye connecting a series of elements — e.g., a row of icons or the gaze direction of a person in a photograph.

blocks.text: Shape

Shapes are closed areas created by lines, colour, or contrast. Geometric shapes (squares, circles, triangles) feel ordered, logical, and modern — which is why they dominate tech branding. Organic shapes feel natural, approachable, and human — used frequently in health and education design. In digital products, shapes define clickable regions (buttons), organise content into cards, and create visual hierarchy through scale differences.

blocks.heading: Real World Example
blocks.text: Google's Material Design system uses rounded rectangles (a blend of geometric precision and organic softness) for cards and buttons. The corner radius is a deliberate shape choice that communicates 'approachable but structured'.
Space

Space is the area within, around, and between elements. Positive space holds the content; negative space (white space) is everything else. Contrary to its name, white space does not need to be white — it is simply empty area. Professional designers use white space strategically: generous padding around a call-to-action button increases click-through rates; breathing room between paragraphs improves reading speed. Overcrowded designs signal amateur work.

blocks.definition: The empty area surrounding or between design elements. Used deliberately to reduce visual noise, improve readability, and guide viewer attention toward key content.

blocks.text: Texture

Digital design uses visual texture — the implied surface quality of an element — rather than physical texture. Subtle paper grain, noise overlays, fabric-like patterns, or brushstroke effects add depth and tactile interest to otherwise flat interfaces. Texture also carries cultural meaning: rough concrete textures suggest industrial or urban contexts; soft linen textures suggest premium lifestyle brands. Flat design (popularised by iOS 7 and Material Design) deliberately removed most texture in favour of clean, minimal surfaces.
Colour

Colour is arguably the most powerful element — it triggers emotional responses before conscious analysis. In professional product design, colour decisions are documented in brand guidelines and applied consistently across all touchpoints. Designers work with colour models (RGB for screens, CMYK for print), colour harmonies (complementary, analogous, triadic), and accessibility standards that require minimum contrast ratios between text and background.

blocks.definition: A deliberate combination of colours that are visually pleasing because of their relationship on the colour wheel — e.g., complementary (opposite hues), analogous (adjacent hues), or triadic (three evenly spaced hues).

A numerical measure of the luminance difference between foreground text and its background. WCAG 2.1 AA standard requires a minimum ratio of 4.5:1 for normal text to ensure readability for users with visual impairments.

blocks.heading: Industry Tip
blocks.text: Tools like Figma's built-in contrast checker, Adobe Color, and the WebAIM Contrast Checker let you verify colour accessibility instantly. Checking contrast ratios should be a standard step in any professional design workflow.

rows: Direct eye movement and create structure
Section dividers, data visualisation axes, UI borders
Group content and define interactive regions
Control focus and reduce cognitive load
Padding, margins, white space in layouts
Add depth and communicate brand personality
Background overlays, hero images, card surfaces
Evoke emotion and create visual hierarchy
Adobe Color, Figma styles, Canva brand kit

question.text: A designer places generous padding around a call-to-action button and leaves large empty areas between sections of a webpage. Which element of design is being deliberately used?
options: Texture
Line
Space
Shape
question.explanation: Space (negative/white space) is the empty area within, around, and between elements. Using it generously around important elements like buttons focuses attention and reduces cognitive load — a professional technique, not an accident.

question.text: A tech startup is designing a new mobile app. Explain how the choice between geometric and organic shapes in the app's UI might communicate different brand personalities to users.
question.explanation: Geometric shapes (squares, circles, sharp corners) communicate precision, logic, and modernity — appropriate for a data or productivity app. Organic shapes (rounded, freeform) communicate approachability, warmth, and humanity — appropriate for a wellness or social app. The choice should align with the intended brand values and target user expectations. (2 marks per shape type with justification.)

blocks.text: Principles of Design

Your design agency has received two poster concepts from junior designers. Both use the same elements — same fonts, colours, and images — but one looks polished and one looks amateur. The difference is almost always the principles: how those elements are arranged, balanced, and emphasised. The Year 12 syllabus focuses on four principles — balance, emphasis, dominance, and unity — and critically, how they relate to the elements you studied in Lesson 1.
Balance

Balance refers to the distribution of visual weight across a composition. Symmetrical balance places equal visual weight on both sides of a central axis — common in formal contexts like government websites or corporate annual reports, where the design communicates stability and authority. Asymmetrical balance uses different elements of unequal size or colour but achieves equilibrium through careful arrangement — a single large dark shape balanced by multiple small light shapes. Asymmetrical layouts feel dynamic and contemporary and are dominant in modern web design.

blocks.definition: The perceived heaviness of an element in a composition. Larger elements, darker colours, complex textures, and isolated elements all carry more visual weight than their opposites.
A third type of balance where elements radiate outward from a central point, like the spokes of a wheel. Used in logos, mandalas, and circular infographics.

blocks.text: Emphasis

Emphasis establishes what the viewer sees first. In a well-designed product, there is a clear visual hierarchy: a primary focal point commands attention immediately, secondary elements provide context, and tertiary content supports or supplements. Emphasis is achieved through contrast (a bright colour on a neutral background), proportion (a large element among small ones), isolation (an element surrounded by space), or placement (top-left or centre positions attract the eye first in Western reading patterns).

Contrast and proportion are the two key mechanisms for creating emphasis in the Year 12 syllabus. Contrast uses difference — size, colour, value, texture — to make one element stand out. Proportion uses relative scale — a large headline against small body text creates immediate hierarchy. A common exam error is confusing emphasis with dominance: emphasis is directional (it guides the eye), while dominance is about presence (one element visually controls the whole composition).

blocks.heading: Exam Note
blocks.text: The SCSA syllabus lists emphasis as including both contrast AND proportion as sub-mechanisms. When asked to identify emphasis in a design, explain WHICH mechanism is creating it — don't just write 'emphasis is present.'
Dominance

Dominance occurs when one element is significantly stronger than all others in a composition — it controls the viewer's eye and sets the visual tone. A dominant element is typically the largest, most colourful, or most centrally placed item. Well-designed products often use a single dominant element (a hero image, a bold headline, a standout call-to-action) to anchor the composition. Designs without a dominant element tend to feel indecisive — the eye has nowhere to rest.

blocks.heading: Real World Example
blocks.text: On Apple's product pages, the product photograph is always the dominant element: large, centrally placed, with minimal competing content. Every other element — headline, price, button — is subordinate. This makes the product the unambiguous focal point and aligns with Apple's brand values of simplicity and product focus.

rows: Colour, size, and position of elements
Two-column layout with equal visual mass on each side
Difference between elements draws the eye
Colour contrast, size contrast, texture contrast
Bright red button on a white background
A dominant large element against small ones
Large hero headline above small subtext
Full-bleed hero image anchoring a landing page

question.text: A web designer places a large, high-saturation product image in the centre of a landing page while all text and navigation elements are noticeably smaller. Which principle of design is most clearly demonstrated?
options: Balance
Unity
Dominance
Repetition
question.explanation: Dominance occurs when one element significantly overpowers all others in a composition — here, the large, saturated image controls the composition. Balance would require equal visual weight distribution; unity relates to cohesion across the whole design.

question.text: Identify TWO differences between emphasis achieved through contrast and emphasis achieved through proportion, using design examples to support your answer.
question.explanation: Contrast creates emphasis through difference in qualities (colour, value, texture, tone) — e.g., a white call-to-action button on a dark hero section. Proportion creates emphasis through relative scale — e.g., a 72pt headline next to 12pt body text. Contrast can involve colour or texture without size change; proportion involves size relationships specifically. (2 marks per mechanism with example.)

blocks.text: Unity and Typography

A national retailer has commissioned a new marketing campaign across three platforms: website, social media tiles, and printed flyers. The same products and brand, but three different design files and three different junior designers. When the campaign launches, the assets look completely different — different fonts, different spacing, different visual mood. The problem is a failure of unity. This lesson covers unity (the Year 12 syllabus principle that pulls all elements together) and typography (the design element with the greatest impact on readability and brand personality).
Unity

Unity is the principle that all parts of a design feel like they belong together — as though they come from a single intentional system rather than a collection of independent decisions. A unified design is visually coherent: the eye moves smoothly through it without jarring inconsistencies. Unity is not the same as monotony; variety can coexist with unity when elements share an underlying logic.
Proximity

Proximity is the primary mechanism for creating unity at the local level: elements that are close together are perceived as belonging together. A product image grouped with its name, price, and purchase button forms a visual unit. Separating those elements across the page breaks the unity and forces the viewer to work harder to understand the relationship. In UI design, proximity is enforced through consistent spacing systems — for example, Figma's 8pt grid where related elements are separated by 8px and distinct sections by 32px or more.

blocks.definition: A design principle stating that related elements should be grouped physically close together to signal their relationship and create visual unity within a composition.

blocks.text: Repetition

Repetition creates unity at the global level by reusing visual elements — colours, typefaces, shapes, icon styles, spacing patterns — consistently across a product or campaign. When a brand uses the same primary colour, the same heading font, and the same button style on every page of a website, the repetition signals coherence and professionalism. Brand style guides exist to formalise repetition rules: they specify exact hex codes, font names and weights, logo spacing, and approved imagery styles.

blocks.definition: A formal document specifying the rules for consistent visual presentation of a brand — including colour palettes, typography, logo usage, imagery style, and spacing systems. Sometimes called a 'design system' in digital contexts.

blocks.heading: Career Connection
blocks.text: Every major organisation maintains a brand style guide. Working within these constraints is a core professional skill — whether you are a junior designer at an agency, an internal comms officer, or a web developer building new pages. Tools like Figma's team libraries and Canva's brand kit enforce repetition rules automatically.
Typography in Professional Products

Typography is the design element with the greatest impact on a product's readability, tone, and professional credibility. Poor typography choices — mismatched fonts, inconsistent sizes, inadequate line spacing — are among the most common reasons designs are rejected by clients. The Year 12 syllabus covers five typographic properties: typeface, size, alignment, format, and spacing.
Typeface

A typeface (or font family) is a coordinated set of letterforms with a consistent visual character. Serif typefaces (Times New Roman, Georgia, Garamond) carry traditional, authoritative, and editorial connotations — preferred in print-heavy contexts like legal documents, academic publishing, and newspapers. Sans-serif typefaces (Helvetica, Inter, Roboto) feel clean, modern, and neutral — dominant in digital UI design. Display typefaces are decorative, used sparingly for headings and branding. Professional work typically uses a maximum of two complementary typefaces: one for headings, one for body text.
Size, Alignment, Format, and Spacing

Type size creates hierarchy — larger text signals importance. Consistent size scales (e.g., 12/14/18/24/36pt) across a product reinforce visual unity. Alignment determines how text sits relative to a margin or axis: left-aligned text is most readable for Western audiences and is standard for body content; centred text suits short headings and invitations; right-aligned is used for specific typographic effects or RTL languages. Format refers to weight (bold, regular, light), style (italic), and case (uppercase, sentence case) — used to create contrast and hierarchy. Spacing includes leading (line height, vertical space between lines), kerning (space between individual letter pairs), and tracking (uniform letter-spacing across a text block).

rows: The font family used — serif, sans-serif, display
Max 2 complementary families per product; align with brand guide
The scale of text in points or pixels
Establish a consistent type scale (e.g. 12/16/24/36px); headings 2–4x body size
How text lines up against an axis
Left-align body text; use centred text sparingly for short headings only
Use format to create contrast and hierarchy, not decoration
Vertical space between lines of text
1.4–1.6× font size for comfortable body text readability
Auto-kern headings; manually adjust display type at large sizes
Uniform letter-spacing across a text block
Increase tracking on uppercase labels; avoid over-tracking body text

blocks.heading: Industry Tip
blocks.text: Google Fonts (free, web-optimised) and Adobe Fonts (included with Creative Cloud) are the two primary professional sources for typefaces in digital products. Canva's built-in font library is convenient for quick projects. When choosing fonts for a client, always check the licence — some free fonts prohibit commercial use.

question.text: A designer ensures that all buttons across a website use the same corner radius, the same colour, and the same font weight. Which principle of design and which mechanism is primarily being applied?
options: Emphasis through contrast
Unity through repetition
Balance through symmetry
Dominance through proportion
question.explanation: Repeating identical visual properties (corner radius, colour, font weight) across all instances of a component is a direct application of unity through repetition. It signals visual coherence and professional consistency — the viewer learns the pattern once and recognises it throughout.

question.text: Explain how proximity and repetition work together to achieve unity in a multi-page digital document such as a business report created in Microsoft Publisher or Adobe InDesign.
question.explanation: Proximity groups related elements together on each page (e.g., heading with its body text, image with its caption), creating local visual unity within each section. Repetition applies consistent colours, fonts, and spacing rules across every page, creating global unity across the document. Together they ensure the document reads as a single coherent product rather than a collection of unrelated pages. (2 marks per mechanism with contextual application.)

blocks.text: Compositional Rules

A photographer has handed you 20 raw product shots for a new e-commerce website. Some feel dynamic and professional; others look flat and forgettable — even though the products are identical. The difference is composition: how elements are arranged within the frame. Compositional rules give designers a structured basis for making placement decisions. The Year 12 syllabus focuses on the rule of thirds and grid-and-alignment systems, plus the skill of annotating designs to justify compositional choices.
Rule of Thirds

The rule of thirds divides any canvas — a webpage, a poster, a photograph — into a 3×3 grid using two horizontal and two vertical lines. The four intersection points (often called 'power points') are the positions where the human eye is naturally drawn. Placing key subjects, focal points, or call-to-action elements at these intersections creates compositions that feel more dynamic and visually interesting than centring everything. Horizon lines in photography placed on the upper or lower horizontal line — rather than the middle — create stronger compositions.

blocks.definition: A compositional guideline that divides the canvas into a 3×3 grid and recommends placing key visual subjects at or near the four intersection points to create dynamic, balanced compositions.

blocks.heading: Real World Example
blocks.text: Most smartphone cameras include an optional 3×3 grid overlay — this is the rule of thirds grid. Professional photographers enable it by default. In web design, Figma and Adobe XD allow you to display custom grid overlays to guide element placement using the same principle.
Grid Systems and Alignment

A grid system is a structural framework of columns, rows, and gutters (the spaces between columns) that organises content into a consistent spatial structure. Grids are invisible in the final product but essential during design. Web design typically uses 12-column grids (popularised by Bootstrap and Material Design) because 12 divides evenly into halves, thirds, quarters, and sixths — giving designers maximum layout flexibility. A three-column editorial layout, a two-column product listing, and a six-column icon row can all coexist within the same 12-column grid.

Alignment means positioning elements so their edges or centres line up with each other or with grid lines. Even when alignment is invisible to casual viewers, misaligned elements create a subconscious sense of disorder. Consistent alignment is one of the fastest ways to improve an amateur design. In Figma and Adobe XD, smart guides and snap-to-grid features enforce alignment automatically. In print tools like Microsoft Publisher, margin and column guides serve the same purpose.

blocks.definition: The fixed space between columns in a grid system. Consistent gutters ensure even spacing between content blocks and prevent elements from crowding each other.

blocks.heading: Industry Tip
blocks.text: The 8-point grid (all spacing values multiples of 8px) has become a de facto standard in professional UI design. It ensures visual consistency across different screen densities and aligns with most icon sizes. Figma's auto-layout feature makes implementing an 8pt grid straightforward.
Annotating Designs

Annotation is the skill of labelling and explaining design choices — identifying which elements and principles are present and justifying why they were used. Detailed annotation is a Year 12 assessment requirement and a professional practice: in industry, annotated design mockups (called 'redlines' or 'specs') communicate design intent to developers and stakeholders. A strong annotation identifies the element or principle by name, describes how it manifests in the specific design, and justifies why it serves the design's purpose or audience.

items: Identify: Name the specific design element or principle (e.g., 'emphasis through contrast').
Describe: Explain how it appears in this particular design (e.g., 'the call-to-action button is rendered in a high-saturation orange against the grey background').

Justify: Explain why this choice serves the design's purpose or audience (e.g., 'the warm, high-contrast colour draws the eye immediately and prompts user action, which is critical for conversion on an e-commerce landing page').

blocks.heading: Exam Note
blocks.text: SCSA assessments require 'detailed annotations relevant to a particular design brief.' A one-word annotation ('balance') scores zero. You must identify, describe, AND justify each choice in the context of the specific brief and audience.

rows: Camera grid overlay; Figma/XD custom grid
Organise content into consistent horizontal structure
Bootstrap 12-col; Figma grid settings; Publisher columns
InDesign baseline grid; CSS line-height systems
Maintain consistent breathing room between columns
CSS gap property; Figma auto-layout gap
Ensure elements share invisible structural axes
Figma smart guides; Publisher alignment tools

question.text: A web designer is arranging a hero section with a product image and a headline. Using the rule of thirds, where would the most effective placement of the key subject be?
options: Centred both horizontally and vertically
In the top-left corner of the frame
At or near one of the four intersection points of a 3×3 grid
Along the bottom edge of the frame
question.explanation: The rule of thirds places key subjects at the four intersection points of a 3×3 grid overlay — these are the positions the human eye is naturally drawn to. Centring subjects is the common amateur approach; the rule of thirds creates more dynamic and visually interesting compositions.

question.text: A student annotates a poster design with the note: 'I used balance.' Rewrite this annotation to meet the Year 12 standard of 'identify, describe, and justify.'
question.explanation: A complete annotation might read: 'The principle of asymmetrical balance is applied: the large product photograph on the left side of the composition is visually counterweighted by the bold white headline text block and call-to-action button on the right, despite the different sizes. This asymmetrical arrangement creates a dynamic, modern feel appropriate for the youth-oriented fashion brand, while still ensuring neither side overwhelms the viewer.' (1 mark identify, 1 mark describe, 1 mark justify with audience/purpose reference.)

blocks.text: Applying Design to Digital Products

You are now a junior designer at a small creative agency. A new client — a local surf school called Coastline Surf Academy — needs a promotional digital flyer for their summer program. They want it to look 'fresh and professional' and be suitable for posting on Instagram and printing as an A5 flyer. This is a realistic design brief, and your job is to apply every concept from this topic — elements, principles, unity, typography, and compositional rules — in an integrated way to produce and annotate a complete design solution.
From Elements to Finished Products

Professional designers do not think about elements and principles as separate checklists — they are internalised as a shared vocabulary for making and critiquing decisions. When a senior designer reviews a junior's work and says 'it lacks emphasis' or 'the typography is fighting itself,' they are using this vocabulary to communicate specific, actionable feedback. Understanding these concepts lets you both produce better work and communicate about it precisely.

The relationship between elements and principles is fundamental: elements are what you use, principles are how you use them. Colour (element) applied with contrast (principle) creates emphasis. Shapes (element) repeated consistently (principle) create unity. Space (element) distributed with balance (principle) creates visual equilibrium. Every design decision involves at least one element and at least one principle working together.

blocks.heading: Career Connection
blocks.text: Graphic and web designers are consistently listed among the fastest-growing occupations in Australia's digital economy. The WA Department of Education's Applied IT pathway prepares students for roles including UI/UX designer, digital marketing coordinator, web developer, and multimedia specialist — all of which require fluency in design concepts as a baseline professional skill.
Choosing the Right Software

The choice of design tool affects workflow efficiency but not the underlying design principles — those apply regardless of software. For digital-first products (websites, apps, social tiles), Figma and Adobe XD are industry standard. For print-digital hybrid products like the surf school flyer, Adobe InDesign is the professional choice, though Canva handles simpler A5 flyer projects effectively. Microsoft Publisher remains available as a lower-barrier option in school and small business contexts.

apps.name: Figma
apps.note: Browser-based UI and graphic design tool. Industry standard for web/app design, prototyping, and team collaboration. Supports auto-layout, component libraries, and real-time co-design.

apps.name: Adobe XD
apps.note: Adobe's UI/UX design and prototyping tool. Deep integration with Photoshop and Illustrator. Supports interactive prototypes and developer handoff specs.

apps.name: Canva
apps.note: Template-based design tool suited to flyers, social tiles, and presentations. Brand kit feature enforces repetition/unity. Excellent for rapid client-ready deliverables.

apps.name: Microsoft Publisher
apps.note: Desktop publishing tool for print-focused products (flyers, brochures, newsletters). Strong grid and alignment guides. Accessible alternative for school and small business use.

blocks.title: Design Brief: Coastline Surf Academy
blocks.scenario: You have been contracted by Coastline Surf Academy to design a promotional flyer for their Summer Surf Program. The brief: the flyer must work as both an Instagram square post (1080×1080px) and an A5 printed handout. Target audience: 14–25 year-olds interested in outdoor activities. Tone: energetic, youthful, professional. Required content: academy logo area, program name ('Summer Surf Program 2027'), three key details (dates, price, location), and a call to action ('Book online at coastlinesurfacademy.com.au'). The client has specified a colour palette of ocean blue (#1A6B9A) and warm sand (#F5E6C8) with white (#FFFFFF) for text.

questions.question: Identify which compositional rule you would apply when placing the hero image and program name headline on the flyer, and justify your placement decision with reference to the design brief and target audience.

The brief specifies two brand colours (ocean blue and warm sand) plus white. Explain how you would use these colours to create emphasis AND unity in the flyer, identifying which specific elements receive each colour and why.

Select ONE typeface for headings and ONE for body text. Name each typeface, classify it (serif/sans-serif/display), and justify both choices with reference to the tone and audience specified in the brief.
Write a three-part annotation (identify, describe, justify) for ONE design principle you would apply in your flyer layout. Your annotation must reference the specific design brief requirements.

blocks.title: Practical Task: Design and Annotate the Flyer
blocks.software: Canva (free) or Figma (free education account) or Microsoft Publisher
blocks.intro: Using the Coastline Surf Academy brief above, create the promotional flyer and produce a detailed annotated version. This task mirrors the type of practical design task assessed in Year 12 Applied IT.
steps: Open your chosen design tool and create a new file at 1080×1080px (square, for Instagram use).
Set up a grid: enable a 3×3 rule-of-thirds overlay (Figma: View > Layout grids; Canva: enable grid from Elements panel; Publisher: use guides).
Apply the brand colours (#1A6B9A, #F5E6C8, #FFFFFF) to your colour palette or brand kit before placing any elements.
Select and apply your heading typeface and body typeface. Set up a type scale: heading (32–40pt), subheading (18–24pt), body (12–14pt).
Place a placeholder hero image (ocean/surfing theme) and position the subject near a rule-of-thirds intersection point.
Add all required text content (program name, dates, price, location, call to action) and arrange using your grid structure and alignment guides.
Apply the design principles: create a dominant element, ensure visual balance, use proximity to group related information, and use repetition to maintain unity.
Export a clean version (JPEG/PNG) for Instagram.

Create an annotated version: add text boxes or callout arrows identifying at least FOUR specific design decisions (one element, one principle, one typographic choice, one compositional choice), each with a full identify-describe-justify annotation.
Save both the clean and annotated versions. Peer-review a classmate's annotated version using the identify-describe-justify criteria.

question.text: A student's flyer design uses four different fonts, three different button styles, and inconsistent spacing between sections. Which design principle is most clearly violated?
options: Dominance
Balance
Unity
Emphasis
question.explanation: Unity is violated when a design lacks visual coherence — inconsistent fonts, button styles, and spacing all signal a breakdown in repetition (global unity) and proximity rules (local unity). The design appears to be assembled from unrelated parts rather than designed as a system.

question.text: Which statement BEST describes the relationship between design elements and design principles?
options: Elements and principles are the same thing described in different ways.
Elements are the tools you design with; principles are how you use those tools together.
Principles apply only to print design; elements apply to digital design.
You must apply all five elements and all four principles in every design.
question.explanation: Elements (line, shape, space, texture, colour) are the fundamental building blocks — what you design with. Principles (balance, emphasis, dominance, unity) are the rules that govern how elements are combined effectively. A principle cannot exist without elements to apply it to; elements without principles produce incoherent design.

question.text: Explain why the relationship between design elements and design principles is important when evaluating a digital product. Use a specific example of one element and one principle working together.
question.explanation: Elements and principles are interdependent: a principle cannot be applied without elements to apply it to, and elements alone do not produce effective design. For example, colour (element) used with contrast (principle) creates emphasis by making one element stand out from its surroundings — as when a high-saturation call-to-action button in orange stands out against a muted grey background, directing user attention and driving conversions. Evaluating a digital product means assessing not just which elements are present, but whether the principles governing their arrangement serve the product's purpose and audience. (2 marks for explaining relationship, 2 marks for specific element+principle example with justification.)

blocks.text: Computer Systems and Components

In Year 11 you covered the basics of CPU, memory, and storage. Year 12 takes this further — you are expected to apply this knowledge when advising on hardware purchases, diagnosing compatibility issues, and recommending the right system type for a given job role or business context.
The Central Processing Unit (CPU)

blocks.definition: The primary processor that executes all program instructions. It carries out the fetch-decode-execute cycle billions of times per second, controlling every other component in the system.

blocks.text: When selecting hardware for a business, the CPU specification matters for the intended workload. A reception computer handling email and scheduling needs far less processing power than a workstation used for video editing or 3D rendering. Over-specifying wastes budget; under-specifying creates bottlenecks.

rows: Independent execution units on the chip
More cores = better multitasking and parallel workloads
Larger cache reduces delays fetching data from RAM
Higher TDP needs better cooling; important in confined spaces
Newer = better efficiency and feature support

blocks.text: Memory: Primary and Secondary
Memory is divided into two categories based on its role. Primary memory is directly accessible by the CPU; secondary memory provides long-term storage.

items.definition: Volatile, high-speed memory that holds currently running programs and active data. Larger RAM allows more applications to run simultaneously without slowdown. Typical office PCs need 8–16 GB; workstations 32 GB or more.
Non-volatile memory that stores the system firmware (BIOS/UEFI). It initialises hardware at startup and cannot normally be modified by the user.
Very fast memory built into or adjacent to the CPU. Stores recently or frequently accessed data so the CPU avoids slower RAM lookups.

Non-volatile storage for the operating system, applications, and files. Persists when power is off. SSDs are now standard in most professional environments due to speed and reliability advantages over HDDs.

blocks.text: Types of Peripheral Devices

Peripheral devices extend the capability of a computer system. They are classified as input, output, or storage/communication devices. In a professional context, choosing the right peripheral for the role is a key IT support skill.

rows: Keyboard, mouse, scanner, microphone, webcam, graphics tablet, barcode reader
Scanners in medical records; graphics tablets in design studios; barcode readers in retail/logistics
Monitor, printer, projector, speakers, label printer
Dual monitors increase productivity in office roles; label printers in warehouses
Touchscreen, USB storage, network card, modem
Touchscreens in point-of-sale systems; network cards in every networked device
Essential for connecting to business networks and wireless peripherals

blocks.text: Types of Computer Systems

rows: High performance, upgradeable, not portable; separate components
Office workstations, CAD/design, reception desks, school computer labs
Portable, battery-powered, integrated display and keyboard; harder to upgrade
Field staff, students, business travel, flexible working
Touchscreen-first, very portable, limited local processing
Frontline workers (retail, healthcare), customer-facing kiosks, presentations
Mobile workforce, communication, field data capture
High reliability, large storage/RAM, designed for 24/7 operation; rack or tower form
School network servers, business file/print/email servers, web hosting
Minimal local hardware; relies on a server for processing
Call centres, school labs where central management is preferred

blocks.heading: Industry Tip
blocks.text: IT support technicians need to know which system type suits which role before they can advise management or complete a hardware request form. A single-purpose reception desk usually justifies a desktop; a travelling sales rep needs a laptop or tablet with a mobile data plan.

question.text: A business is setting up a call centre with 40 workstations. They want centralised management and the ability to control all software from a single server. Which computer system type is most suitable for each workstation?
options: High-end gaming desktop
Thin client
Server
Tablet
question.explanation: Thin clients have minimal local hardware and rely on a server for processing. This makes them ideal for centralised management — software and data are managed on the server, reducing IT maintenance at each workstation.

question.text: Explain the difference between primary and secondary memory. Give one example of each.
question.explanation: Primary memory (e.g. RAM) is directly accessible by the CPU, volatile (lost when power is off), and used for currently running programs. Secondary memory (e.g. SSD/HDD) is non-volatile, used for long-term storage of files and the OS, and is accessed more slowly than primary memory.

blocks.title: System Specification Analysis
blocks.intro: Read two hardware specification sheets (or product listings from a retailer website) — one for a budget office laptop and one for a mid-range workstation desktop. Answer the following.
steps: Record the CPU model, core count, and clock speed for each system.
Record the amount of RAM and storage type (SSD/HDD) for each system.
Identify which system you would recommend for: (a) a receptionist who uses email and calendar software; (b) a graphic designer who edits large image files.
Justify each recommendation with reference to specific specifications.
Calculate the price difference and explain whether the extra cost of the workstation is justified for each role.

blocks.text: Operating Systems

An operating system (OS) is the software layer that manages all hardware resources and provides a platform for applications to run. Without an OS, a computer is a collection of circuits with no way to receive user input, run programs, or communicate with peripherals.

blocks.definition: System software that manages hardware resources (CPU, memory, storage, peripherals), provides a user interface, and enables application software to function. It acts as the intermediary between hardware and software.

blocks.text: Core Functions of an OS

items: Process management — allocates CPU time to running programs
Memory management — controls which programs can use which areas of RAM
File system management — organises how data is stored and retrieved on drives
Device management — communicates with hardware using drivers
Security — manages user accounts, permissions, and authentication
User interface — provides GUI (graphical) or CLI (command-line) for interaction

blocks.text: Major Operating Systems

rows: Widest software compatibility, enterprise tools, Active Directory integration
Creative software ecosystem, Unix base, tight hardware/software integration
Closed ecosystem, strong security, App Store, optimised for touch
Consumer devices, corporate BYOD, frontline workers
Open platform, wide device range, Google services, customisable
Diverse business and consumer mobile use
Free, highly customisable, stable for servers, strong security
Web servers, developers, IT professionals, embedded systems

blocks.text: Choosing an OS for a Job Role
In industry, the OS choice is rarely just personal preference — it is driven by the software required for the role, existing infrastructure, licensing costs, and support considerations.

items.label: School IT administrator
points: Windows Server for file/print services and Active Directory user management
Windows 10/11 on student workstations for compatibility with curriculum software
Linux possible on servers for web hosting and cost savings

items.label: Graphic designer / video editor
points: macOS preferred for Adobe Creative Cloud, Final Cut Pro, Logic Pro
Windows viable with same Adobe software if budget is a constraint
Linux rarely used due to limited professional creative app support

items.label: Web developer / software engineer
points: Linux (Ubuntu) popular for its Unix toolchain, native terminal, and server parity
macOS also widely used for Unix-based development
Windows with WSL (Windows Subsystem for Linux) is a common compromise

items.label: Field sales / customer service
points: iOS or Android smartphone/tablet for CRM apps and communication
Windows laptop if detailed document work is required
Choice often dictated by company MDM (Mobile Device Management) platform

blocks.heading: Career Connection
blocks.text: IT support technicians need to be comfortable working across all major operating systems — not just the one they use at home. Many businesses run a mix of Windows desktops and Apple phones, or Linux servers alongside Windows clients. Cross-platform fluency is a key employability skill.
OS Licensing and Cost

Operating systems are not all free. Windows requires a licence per device (retail) or per-user (volume licensing for businesses). macOS is free updates but tied to Apple hardware. Linux distributions are generally free and open source, which makes them attractive for server deployments and organisations with large numbers of devices.

blocks.definition: A purchasing agreement that allows a business to deploy software on many devices at a reduced per-unit cost. Microsoft offers volume licensing through agreements like Microsoft 365 Business and Microsoft Open License.

question.text: A small business is deploying 15 new desktop computers. They need to run Microsoft Office and accounting software. The IT manager wants centralised user account management. Which OS is most appropriate?
options: Android
iOS
Linux (Ubuntu Desktop)
Windows 11 with Microsoft 365
question.explanation: Windows 11 with Microsoft 365 provides native compatibility with Office applications, centralised user management via Azure Active Directory or on-premises Active Directory, and is supported by most business accounting software.

question.text: Which operating system is most commonly found on web servers because it is free, stable, and highly customisable?
options: Windows Server
macOS
iOS
Linux
question.explanation: Linux (in distributions such as Ubuntu Server, Debian, or CentOS) powers the majority of web servers worldwide. It is free, open source, extremely stable for long-running server environments, and can be configured precisely for the server's purpose.

question.text: A school wants to deploy tablets for Year 7 students. Give two reasons why iOS (iPad) might be preferred over Android tablets in a school environment.
question.explanation: Accept any two: iOS is a closed ecosystem with stricter app vetting (App Store), reducing risk of students installing inappropriate apps; Apple School Manager and MDM tools allow centralised control of all devices; iPads have a consistent hardware/software experience unlike Android's many device variations; strong integration with education apps such as Keynote, Pages, Numbers, and Google Workspace.

blocks.title: OS Comparison Research Task
blocks.intro: You are advising a startup business with three roles: office manager, software developer, and field sales representative. Research and recommend an OS for each role.
steps: Create a table with columns: Role | Recommended OS | Software needed | Reason for OS choice | Estimated cost.
For the office manager, consider what productivity and accounting software they will need and which OS supports it best.
For the software developer, research what OS is most common in the software development industry and why.
For the field sales rep, determine whether they need a desktop/laptop OS or a mobile OS.
Present your completed table and write a two-sentence justification for each recommendation.

blocks.text: Software Compatibility

Software compatibility refers to how well a piece of software functions on a given hardware and operating system combination. Compatibility issues are among the most common problems IT support staff encounter — they arise whenever there is a mismatch between what software requires and what the system provides.

blocks.definition: The ability of software to run correctly on a given hardware configuration, operating system version, and alongside other installed software without errors or loss of function.

blocks.text: Running Older Software on Current Hardware

Businesses often continue using software long after it was released — particularly specialised applications in accounting, manufacturing, or medicine. When that software is moved to newer hardware or a newer OS, compatibility problems can emerge.

items: 32-bit software may not install or run correctly on a 64-bit OS (though most modern OSes still support 32-bit apps through compatibility layers)
Drivers for old peripherals (e.g. a legacy label printer) may not exist for Windows 11
Old software may rely on deprecated system libraries or security protocols (e.g. TLS 1.0) that have been removed from the OS
Interface elements may render incorrectly at modern screen resolutions
Some macOS updates have dropped support for 32-bit applications entirely (macOS Catalina onwards)

blocks.heading: Industry Tip
blocks.text: In practice, IT technicians are sometimes asked to keep an older computer running a legacy OS (e.g. Windows 7) purely to support one piece of critical software that cannot be updated. This is a security risk — those machines must be isolated from the network.
Running Newer Software on Older Hardware
The reverse problem occurs when software is updated and its minimum hardware requirements increase beyond what existing machines can provide.

items: Minimum RAM requirements increase — e.g. an accounting suite that required 4 GB RAM now requires 8 GB after a major update
New software features may require a CPU instruction set not present in older processors
Storage requirements grow — a new OS update may require 64 GB of disk space on a device that only has 32 GB
GPU requirements for modern software (e.g. AI features in Microsoft 365) may exceed what older integrated graphics can provide
Windows 11 requires a TPM 2.0 chip and a compatible 8th-generation or newer Intel CPU — many business machines from 2017–2018 were excluded

blocks.text: Real Workplace Scenarios

rows: School replaces all PCs with Windows 11; old NAPLAN software doesn't launch
Contact vendor for updated version; run in compatibility mode; or keep one old machine
Small business upgrades accounting software; older laptops slow to a crawl
New version requires 16 GB RAM; machines have 8 GB
Upgrade RAM in machines where possible; replace machines that cannot be upgraded
Graphic designer updates macOS to latest version; font management plugin stops working
Plugin developer has not released a macOS update
Roll back OS or wait for plugin update; contact vendor
Medical practice uses custom patient management software from 2012
Software only runs on Windows 7; vendor no longer exists
Virtualise the old OS using a VM; procure modern replacement software
Staff member tries to open a .docx file received from a client in Office 2007
Newer file format not fully supported by old Office version
Install compatibility pack; upgrade Office; use Microsoft 365 online

blocks.text: Checking Minimum System Requirements

Before installing any software, IT staff check the minimum hardware requirements published by the software vendor. These specify the minimum OS version, CPU, RAM, and storage needed for the software to function. Recommended requirements describe what is needed for comfortable performance.

items.definition: The lowest hardware and OS specification on which software will technically run — often with degraded performance.
The hardware and OS specification at which software runs with full features and acceptable speed for everyday professional use.

blocks.heading: Career Connection
blocks.text: IT support technicians and procurement officers always check software requirements before approving purchases or upgrades. Recommending a software package that won't run on a client's current hardware is a costly mistake — both financially and for client trust.

question.text: A business has computers running Windows 10 with 4 GB RAM. They want to install new project management software that requires Windows 10 or later and a minimum of 8 GB RAM. What is the compatibility issue?
options: The operating system is incompatible
The computers do not have enough RAM
The CPU architecture is wrong
The storage capacity is too small
question.explanation: The OS requirement (Windows 10 or later) is met, but the RAM requirement (8 GB minimum) is not — the computers only have 4 GB. The computers will need a RAM upgrade before the software can be installed.

question.text: A company's accounts department uses software that only runs on Windows 7. The rest of the business has upgraded to Windows 11. What is the most appropriate solution?
options: Reinstall Windows 7 on all computers
Delete the accounting software and stop using it
Run the Windows 7 software in a virtual machine on a Windows 11 computer
Downgrade the operating system of the entire network
question.explanation: Virtualisation allows the legacy OS to run inside a virtual machine on modern hardware and OS. This keeps the business secure and up-to-date while still running the legacy software — without needing to maintain old physical hardware running Windows 7.

question.text: Explain what 'minimum hardware requirements' means and why IT professionals check them before installing software.
question.explanation: Minimum requirements are the lowest CPU, RAM, storage, and OS specifications on which software will run. IT professionals check them before installation to confirm the existing hardware can support the software — installing software on under-spec hardware causes poor performance, crashes, or failed installation, which wastes time and affects productivity.

blocks.title: Compatibility Check Scenario
blocks.intro: You are an IT support technician at a small business. They want to upgrade from Microsoft Office 2016 to Microsoft 365. Three of their computers have the following specifications.
steps: Look up the minimum and recommended system requirements for Microsoft 365 on the Microsoft website (support.microsoft.com).
Computer A: Windows 10, Intel Core i3 (7th Gen), 4 GB RAM, 128 GB SSD. Assess whether it meets minimum requirements.
Computer B: Windows 8.1, Intel Core i5 (6th Gen), 8 GB RAM, 256 GB HDD. Assess whether it meets minimum requirements.
Computer C: Windows 11, Intel Core i5 (10th Gen), 16 GB RAM, 512 GB SSD. Assess whether it meets minimum requirements.
For each computer that does not meet requirements, state what would need to be upgraded or changed.
Write a brief recommendation to the business manager explaining which computers can be updated immediately and which require hardware upgrades first.

blocks.text: Selecting and Purchasing Hardware

Selecting the right hardware is a core IT skill that goes beyond simply choosing the most powerful or most expensive option. Effective hardware procurement balances three key factors: cost, technical specifications, and the actual needs of the user.
Key Considerations for Hardware Purchase

items.definition: The total expenditure including hardware purchase price, any required software licences, peripherals, installation labour, and ongoing maintenance. Total cost of ownership (TCO) often exceeds the initial purchase price.

The technical details of the hardware: CPU model, RAM capacity, storage type and size, display resolution, connectivity ports, and battery life. Specifications must meet or exceed the requirements of intended software and workloads.
The actual functional requirements of the person or role using the hardware. Includes portability, the software they run, the volume of work, accessibility needs, and environment of use.

blocks.text: Balancing Cost Against Specifications

Higher specifications do not always translate to better value. An IT purchaser must identify the minimum viable specification for the role and avoid over-provisioning where budget could be better spent elsewhere.

rows: Intel Core i5, 8 GB RAM, 256 GB SSD, 24" monitor
Intel Core i7 / Ryzen 7, 32 GB RAM, 1 TB NVMe SSD, colour-accurate 4K display
Mid-range laptop, 8 GB RAM, 256 GB SSD, 4G/LTE capability, rugged chassis
Intel Core i5, 8 GB RAM, 128 GB SSD, Wi-Fi 6
Xeon or EPYC CPU, 32–64 GB ECC RAM, RAID storage, redundant PSU

blocks.text: Understanding User Needs

Before recommending hardware, a professional IT purchaser gathers requirements from the user or department. This is sometimes formalised as a Request for Quotation (RFQ) — a document sent to suppliers asking them to provide pricing and product details for specified requirements.

blocks.definition: A formal document sent to suppliers asking for pricing on specified hardware or services. It outlines minimum requirements, quantity needed, delivery timeframe, and any support conditions. Suppliers respond with a quote; the business then evaluates and selects.

blocks.heading: Industry Tip
blocks.text: When drafting an RFQ for a business client, always specify minimum requirements rather than exact models. This gives suppliers flexibility to quote comparable products and encourages competitive pricing. Locking in a single brand or model in an RFQ can inflate cost and reduce your options.
Sample Hardware Comparison

Imagine a small business with 10 staff members needs to replace their ageing desktop computers. The IT manager receives three quotes. Each quote must be evaluated against the required specifications and total cost.

For a standard office environment running Microsoft 365, email, and accounting software, Quote B (mid-range) likely represents the best value: it meets requirements with headroom, includes a 3-year on-site warranty (reducing future labour costs), and the per-seat cost is reasonable for a professional deployment.

blocks.heading: Career Connection
blocks.text: Procurement is a real IT career pathway. IT procurement officers, vendor managers, and asset managers spend significant time evaluating hardware quotes, negotiating with suppliers, and managing asset lifecycles. Understanding how to read a spec sheet and compare quotes is a foundational workplace skill.

question.text: A business is selecting laptops for 20 field technicians who work outdoors and travel frequently. Which specification is MOST important for this use case?
options: Dedicated gaming GPU
64 GB RAM
Battery life and durable chassis
4K OLED display
question.explanation: Field technicians need laptops they can use away from power sources and in varied environments. Battery life and durability are critical — a premium display or gaming GPU add cost and weight without benefiting the role.

question.text: What is the purpose of a Request for Quotation (RFQ)?
options: To install new software on purchased hardware
To formally request pricing from suppliers for specified hardware or services
To report a fault with purchased equipment to the vendor
To update firmware on existing hardware
question.explanation: An RFQ is a procurement document sent to suppliers specifying what is needed (minimum specs, quantity, delivery, warranty) and asking suppliers to respond with pricing. It is part of the formal purchasing process in businesses.

question.text: A school IT department has a budget of $8,000 to purchase computers for a media arts classroom. Students will be using video editing software. Identify two specifications that should be prioritised and explain why each is important for this use case.
question.explanation: Any two of: RAM (16–32 GB recommended for video editing — large video files are held in memory during editing; insufficient RAM causes crashes and slowdowns); dedicated GPU (video editing software uses GPU acceleration for rendering and playback); fast NVMe SSD (video files are very large; a fast drive reduces load times and prevents stuttering during playback); multi-core CPU (rendering video is CPU-intensive; more cores = faster export times).

blocks.title: RFQ Scenario — School IT Department
blocks.intro: The school IT department has asked you to draft a hardware requirements list for 30 new student laptops to be used across Years 10–12 in all subject areas. The budget is $1,200 per unit.
steps: List the core tasks students will perform on these laptops: word processing, spreadsheets, presentations, web research, light image editing.
Based on these tasks, specify the minimum requirements for: OS, CPU, RAM, storage, display, battery life, connectivity (Wi-Fi standard, USB ports, HDMI).
Search two different retailers (e.g. JB Hi-Fi Education, Officeworks, or a business IT supplier) for laptops that match your minimum requirements within the $1,200 budget.
Compare the two options in a table, including model name, specifications, price, and warranty.
Write a recommendation paragraph: which laptop should the school purchase, and why?

blocks.text: Maintenance and Troubleshooting

Maintaining computer hardware and resolving common faults are core competencies for anyone working in IT support. Proactive maintenance prevents downtime; systematic troubleshooting minimises its impact when it does occur.
Physical Maintenance Strategies

Physical maintenance focuses on keeping hardware in good working order through environmental controls and protective measures. The two most important strategies at Year 12 level are the use of an Uninterruptible Power Supply and appropriate temperature management.

blocks.definition: A device that provides emergency battery power to connected equipment when the mains power supply fails or fluctuates. A UPS gives users time to save work and shut down safely, and protects hardware from power surges and voltage spikes.

blocks.text: A UPS is essential for servers, network equipment, and any system where sudden power loss could result in data corruption or hardware damage. In a school environment, the network server room should be protected by a UPS. In a home office, a UPS protects against brownouts and storms.

items: Provides battery backup power when mains power fails
Protects against power surges and voltage spikes that can damage components
Allows a graceful shutdown — preventing file system corruption and data loss
Higher-capacity UPS units can power equipment for 10–30 minutes, allowing full system shutdown procedures
UPS units include a display showing battery level, load percentage, and estimated runtime

blocks.definition: Managing the operating temperature of computer hardware to keep it within the manufacturer's specified range. Excessive heat is the most common cause of hardware failure and reduced component lifespan.

blocks.text: Computers generate heat during normal operation. If that heat cannot escape, the system will throttle performance to protect components — and eventually shut down or fail permanently. In professional environments, server rooms and data centres use dedicated air conditioning to maintain a safe temperature range (typically 18–27°C).

items: Ensure adequate airflow: don't block case vents or place equipment in enclosed spaces
Clean dust from heatsinks, fans, and vents using compressed air — dust acts as thermal insulation
Server rooms require dedicated air conditioning, often with redundant units in case one fails
Monitor CPU and case temperatures using built-in software (e.g. HWiNFO, BIOS/UEFI sensors)
In hot climates or summer conditions, pay extra attention to laptop vents and cooling surfaces
Never place a laptop on a soft surface (bed, carpet) that blocks the underside vents

blocks.heading: Industry Tip
blocks.text: Server room temperature is taken seriously in enterprise IT. Industry standards (such as ASHRAE A2) define acceptable temperature and humidity ranges for data centre equipment. IT teams set temperature alarms so they are notified immediately if cooling fails — uncontrolled heat can destroy thousands of dollars of equipment within hours.
Troubleshooting Common Problems

Effective troubleshooting is systematic, not random. The professional approach: identify the symptom, form a hypothesis about the cause, test it, implement the fix, verify the problem is resolved, and document the outcome.
No Sound

headers: Fix if this is the cause
rows: Check the volume icon in the taskbar; check physical volume buttons on speakers/headphones
Windows: Settings > Sound > Output device; check if headphones are selected instead of speakers
Check physical cables (3.5mm, USB) or Bluetooth pairing status
Device Manager > Sound — look for yellow warning icons
Check the volume mixer (right-click taskbar volume icon) for individual app volumes
Test with different speakers/headphones on the same computer
If the fault follows the device, replace device; if it stays with computer, service the audio jack or card

blocks.text: Frozen Screen, Keyboard, and Mouse

headers: Fix if this is the cause
rows: Observe whether other applications still respond; check if cursor moves
Task Manager (Ctrl+Shift+Esc) > End Task on frozen application
No cursor movement, no response to keyboard
Hold power button 5–10 seconds to force shutdown; restart
After restart, check Task Manager — RAM usage at or near 100%
Close background applications; consider RAM upgrade
Check CPU temperature in BIOS or third-party software after a brief period of load
Clean dust from cooling system; improve airflow; replace thermal paste
Test with a wired keyboard or mouse
Replace batteries; re-pair wireless device; check USB receiver

blocks.text: No Connection to a Data Projector

headers: Fix if this is the cause
rows: Check both ends of the HDMI or VGA cable
Cycle through inputs (HDMI 1, HDMI 2, VGA, etc.) until the computer is detected
Computer not set to output to external display
Windows: Win+P shortcut to cycle display modes (PC screen only / Duplicate / Extend / Second screen only)
Select Duplicate or Extend as appropriate
Check laptop output port (USB-C, Mini DisplayPort, Thunderbolt) vs projector input (HDMI, VGA)
Use the appropriate adapter (USB-C to HDMI, DisplayPort to HDMI, etc.)
Some projectors take 30–60 seconds to display an image after power on
Wait for the projector to fully warm up before concluding there is a connection issue

blocks.heading: Industry Tip
blocks.text: Before assuming a hardware fault, always check the simple things first: cables, mute buttons, input selection, and whether the device is powered on. In IT support, the most common faults have the simplest causes — and the most embarrassing call-outs are for things the user could have fixed themselves. Good technicians confirm the basics before escalating.
Documenting Solutions

Professional IT staff document every fault and its resolution in a helpdesk or ticketing system. This creates a knowledge base that helps other technicians resolve similar issues faster in future, tracks recurring problems that may indicate a systemic hardware fault, and provides evidence for warranty claims.

question.text: What is the primary purpose of an Uninterruptible Power Supply (UPS)?
options: To increase the processing speed of a server
To provide backup power during a mains power failure, allowing safe shutdown
To regulate the operating temperature of server hardware
To distribute power evenly across multiple monitors
question.explanation: A UPS provides battery backup power when mains power fails or fluctuates. It gives users and systems time to save work and shut down safely, preventing data corruption and protecting hardware from sudden power loss and voltage spikes.

question.text: A teacher reports that their laptop won't display on the classroom projector. The projector is on and the HDMI cable is connected. What is the NEXT most likely thing to check?
options: Replace the projector
Reinstall Windows
Check the projector's input source setting and press Win+P on the laptop
Upgrade the laptop's RAM
question.explanation: After confirming cables and power, the next checks are: the projector's input/source setting (it may be set to VGA while the laptop is connected via HDMI) and the laptop's display output mode (Win+P cycles through PC only / Duplicate / Extend / Second screen).

question.text: A computer in a server room begins to throttle performance and eventually shuts down during a busy period. What is the most likely cause?
options: Insufficient RAM
Software virus
Overheating due to inadequate temperature control
Hard drive failure
question.explanation: CPUs and other components throttle (reduce performance) to protect themselves from heat damage, and shut down entirely if temperatures become critical. In a server room, this usually indicates cooling failure, blocked airflow, or dust buildup — all related to temperature control.

question.text: Describe the steps an IT technician should follow when troubleshooting a 'no sound' problem on a Windows computer. Include at least three distinct checks.
question.explanation: Expected steps (any three, with explanation): Check whether audio is muted in the taskbar volume icon and unmute if so; check the audio output device in Sound Settings to ensure speakers (not headphones) are selected; check the volume mixer to confirm the specific application is not muted; check Device Manager for driver issues (yellow warning icons) and update/reinstall audio drivers; test with different speakers/headphones to isolate whether the issue is with the device or the computer's audio output.

question.text: Why should IT technicians document the cause and solution of each technical fault they resolve?
options: It is required by hardware warranty conditions
It creates a knowledge base that helps resolve similar future faults more quickly
It automatically backs up user data
It updates the computer's operating system
question.explanation: Documentation creates an organisational knowledge base. When a similar fault occurs in future, technicians can search previous tickets for the solution rather than spending time re-diagnosing. It also helps identify recurring faults that may indicate a systemic or hardware problem.

blocks.title: Troubleshooting Role-Play Task
blocks.intro: Work in pairs. One person plays the IT support technician; the other plays the user reporting the problem. Work through two of the following scenarios using the systematic troubleshooting approach.
steps: Scenario A — No sound: The user says they cannot hear audio during a video call. As the technician, work through at least four checks in a logical order. Document each check and the result.

Scenario B — Frozen screen: The user's computer has completely stopped responding. As the technician, walk the user through the steps to attempt recovery, and document what you would check after restart.

Scenario C — No projector connection: A staff member cannot get their laptop to display on the projector for a presentation in 10 minutes. As the technician, guide them through the fastest possible diagnostic steps.
After each scenario, write up your troubleshooting log: Problem reported | Steps taken (in order) | Cause identified | Solution applied | Resolution confirmed.
Reflection: For one of your scenarios, describe one thing you would do differently next time to solve the problem faster.

blocks.title: Maintenance Plan for a School Computer Lab
blocks.intro: You are the IT support officer for a school with a 30-seat computer lab. Create a physical maintenance plan for the next 12 months.
steps: List the physical maintenance tasks that should be performed: include dust cleaning, cable checks, temperature monitoring, UPS testing, and any others you can identify.
For each task, specify the recommended frequency (weekly / monthly / termly / annually).
Research the recommended temperature range for computer operation (hint: check manufacturer documentation or ASHRAE standards).
Explain how you would monitor server room temperature and what would trigger an alert.
Write two paragraphs explaining to the school principal why investing in a UPS for the server room is worthwhile, using cost-benefit reasoning.

blocks.text: The Copyright Act 1968 (Australia)

The Copyright Act 1968 (Cth) is Australia's primary legislation protecting original creative works. Copyright applies automatically the moment an original work is expressed in a material form — there is no registration process and no need for the © symbol. The Act protects literary works, artistic works, musical works, films, sound recordings, broadcasts, and computer programs.

items.definition: The exclusive legal right of a creator to reproduce, publish, adapt, communicate, and sell their original work. In Australia it is automatic and governed by the Copyright Act 1968 (Cth).
Legal rights that protect creations of the mind — literary, artistic, musical, and technological works — giving creators control over how those works are used and commercially exploited.

Personal rights that belong to the creator of a work, separate from economic copyright. They include the right of attribution (to be credited), the right against false attribution (not to have someone else's work attributed to you), and the right of integrity (not to have the work treated in a derogatory way).

blocks.text: Fair Dealing Exceptions

The Copyright Act 1968 does not give copyright owners absolute control. "Fair dealing" is a set of statutory exceptions that allow limited use of copyrighted material without permission, provided the use is for a specified purpose and the amount used is reasonable. Fair dealing in Australia is narrower than the American concept of "fair use" — only the following purposes qualify:

rows: Copying a reasonable portion for your own educational use
Saving excerpts from a technical whitepaper to include in a research report
Quoting from a work to comment on or analyse it
Reproducing a screenshot of an app's UI in a published review
Using a work for humorous creative commentary
Creating a satirical video that parodies a software company's advertising
Reproducing content incidentally in news reporting
A news website embedding a social media post in a breaking news story
Using works in the course of legal or professional advisory processes
A lawyer reproducing software code as evidence in a court case

blocks.heading: Legal Alert
blocks.text: Fair dealing is not a blanket exception. Copying an entire work — even for study — is unlikely to qualify. Courts consider the purpose, the nature of the work, the amount copied, and the effect on the market for the original. When in doubt, seek permission or use openly licensed alternatives.
Private Use

The Act also contains a private use exception for certain types of content. The most relevant to digital consumers is the time-shifting provision: you may legally record a broadcast to watch or listen to later at a more convenient time, provided you do not keep the copy indefinitely or share it. Similarly, format-shifting allows you to convert legally purchased content (a CD you bought) to another format for personal use only.

blocks.heading: Legal Alert
blocks.text: Streaming a film from an unlicensed website is not private use — it is infringement. Private use exceptions apply only to content you have lawfully acquired. Downloading content from torrent sites or illegal streaming services is copyright infringement regardless of whether you share it.
Moral Rights

Unlike economic copyright (which can be sold or transferred), moral rights belong permanently to the creator of the work — they cannot be assigned to another party. The three moral rights under the Copyright Act 1968 are:

items: Right of attribution — the creator has the right to be identified as the author of the work. Posting someone's photo or code online without crediting them violates this right.
Right against false attribution — a person must not have someone else's work falsely attributed to them. AI-generated content falsely credited to a human author could constitute a breach.

Right of integrity — the creator's work must not be treated in a derogatory way that harms their honour or reputation. Cropping an artwork to remove important context, or altering a photograph in a misleading way, may breach this right.

blocks.text: Real Cases: IP Violations in Digital Media

blocks.title: Notable Australian and International IP Cases

items.label: Freelancer/UGC Image Theft
points: Common scenario: businesses use Google Image search results in marketing materials without checking licence terms
Consequence: rights holders (stock image agencies, photographers) issue DMCA takedown notices and pursue civil claims
Lesson: always verify the licence — image appearance on Google does not mean it is free to use

items.label: Music in YouTube Videos
points: YouTube's Content ID system automatically detects copyrighted music in uploaded videos
The rights holder can monetise the video (taking ad revenue), mute the audio, or block the video globally
Using Spotify music as background in a social media post or promotional video is infringement — a Spotify licence gives you personal listening rights only

items.label: Software Piracy in Workplaces
points: Installing unlicensed copies of Adobe Creative Suite, Microsoft Office, or other software on work computers exposes organisations to audits and significant fines
The Business Software Alliance (BSA) audits Australian companies and has recovered millions in unpaid licence fees
IT professionals are expected to maintain software asset management records and ensure licence compliance

items.label: Open Source Licence Violations
points: Using GPL-licensed open source code in a commercial product without releasing your source code breaches the licence conditions
This is a growing issue as developers incorporate open source libraries into proprietary software without checking licence obligations

blocks.text: Acknowledging the IP Owner

When you legitimately use someone else's intellectual property — under a fair dealing exception, a Creative Commons licence, or with explicit permission — you must acknowledge the IP owner. Acknowledgement does not transfer or waive copyright; it is a legal and ethical obligation that respects the creator's moral right of attribution. In professional and academic contexts, this acknowledgement takes the form of referencing (covered in the next lesson).

blocks.heading: Industry Standard
blocks.text: In professional IT work, always document the licence for every third-party asset you use — images, fonts, code libraries, audio files. Maintain a licences.txt or CREDITS file in your project. This is standard practice in software development and protects both you and your employer from legal risk.
Spot the IP Violation

blocks.title: Spot the IP Violation
blocks.instruction: Read each scenario. Decide whether it is a legitimate use of copyrighted content or an IP violation — then review the analysis.

emails.subject: New Instagram campaign — using influencer photos
emails.body: I found some great photos on Instagram that perfectly match our brand. I downloaded them, cropped out the photographer's watermark, and uploaded them to our business Instagram page. The photos look great and our engagement has doubled this week!

redFlags.detail: Instagram photos are the copyright of the person who took them. Downloading and using them commercially without the photographer's consent is infringement.
Deliberately removing a copyright notice or watermark is a separate aggravating offence and may also breach the photographer's moral right of attribution.
Using images in a business promotion — even if the images were publicly visible — requires a licence. Increased engagement does not justify infringement.

emails.subject: Using a graph from the ABS website in my report
emails.body: I included a graph from the Australian Bureau of Statistics website in my research report on digital access. I cited it as: Australian Bureau of Statistics (2023), Internet Activity, Australia, ABS, Canberra — and noted that Australian Government data is available under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 licence.

emails.subject: Background music sorted!
emails.body: I downloaded the intro music for our tech podcast from Spotify — it's a song I really love and it fits the vibe perfectly. I've uploaded the first episode to YouTube with the song playing in the background. Can't wait to grow our audience!

redFlags.element: Spotify licence does not cover redistribution
redFlags.detail: A Spotify subscription gives you the right to stream music for personal listening only. It does not grant a licence to use the music in content you publish.
The record label will almost certainly detect the song via Content ID and either monetise the video (taking all ad revenue) or block it entirely.
A podcast aiming to build an audience is a commercial or semi-commercial activity, making fair dealing for personal study or private use inapplicable.

blocks.text: Check Your Understanding

question.text: Under the Copyright Act 1968 (Cth), which of the following is a moral right that belongs to a creator?
options: The right to sell or transfer ownership of their work to another party
The right to be identified as the author of their work (right of attribution)
The right to prevent any criticism or review of their work
The right to receive royalty payments from all users of their work
question.explanation: Moral rights are personal rights that cannot be transferred. The right of attribution means the creator must be credited when their work is used. The other options describe economic rights (which can be sold) or are not rights that exist under Australian law.

question.text: A student copies three chapters from a textbook to avoid buying it and shares the scanned PDF with their class group chat. Which fair dealing exception could they rely on?
options: Research or study, because they are using it for educational purposes
Private use, because they are not selling the copy
No exception applies — the amount copied is excessive and sharing it publicly removes any private use defence
Criticism or review, because they might discuss the chapters in class
question.explanation: Fair dealing for research or study requires that the amount copied is reasonable — three chapters of a textbook is likely to exceed what is 'fair'. More importantly, sharing the copy with a class group chat means it is no longer private use. Both conditions fail, so the copying is an infringement.
