question.text: Explain the difference between economic copyright and moral rights, and give one example of how each could be relevant to a freelance web developer who creates a website for a client. (4 marks)
question.explanation: Economic copyright: the developer owns the copyright in the code and design assets they create (2 marks). They can assign or licence this to the client — many contracts transfer economic copyright to the client on final payment. Moral rights: even after the economic copyright is transferred, the developer retains the moral right of attribution (to be credited as creator) and the right of integrity (the client cannot alter the site in a way that is derogatory to the developer's reputation) (2 marks).

question.text: In Australia, you must register your creative work with a government authority before copyright protection applies.
question.explanation: False. Copyright in Australia is automatic. As soon as an original work is expressed in material form (written, recorded, coded), copyright applies immediately under the Copyright Act 1968. No registration, payment, or © symbol is required.

blocks.text: Why Referencing Matters in IT Contexts

Referencing is not just an academic formality — it is a professional and legal obligation. When you use another person's ideas, data, images, code, or research in your work, you must acknowledge the source. This respects the creator's moral right of attribution, demonstrates academic integrity, allows readers to verify your sources, and protects you from allegations of plagiarism. In industry, properly crediting third-party components (libraries, APIs, datasets) in technical documentation and public-facing products is standard professional practice.

blocks.heading: Career Connection
blocks.text: Technical writers, content creators, and IT consultants routinely produce reports, proposals, and documentation that draw on external sources. Professionals who reference accurately and consistently are seen as trustworthy and detail-oriented — two highly valued qualities in IT roles.
Referencing Techniques for Digital Publications
Two referencing styles are commonly used in Australian education and many industry contexts. Both can be applied to digital sources, but each treats online content slightly differently.

items.label: APA 7th Edition
points: Used in psychology, social sciences, education, and many IT vocational courses
Author-date citations in-text: (Gibbs, 2024)
Reference list at the end, alphabetical by author surname
For websites: Author, A. A. (Year, Month Day). Title of page. Site Name. URL
DOI (Digital Object Identifier) preferred over URL where available

items.label: Harvard (Author-Date)
points: Common in business, engineering, and many Australian university faculties
In-text citation: (Gibbs 2024) — no comma before year
Reference list at the end, alphabetical by author surname
For websites: Author, A.A. Year, Title of page, Site Name, viewed Day Month Year, <URL>
Accessed/viewed date is included to show when you retrieved the content

blocks.text: Referencing Digital Sources — Worked Examples

rows: Australian Bureau of Statistics. (2024, March 15). Internet activity, Australia. ABS. https://www.abs.gov.au/statistics/industry/technology-and-innovation
Australian Bureau of Statistics 2024, Internet activity, Australia, ABS, viewed 15 March 2024, <https://www.abs.gov.au/statistics/industry/technology-and-innovation>
Crash Course. (2023, September 12). How the internet works [Video]. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=example
Crash Course 2023, How the internet works, YouTube, viewed 12 September 2023, <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=example>
eSafety Commissioner. [@eSafetyOffice]. (2024, February 20). New research shows... [Post]. Instagram. https://www.instagram.com/p/example
eSafety Commissioner 2024, New research shows..., Instagram post, viewed 20 February 2024, <https://www.instagram.com/p/example>
Smith, J. (2024, January 8). Cybercrime costs Australian businesses $4 billion. The Australian. https://www.theaustralian.com.au/example
Smith, J. 2024, 'Cybercrime costs Australian businesses $4 billion', The Australian, 8 January, viewed 8 January 2024, <https://www.theaustralian.com.au/example>
PDF or document from a website
Department of Home Affairs. (2023). Australian Cyber Security Strategy 2023–2030. Australian Government. https://www.homeaffairs.gov.au/cyber/example.pdf
Department of Home Affairs 2023, Australian Cyber Security Strategy 2023–2030, Australian Government, viewed 1 November 2023, <https://www.homeaffairs.gov.au/cyber/example.pdf>

blocks.heading: Industry Standard
blocks.text: When no author is listed for a digital source, use the organisation name (e.g. 'Australian Signals Directorate') as the author. If no date is available, write 'n.d.' (no date) in place of the year. Always include a retrieval date for webpages because online content can change or be removed.
In-Text Citations for Digital Sources
Every reference list entry needs a corresponding in-text citation at the point where you use the source. In-text citations are brief — they point the reader to the full entry in your reference list.

rows: (Smith, 2024, p. 14) or (eSafety Commissioner, 2024, para. 3) for webpages without page numbers

blocks.text: Plagiarism — Definition and Consequences

blocks.definition: Presenting another person's words, ideas, images, code, or data as your own without attribution. Plagiarism can be deliberate (copying and passing off as your own) or unintentional (poor paraphrasing, forgotten citations, sloppy referencing).

rows: Zero mark for the assessment task; academic misconduct notation; possible exclusion from qualification
Failing grade for the unit; suspension; expulsion; notation on academic record
Dismissal; damage to professional reputation; legal action from the original IP owner; public exposure
Article retraction; career destruction; civil lawsuit; damages payable to the original creator

blocks.heading: Legal Alert
blocks.text: Plagiarism of code is a growing issue in software development. Copying code from Stack Overflow, GitHub, or other repositories without checking the licence — and without attribution — can constitute both plagiarism and copyright infringement. Many open source licences (MIT, Apache 2.0, GPL) allow use but require attribution.
Referencing in Professional IT Documents

Professional IT documents — feasibility studies, technical reports, security audits, proposals — often draw on industry standards, vendor documentation, research papers, and government guidelines. Referencing in these documents typically follows a simpler in-line style (hyperlinks and footnotes) rather than a formal academic style, but the principle of acknowledging sources remains the same.

items: Technical reports: use numbered footnotes or endnotes with source details
Business proposals: embed hyperlinks to sources and list references in an appendix
Software documentation: include a CREDITS or ATTRIBUTION file listing third-party libraries and their licences
Presentations: include source citations on individual slides; list all references on a final 'References' slide

blocks.heading: Career Connection
blocks.text: Tools like Zotero (free), EndNote, and Mendeley help professionals manage references automatically. Many IT students start using these tools in Year 12 and find them invaluable throughout university and their careers. Zotero has a browser extension that can capture webpage references in one click.
Referencing Practice Activity

blocks.title: Build the Reference
blocks.instruction: A student is writing a report on cyberbullying. They want to reference the following webpage: The eSafety Commissioner published an article called 'Cyberbullying and young people' on 12 June 2024 on the website esafety.gov.au. The URL is https://www.esafety.gov.au/young-people/cyberbullying. Write this reference in APA 7th edition format.
blocks.answer: eSafety Commissioner. (2024, June 12). Cyberbullying and young people. eSafety Commissioner. https://www.esafety.gov.au/young-people/cyberbullying
blocks.explanation: Note: the organisation name (eSafety Commissioner) is used as the author because no individual author is named. The year, month, and day are all included because the page has a specific publication date. The website name and URL are both required in APA 7th edition.

blocks.text: Check Your Understanding

question.text: A student includes the following in-text citation in their report: (Department of Home Affairs, 2023). Which referencing style are they using?
options: Harvard, because there is no comma between the author and year
APA, because there is a comma between the author and year inside the brackets
Chicago, because the author is an organisation rather than an individual
MLA, because they used round brackets
question.explanation: APA 7th edition uses a comma between the author and year: (Author, Year). Harvard style does NOT use a comma: (Author Year). The type of author (individual vs organisation) and the use of round brackets do not distinguish the styles.

question.text: If you put someone else's idea into your own words (paraphrase) rather than quoting them directly, you do not need to include a reference.
question.explanation: False. You must reference the source whenever you use someone else's idea, regardless of whether you quote directly or paraphrase. Failing to reference a paraphrase is plagiarism — the only difference from a direct quote is that you do not need quotation marks or a page number.

question.text: Explain why IT professionals need to acknowledge third-party code libraries and APIs in their software documentation. Refer to both intellectual property law and professional practice. (4 marks)
question.explanation: IP law: open source libraries are protected by copyright and released under specific licences (e.g. MIT, GPL, Apache 2.0). These licences require attribution — failure to acknowledge the library is a licence breach and may constitute copyright infringement (2 marks). Professional practice: documenting dependencies (in a CREDITS, NOTICES, or ATTRIBUTION file) is a standard requirement in professional software development, required by many organisations' legal and compliance teams. It also allows future maintainers to verify licence compatibility and update dependencies safely (2 marks).

blocks.text: Digital Citizenship in a Professional Context

Digital citizenship refers to the responsible, ethical, and legal use of technology. At Year 12 and beyond, digital citizenship extends beyond online safety into professional contexts: how you represent yourself and your employer online, how you engage with colleagues and clients on digital platforms, and how your digital footprint affects your career. Employers now routinely review candidates' social media profiles during hiring processes.

blocks.definition: The responsible, ethical, and safe participation in digital environments — including understanding digital rights and responsibilities, contributing positively to online communities, and understanding the personal, legal, and professional consequences of online behaviour.

blocks.text: Responsible Use of Social Networking
Social networking platforms — LinkedIn, Instagram, X (formerly Twitter), Facebook, TikTok, Snapchat — are part of both personal and professional life. Responsible use requires understanding that:

items: Content posted online is permanent — even deleted posts can be screenshot and shared
You represent not only yourself but potentially your employer when you post publicly
Platform terms of service are legally binding agreements — violating them can result in account suspension
Sharing another person's private information without consent may breach the Privacy Act 1988
Defamatory posts — false statements of fact that damage someone's reputation — carry legal consequences regardless of the platform
Many Australian workplaces have explicit social media policies that employees must follow

blocks.heading: Career Connection
blocks.text: LinkedIn is the world's largest professional network, with over 1 billion members. IT students and professionals use it to showcase projects, connect with industry, find internships and jobs, and follow companies. A professional LinkedIn profile is increasingly expected when applying for IT roles. Ensure your profile is complete, professional in tone, and consistent with how you present yourself in job applications.
Forms of Cyberbullying

Cyberbullying is the repeated, intentional use of digital technology to harass, intimidate, humiliate, or harm another person. At Year 12 level, it is important to understand the specific forms cyberbullying takes — this knowledge is relevant both personally and professionally, as IT graduates may work in schools, community organisations, or platform companies that deal with cyberbullying.

rows: Sending repeated offensive, threatening, or abusive messages
Continuous DMs on Instagram; repeated abusive comments on TikTok videos
Deliberately and repeatedly leaving someone out of online groups or activities
Removing someone from a class group chat; excluding from a gaming group
Creating fake accounts or profiles to pose as the victim or to post harmful content as if it were from them
Creating a fake Instagram profile with the victim's name and photos
Sharing a person's private or sensitive information — including location, sexual orientation, health details — without consent
Posting someone's address or photos in private spaces to a public forum
Repeated, threatening monitoring of a person's online activity, location check-ins, or social media posts
Tracking check-ins on Facebook; monitoring location-tagged posts
Posting inflammatory, provocative, or offensive content specifically intended to upset or provoke others
Deliberately posting negative comments on a person's creative content
Accessing another person's social media account without permission and posting embarrassing or harmful content
Logging into a friend's account while they are away and posting offensive content

blocks.text: Strategies to Manage and Limit Cyberbullying
Managing cyberbullying requires a multi-layered response: personal action, platform reporting mechanisms, and legal escalation where necessary.

items: Save evidence — screenshot all instances of cyberbullying before blocking the person, as blocking may prevent you from accessing evidence later
Block and restrict — use the platform's block and restrict tools to prevent further contact

Report to the platform — all major platforms (Instagram, TikTok, Snapchat, YouTube) have reporting mechanisms; platforms with more than 10,000 Australian users must comply with the Online Safety Act 2021
Tell a trusted adult or HR — in school contexts, report to a teacher; in workplace contexts, report to HR or a manager
eSafety Commissioner — if the platform fails to act, you can submit a complaint to the Australian eSafety Commissioner at esafety.gov.au, who has legal powers to require content removal
Police — if the behaviour constitutes threats, stalking, or criminal conduct, report to police

blocks.heading: Industry Standard
blocks.text: Australia's Online Safety Act 2021 created a Basic Online Safety Expectations (BOSE) framework. Under it, large social media platforms must take reasonable steps to minimise harmful content — including cyberbullying material. Platforms that fail to meet these expectations can face regulatory action from the eSafety Commissioner.
Workplace Social Media Policies

Most organisations have formal social media policies that govern how employees use social media — both on company platforms and in their personal accounts when they reference the organisation or their role. These policies typically address:

items: Confidentiality — prohibiting the posting of commercial-in-confidence information, client details, or internal project information
Professional conduct — requiring that employees do not post content that could embarrass the organisation or constitute harassment
Disclosure — if an employee publicly discusses their employer's products or services, many policies require them to disclose the employment relationship
Personal use — many policies limit personal social media use during work hours and on work devices
Consequences — breaching a social media policy is typically a disciplinary matter and in serious cases grounds for dismissal

blocks.heading: Legal Alert
blocks.text: Fair Work Commission rulings have upheld dismissals of employees who posted critical or embarrassing content about their employers on personal social media accounts. The law recognises that an employer's reputation can be damaged by an employee's private posts — especially when the employee is publicly identifiable. Posting about a client or a workplace incident on social media can also breach confidentiality obligations in your employment contract.
Spot the Cyberbullying

blocks.title: Spot the Cyberbullying
blocks.instruction: Read each scenario. Identify whether it is an example of cyberbullying and, if so, which form — then review the analysis.

emails.from: Year 12 group chat — screenshot posted by Jordan
emails.subject: Group chat behaviour
emails.body: Jordan screenshots a private conversation between two classmates and posts it to the broader year group chat, including personal comments the classmates made about their health struggles. Jordan adds a laughing emoji and says 'look what these two have been saying lol'.

redFlags.detail: Sharing private information (personal health details from a private conversation) without consent is a form of cyberbullying called outing or doxing.
Posting to the whole year group magnifies the harm significantly — once sent to a group, the content can be further shared.
The laughing emoji and mocking comment indicate intent to humiliate, satisfying the 'intentional' element of cyberbullying.

emails.subject: Warning issued to disruptive member
emails.body: A member of our gaming server was repeatedly posting offensive slurs in the general chat. I issued them a formal warning, muted them for 24 hours per our server rules, and documented the incidents. I've told them that a second violation will result in a permanent ban.

emails.subject: Instagram account — impersonation
emails.body: An account using Mia's name, profile photo, and school has been created. The account has been posting rude comments on other students' photos as if it were Mia, and has sent messages to Mia's friends claiming Mia said things she never said.

redFlags.detail: Creating a fake account using someone's real identity, photo, and personal details is a clear form of cyberbullying — and may also breach the platform's terms of service and Australian defamation law.
False statements posted as if they came from Mia could constitute defamation — a legal wrong as well as a form of cyberbullying.
Report the impersonation account to the platform immediately and preserve screenshots. If the platform does not act, escalate to the eSafety Commissioner.

blocks.text: Check Your Understanding

question.text: A student is being cyberbullied. The bully's posts are visible on Instagram. What is the MOST important first action the student should take?
options: Immediately delete all their social media accounts to prevent further harassment
Reply to the bully directly to tell them to stop
Screenshot and save all evidence before taking any other action
Wait and see if the bully stops on their own after a few days
question.explanation: Saving evidence (screenshots with timestamps) is the most critical first step. If you block the bully first or the platform removes the content, the evidence may become inaccessible. Evidence is required for platform reports, eSafety Commissioner complaints, and potential police involvement. Replying can escalate the situation; waiting allows harm to continue.

question.text: Which form of cyberbullying involves accessing another person's social media account without permission and posting content as if it were from them?
options: Outing
Cyberstalking
Fraping
Trolling
question.explanation: Fraping involves accessing someone else's account without consent and posting as them. It may also constitute an offence under the Criminal Code Act 1995 (Cth) regarding unauthorised access to computer systems, in addition to being a form of cyberbullying.

question.text: An IT graduate starts a new job at a software company. After their first week, they post on their personal Instagram: 'Settling into my new role at TechCorp — the code is a mess and the clients are demanding! #newjob'. Explain two ways this post could have professional consequences. (4 marks)
question.explanation: 1. Breach of confidentiality/employment contract: describing internal work quality ('the code is a mess') publicly may breach confidentiality obligations in the employee's contract, even on a personal account — because TechCorp's professional reputation is affected (2 marks). 2. Breach of workplace social media policy: many organisations have social media policies requiring employees to represent the company professionally. A post that could be seen as critical of the company or its clients may be grounds for disciplinary action or, in serious cases, dismissal — as upheld in Fair Work Commission rulings (2 marks).

question.text: Under Australia's Online Safety Act 2021, large social media platforms are legally required to take reasonable steps to minimise cyberbullying content targeting Australian users.
question.explanation: True. The Online Safety Act 2021 established the Basic Online Safety Expectations (BOSE) framework. Platforms with significant Australian user bases must take reasonable steps to reduce harmful content, including cyberbullying. The eSafety Commissioner can enforce these requirements.

blocks.text: The Impact of Digital Technologies on Work-Life Balance

Digital technologies have fundamentally changed the relationship between work and personal life. Email, instant messaging (Slack, Teams), cloud-based systems, and remote access tools mean that work is accessible from anywhere at any time. For IT professionals — who may be on call, managing cloud infrastructure, or supporting global teams across time zones — the boundary between work and personal time is particularly blurred. While this flexibility offers benefits, it creates significant risks for physical and mental health.

items.definition: The equilibrium between the time and energy a person devotes to work and paid obligations versus personal life, rest, relationships, and leisure activities. Digital connectivity has made this balance increasingly difficult to maintain.

The state of using technology in a way that supports physical and mental health — including managing screen time, digital boundaries, notification management, and the ability to disconnect from digital devices.
The expectation — sometimes explicit, often implicit — that employees remain contactable and responsive outside of formal working hours through digital devices and communication tools.

blocks.text: How Technology Affects Personal and Professional Boundaries

rows: Flexibility to respond when convenient; stay informed while travelling
Inability to mentally 'switch off'; pressure to check email constantly, including evenings and weekends
Remote access / VPN to work systems
Can work from home; business continuity during disruptions
No physical separation between 'work space' and 'home space'; work can intrude at any hour
Fast team communication; reduces email volume
Informal culture of 24/7 availability; message read receipts create pressure to respond immediately
Real-time document collaboration; access from any device
Work is never 'put away'; colleagues can see when you are active and may expect responses
Ensures systems are monitored and incidents resolved quickly
Significant disruption to sleep, social plans, and family time; sustained on-call obligations can lead to burnout

blocks.heading: Industry Standard
blocks.text: The 'Right to Disconnect' — the legal right of employees not to be required to respond to work communications outside of their working hours — became law in Australia in August 2024 under the Fair Work Legislation Amendment (Closing Loopholes No. 2) Act 2024. For large employers, it applies immediately; for small businesses, it applies from August 2025. IT professionals and their managers need to understand how this affects rostering, on-call expectations, and communication norms.
Employer Expectations and Technology

Employers in the IT sector often have explicit or implicit expectations around availability that go beyond standard hours. Understanding these expectations — and your rights — is essential as you enter the workforce.

items: On-call expectations: many IT operations, DevOps, and support roles include structured on-call components — these should be documented in your employment contract and compensated (through loading, time in lieu, or overtime)

Response time expectations: some organisations have informal cultures where fast responses to messages — even after hours — are rewarded and slow responses are penalised. Under the Right to Disconnect legislation, unreasonable after-hours contact expectations may now be legally challengeable

Bring Your Own Device (BYOD) policies: if you use a personal device for work, your employer may require you to install mobile device management (MDM) software — understand what access this gives them to your device

Monitoring: Australian employers can legally monitor work devices and work email accounts — but must disclose this in workplace policies. Personal devices and personal email are generally not subject to monitoring

blocks.text: Strategies for Switching Off

Actively managing the boundary between work and personal life is a professional skill. Research consistently shows that recovery time — complete disengagement from work — is essential for long-term performance, health, and job satisfaction. IT professionals are particularly at risk of burnout due to demanding problem-solving work and always-on cultures.

items: Notification management: set do-not-disturb schedules on your phone; use work communication apps' focus modes to silence non-urgent messages outside work hours
Physical separation: if you work from home, designate a specific workspace — when you leave it, you are 'off work'. Avoid checking work email from the couch or bedroom
Clear communication: let colleagues and managers know your working hours. Set an auto-response if you will be unavailable. Normalise others doing the same
Structured end-of-day routines: close all work apps, write a brief task list for tomorrow, and perform a deliberate 'shutdown ritual' that signals the end of your work day
Digital detox periods: schedule regular periods — evenings, weekends, holidays — where you do not access work systems. Protect these times as you would a meeting

Sleep hygiene: avoid screens for at least 30–60 minutes before bed. Blue light from screens suppresses melatonin production and impairs sleep quality — crucial for cognitive performance in demanding IT roles

blocks.heading: Career Connection
blocks.text: Burnout is one of the leading causes of career departure in IT. The 2023 Stack Overflow Developer Survey reported that work-life balance is among the most important factors for developer job satisfaction — ranking above salary for many respondents. Graduates who establish healthy digital boundaries early in their careers demonstrate maturity, self-awareness, and sustainability — qualities valued by progressive employers.
Ethical Dilemma — Work-Life Balance

blocks.title: Scenario Challenge — Digital Work-Life Boundaries
blocks.scenario: Read the following scenarios carefully. Answer each structured response question using your knowledge of digital wellbeing, Australian workplace law, and professional practice. These are the kinds of extended response questions that appear in Applied IT assessments.

questions.question: Scenario A — After-hours contact: Mia has just started her first IT graduate role at a cloud services company. Her manager regularly sends Slack messages at 9 pm expecting same-evening responses. The company's employment contract states 'standard business hours are 8:30 am – 5:00 pm'. No on-call component is mentioned. Identify whether the manager's expectations are reasonable under Australian law, and explain what steps Mia could take to manage the situation professionally. (5 marks)

Scenario B — BYOD and monitoring: A small IT consultancy asks all staff to install the company's MDM (mobile device management) software on their personal iPhones to receive work email. The MDM software can remotely wipe the phone and view app usage statistics. Discuss one benefit and two concerns a new employee should raise before agreeing to this arrangement. (4 marks)

blocks.text: Check Your Understanding

question.text: Australia's Right to Disconnect legislation (2024) gives employees the right to:
options: Refuse to work any hours beyond their contracted hours under any circumstances
Ignore unreasonable contact from their employer outside their working hours without facing disciplinary action
Disconnect their work devices from the internet permanently after 5 pm
Work from home permanently regardless of their employer's policy
question.explanation: The Right to Disconnect means employees can refuse to monitor, read, or respond to employer contact outside of working hours — without this being considered insubordination — when the request is unreasonable. It does not prohibit all after-hours contact, but it protects employees from being penalised for not responding to unreasonable requests.

question.text: Which of the following is the MOST effective strategy for an IT worker to maintain work-life balance when working from home?
options: Check work email first thing in the morning and last thing at night to stay on top of everything
Designate a specific physical workspace and perform a deliberate shutdown ritual at the end of each work day
Keep all work apps open permanently so you never miss an urgent message
Avoid telling colleagues your working hours so you appear available and committed
question.explanation: Physical and psychological separation is key to work-life balance when working from home. A designated workspace and deliberate shutdown ritual signals to your brain that work has ended. Checking email first/last thing and keeping apps permanently open blurs the work-personal boundary, increasing the risk of burnout.

question.text: Australian employers can legally monitor employees' personal devices and personal email accounts as long as employees are using those devices for some work-related tasks.
question.explanation: False. Australian employers can generally monitor work-provided devices and work communication systems — but only if they have disclosed this in workplace policies. Personal devices (and personal email/social media accounts on those devices) are generally not subject to lawful employer monitoring. Installing MDM software on a personal device requires the employee's informed consent.

blocks.text: The Concept of Social Networking

Social networking refers to the use of internet-based platforms and applications that allow individuals and organisations to create profiles, connect with others, share content, and participate in online communities. Social networking is both a technology and a social behaviour — platforms provide the infrastructure, but users generate the content and relationships that give the platforms value.

items.definition: The use of digital platforms to create profiles, form connections, and share content with other users — including individuals, groups, organisations, and brands.
Any content — text, photos, video, reviews, comments — created and published by individual users of a platform, rather than by the platform itself or professional media organisations.

A set of rules used by social networking platforms to determine which content is shown to which users, in what order, and how prominently — typically optimised to maximise engagement and time spent on the platform.
Content that spreads rapidly across social networks through sharing, reaching large audiences organically — often unpredictably — without paid promotion.

blocks.text: How Social Networking Changed Information Publication and Distribution

Before social networking, information publication followed a controlled, hierarchical model. Professional journalists, editors, and publishers acted as gatekeepers — deciding what was published, when, and to whom. Social networking fundamentally disrupted this model by enabling anyone with internet access to publish content immediately, at scale, and at near-zero cost.

blocks.title: Traditional vs Social Media Information Distribution

items.label: Traditional Media (Pre-Social Networking)
points: Gatekeepers: editors, publishers, broadcasters decided what was published
Distribution: newspapers, TV, radio — physical or broadcast-limited reach
Timescales: daily newspapers; nightly TV news; hours or days to publication
Cost: high — printing, broadcasting, distribution infrastructure required
Audience interaction: letters to the editor; phone-ins; passive consumption
Corrections: published in later editions — limited reach compared to the original
Geographic reach: national or regional; international distribution was expensive

items.label: Social Networking Media
points: No gatekeepers: anyone can publish; moderation is reactive, not preventative
Distribution: global, instant, mobile-first — reach is determined by algorithms and sharing behaviour
Timescales: real-time — breaking news on Twitter/X often precedes traditional media by hours
Cost: near-zero — free to publish on most platforms; paid promotion available
Audience interaction: comments, likes, shares, replies — highly interactive and two-way
Corrections: misinformation can spread faster than corrections; retractions rarely achieve the same reach
Geographic reach: global by default; language is the primary barrier

blocks.heading: Legal Alert
blocks.text: The ability to publish instantly and globally does not remove legal responsibility. Defamation law applies to social media posts — Australian courts have found individuals and media organisations liable for defamatory content posted on or shared via social media platforms. The 'Voller case' (Fairfax Media Publications v Voller, 2021) established that Australian media companies can be liable as publishers of defamatory third-party comments posted on their social media pages.
Mobile Devices and Information Distribution

The proliferation of smartphones has been the single biggest enabler of social networking's disruption of traditional media. Mobile devices allow users to capture events in real time (photo, video, live streaming), publish immediately from the scene, and consume news and information at any time and place. This has created phenomena such as citizen journalism — ordinary people reporting on events as they happen — which has both democratised information and created significant challenges for accuracy and verification.

rows: Journalists travel to scene; broadcast at scheduled news times
Eyewitnesses livestream from smartphones within seconds; events trend globally before journalists arrive
Newspapers and TV earned revenue from classified and display advertising
Advertising spend has shifted massively to Facebook, Google, and Instagram — decimating newspaper revenue models
Editors, sub-editors, and fact-checkers reviewed content before publication
No pre-publication review — misinformation, deepfakes, and false reports spread before they can be checked
Measured in subscribers or viewers — typically regional or national
A single post can reach millions globally within hours through sharing and algorithms

blocks.text: Implications for IT Careers

The transformation of information distribution has created entirely new IT and digital career categories, while requiring traditional IT roles to adapt. Understanding social media platforms at a technical and strategic level is now a core competency for many IT graduates.

blocks.title: IT Career Roles Created or Expanded by Social Networking

items.label: Social Media Manager
items.description: Plans and publishes content across social platforms, manages community engagement, monitors analytics, and runs paid campaigns. Requires understanding of platform algorithms and audience behaviour.

items.label: Content Creator / Digital Storyteller
items.description: Produces video, written, graphic, and interactive content for social platforms and digital publications. Increasingly uses AI tools for content generation and editing.

items.label: Digital Marketing Analyst
items.description: Uses data analytics tools to measure the reach, engagement, and conversion rates of social media and digital marketing campaigns. Heavy use of tools like Google Analytics, Meta Business Suite, and Tableau.

items.label: Trust and Safety Specialist
items.description: Works within social media companies to review reported content, develop moderation policies, and work with AI-based content detection systems to combat misinformation, cyberbullying, and harmful content.

items.label: SEO / Digital Content Strategist
items.description: Optimises digital content to rank highly in search engines and gain organic traction on social platforms. Combines technical knowledge of search algorithms with content strategy skills.

items.label: Platform Developer
items.description: Builds and maintains the APIs, features, and infrastructure that power social networking platforms. Involves backend development, distributed systems, real-time data processing, and ML recommendation systems.

blocks.heading: Career Connection
blocks.text: Social media management and content creation are among the fastest-growing job categories in Australia's digital economy. These roles increasingly require technical skills alongside communication abilities — understanding how platforms' APIs work, how to read analytics dashboards, how to run A/B tests on content, and how to use AI tools for content generation and optimisation. IT graduates are well-positioned to move into these roles because they understand the technical infrastructure behind the platforms.
Misinformation and the Responsibility of Platforms

One of the most significant negative impacts of social networking on information distribution is the rapid spread of misinformation — false or inaccurate information — and disinformation — deliberately false information spread with intent to deceive. Social media algorithms optimise for engagement, and emotionally charged false content often generates more engagement than accurate but less dramatic reporting. This creates structural incentives that favour the spread of misinformation.

items: Deepfakes — AI-generated synthetic video or audio content that realistically depicts people saying or doing things they never did
Health misinformation — false medical claims that spread rapidly on social platforms (e.g. vaccine misinformation during COVID-19)
Electoral misinformation — false information about voting processes, candidates, or election results
Financial scams — fraudulent investment schemes and cryptocurrency scams amplified through social media reach

blocks.text: Ethical Dilemma — Social Media and Information

blocks.title: Scenario Challenge — Social Media and Information Distribution
blocks.scenario: Apply your knowledge of social networking, information distribution, digital citizenship, and copyright law to the following scenarios. Write structured responses that address the specific questions asked.

questions.question: Scenario A — Viral misinformation: A student working as a social media manager for a local business notices that a post they shared (which they did not create) has gone viral. The post contains a health claim that a new study has found is false. The post now has 45,000 shares. The student's employer is asking them to leave the post up because 'the engagement is great for our reach.' Discuss the ethical and legal obligations the student faces, and recommend a course of action. (6 marks)

Scenario B — Traditional vs digital publication: A Year 12 student argues that 'newspapers are irrelevant because everything is on social media now.' Evaluate this claim by identifying two ways traditional media still provides value that social networking cannot fully replicate, and two genuine advantages social networking has over traditional media for information distribution. (6 marks)

blocks.text: Check Your Understanding

question.text: Which of the following BEST describes the role of algorithms in social networking platforms?
options: They ensure all users see content in strict chronological order from accounts they follow
They determine which content is shown to which users based on rules optimised to maximise engagement
They fact-check all posts before they appear in users' feeds
They are used exclusively to display paid advertising content
question.explanation: Social networking algorithms decide what content users see, in what order, and how prominently — based on predicted engagement (likes, comments, shares, time spent). This is not chronological, not based on fact-checking, and applies to both organic and paid content. The optimisation for engagement is one reason misinformation can spread rapidly — it often generates strong emotional reactions that drive engagement metrics.

question.text: The Voller case (Fairfax Media Publications v Voller, 2021) established which important legal principle about social media in Australia?
options: Social media companies are not liable for any content posted by their users
Individual users cannot be sued for defamatory comments posted on social media
Media organisations can be held liable as publishers of defamatory third-party comments posted on their social media pages
The Australian Government must approve all social media posts before publication
question.explanation: The Voller case (affirmed by the High Court of Australia) established that media companies which operate public Facebook pages and invite public comment can be held responsible as publishers of defamatory comments left by third-party users — even if the company did not write the comment itself. This has significant implications for any organisation that runs public social media accounts.

question.text: Identify two specific ways that the widespread use of mobile devices has changed how information is distributed through social networking platforms. For each, explain whether this change has been beneficial, harmful, or both. (6 marks)
question.explanation: Acceptable answers include: 1. Real-time citizen journalism — mobile cameras and live streaming allow anyone to capture and publish events as they happen (beneficial: democratises reporting, enables accountability; harmful: unverified footage can spread misinformation, invades privacy of people filmed without consent). 2. Constant connectivity — smartphones mean users are always connected to social platforms (beneficial: breaking news reaches people instantly; harmful: information overload, reduced ability to critically evaluate content, addiction to notification cycles). 3. Global reach from any location — content can be shared globally from a remote location without infrastructure (beneficial: amplifies voices that would otherwise be unheard; harmful: misinformation and harmful content spread before moderation can act). Award 2 marks per point: 1 for identifying the change with an example, 1 for the nuanced beneficial/harmful analysis.

question.text: A small business owner shares a news article on their Facebook page. The article is later found to contain false health information. Under what circumstances could the business owner face legal consequences?
options: Never — sharing someone else's article means the original publisher is always responsible
Only if they personally wrote the false information in the original article
If the false information in the shared post constitutes defamation and they continued to share it after being made aware it was false
Only if they are a media company — individuals cannot be sued for social media shares
question.explanation: Sharing false information that is defamatory can expose the sharer to liability — particularly if they continue sharing after being notified the information is false. While the original publisher bears primary responsibility, anyone who republishes defamatory content can be treated as a publisher in Australian law. Individuals — not just media companies — can be sued for defamatory social media posts and shares.

lessons.title: File Formats for Graphics and Audio

blocks.text: File Formats for Graphics and Audio

Choosing the right file format is one of the most fundamental decisions in digital production. The wrong format can result in blurry images, enormous file sizes, or audio that sounds muffled on professional speakers. Industry professionals select formats deliberately based on how the asset will be used — and so should you.
Vector Graphics

Vector graphics store images as mathematical equations describing lines, curves, and shapes rather than a grid of pixels. This means they can be scaled to any size — from a business card to a billboard — without any loss of quality.

items.definition: An image defined by mathematical paths and shapes. Infinitely scalable with no quality loss. Ideal for logos, icons, and illustrations.
The standard web vector format. Text-based XML file that can be opened in a browser or edited in a text editor. Supported by all modern browsers.
Adobe's native vector format. Full editing capability but requires Adobe Illustrator to open in its original form.
An older vector format widely used in print production and compatible with most professional design software.

blocks.heading: Industry Standard
blocks.text: Professional designers deliver logo files in SVG (for web/digital) and EPS or AI (for print). If a client asks for a logo "in the highest quality", they want a vector file — not a JPG.
Raster (Bitmap) Graphics

Raster graphics store images as a grid of coloured pixels. They are ideal for photographs and complex imagery with gradients and fine detail, but they lose quality when scaled up — the pixels become visible, causing a blurry or blocky appearance known as pixelation.

items.definition: An image stored as a fixed grid of pixels. Quality is tied to resolution — enlarging a raster image causes pixelation.
Lossless raster format. Supports transparency (alpha channel). Best for graphics, logos on backgrounds, screenshots, and web images where quality must be preserved.
Lossy compressed raster format. Excellent for photographs. Smaller file sizes than PNG but loses some data each time it is saved — not suitable for images that will be repeatedly edited.
Limited to 256 colours. Supports simple animation. Used for short looping animations on the web.
High-quality lossless raster format used in professional print production and archiving. Files are large but contain all original image data.
Adobe Photoshop's native format. Stores layers, masks, and adjustment data for continued editing.

blocks.text: Vector vs Raster: Comparison

rows: Can be very large at high resolution

blocks.text: Audio File Formats

Audio formats fall into two main categories: uncompressed (lossless) formats that preserve every detail of the original recording, and compressed formats that reduce file size by removing audio data the human ear is less likely to notice.

items.definition: Uncompressed, lossless audio format developed by Microsoft. Highest quality but very large file sizes. Industry standard for recording, editing, and audio production.
Lossy compressed audio format. Significantly smaller than WAV. Universally supported. Quality degrades slightly but acceptable for music distribution and podcasting.
Lossy compressed format, successor to MP3. Better audio quality at the same file size. Used by Apple (iTunes, iPhone) and YouTube.
Lossless compressed format — smaller than WAV but no quality loss. Preferred by audiophiles and for archiving audio.
Open-source lossy compressed format. Often used in games and open-source projects where MP3 licensing is a concern.

rows: Better than MP3 at same size

blocks.heading: Workplace Application
blocks.text: Always record and edit audio in an uncompressed format (WAV) to preserve quality during production. Export to a compressed format (MP3 or AAC) only for final distribution. Re-compressing already-compressed audio multiplies quality loss.
Choosing the Right Format

items: Company logo for a website → SVG (vector, sharp at any size, small file)
Company logo for a printed banner → AI or EPS (vector, print-ready)
Photograph for a news article website → JPG (compressed, small, acceptable quality)
Graphic with a transparent background for web → PNG (supports transparency, lossless)
Audio recording to edit in Audacity → WAV (lossless, preserves all data for editing)
Podcast episode to upload to Spotify → MP3 at 128–192kbps (industry-standard distribution format)
Music track for an iPhone app → AAC (Apple's preferred format)

question.text: A graphic designer needs to provide a client's logo to a sign-making company for a 3-metre banner. Which file format is most appropriate?
options: JPG
PNG
SVG or EPS
GIF
question.explanation: Vector formats (SVG, EPS, AI) are required for large-format printing because they scale to any size without quality loss. JPG and PNG are raster formats that would pixelate when enlarged to banner size.

question.text: A podcaster records their episode in WAV format using Audacity. When they finish editing, which format should they export to for publishing on Spotify?
options: WAV — keep it as-is for best quality
MP3 — smaller file size, universally supported for streaming
FLAC — lossless compression
TIFF — professional audio format
question.explanation: MP3 (and AAC) are the standard distribution formats for podcasts. WAV files are far too large to stream efficiently. FLAC is lossless but not widely supported by streaming platforms. TIFF is an image format, not audio.

blocks.text: Software Licences

When you install software, you do not own it — you purchase (or receive for free) a licence to use it under specific conditions. Understanding licence types is essential for both legal compliance and making informed software decisions for yourself, your clients, and your organisation.

blocks.heading: Workplace Application
blocks.text: Using software beyond the terms of its licence is software piracy — a form of theft with serious legal consequences. IT professionals are responsible for ensuring their organisation's software is properly licensed. Audits by the Business Software Alliance (BSA) can result in significant fines.
Open Source vs Closed Source

items.definition: Software where the source code is publicly available. Anyone can view, modify, and distribute it (subject to the specific open-source licence terms). Examples: Linux, Firefox, LibreOffice, GIMP, Audacity, VLC.
Software where the source code is kept secret by the developer. Users can only run the software — they cannot modify or distribute it. Examples: Microsoft Windows, Adobe Photoshop, Microsoft Office.

blocks.text: Types of Software Licences

items.definition: The software is owned by a company. Users pay for a licence to use it but cannot view, modify, or redistribute the source code. Full terms defined by the EULA (End-User Licence Agreement).

Software distributed freely for a trial period or with limited functionality. Users are expected to pay for the full version after the trial. Example: WinRAR (technically shareware — the trial never expires but prompts for payment).
Software that is free to use permanently, but the source code is not available and the software cannot be modified or redistributed. Example: Adobe Acrobat Reader, Skype.
A widely-used open-source licence. Any software built using GPL code must also be released under the GPL — known as 'copyleft'. Example: Linux kernel, WordPress.

A permissive open-source licence. Developers can use, modify, and distribute the code — even in commercial closed-source projects — with minimal restrictions. Example: many web frameworks and libraries.

A family of licences primarily for creative works (images, music, text). Different CC licences specify whether attribution is required, whether commercial use is allowed, and whether derivatives must share the same licence.

blocks.text: Licence Type Comparison
Real-World Software Examples

apps.name: Microsoft Windows 11
apps.note: Closed source. Purchased via OEM licence (pre-installed on PCs) or retail. Cannot be copied or redistributed. Source code is a closely-guarded trade secret.

apps.name: Ubuntu Linux
apps.note: Free to download, use, modify, and redistribute. Source code is public. Powers a significant proportion of the world's web servers.

apps.name: Adobe Photoshop
apps.note: Industry-standard raster image editor. Requires a Creative Cloud subscription (~$35/month). Source code is closed. Cannot be shared between users.

apps.name: GIMP
apps.note: Free and open-source raster image editor. Equivalent to many Photoshop features. Widely used in education and by organisations with limited software budgets.

apps.name: Adobe Acrobat Reader
apps.note: Free to download and use permanently. Cannot be modified or redistributed. Adobe monetises through the paid Acrobat Pro (for editing PDFs).

apps.name: Canva (Free tier)
apps.note: Core features free permanently. Premium content and features (Canva Pro) require a subscription. Assets created in Canva are subject to Canva's own content licence.

blocks.text: Business Implications of Licence Choices

items: Cost: Open source and freeware reduce software costs, which is significant for small businesses and schools.
Support: Proprietary software typically includes vendor support. Open source relies on community forums and documentation.
Security: Open source code can be reviewed by anyone — vulnerabilities can be found and fixed quickly, but also exploited by attackers before patching.
Customisation: Open source allows modification to suit specific business needs. Proprietary software cannot be altered.
Compliance: Organisations must track all installed software and ensure it is correctly licensed to avoid legal risk.
Vendor lock-in: Proprietary software can create dependency on a single vendor — if they raise prices or discontinue the product, migrating is costly.

question.text: A small business wants to use image editing software but cannot afford Adobe Photoshop subscriptions. They want software they can use permanently for free. Which is the best option?
options: Microsoft Windows
WinRAR (shareware)
GIMP (open source)
Adobe Acrobat Reader
question.explanation: GIMP is a free, open-source raster image editor with capabilities comparable to Photoshop. It can be used permanently at no cost. WinRAR is shareware (requires eventual payment). Adobe Reader is freeware but is a PDF reader, not an image editor.

question.text: Freeware software provides access to its source code, allowing users to modify and redistribute it.
question.explanation: False. Freeware is free to use but the source code is not available and users cannot modify or redistribute it. Open source software is what provides access to source code.

blocks.title: Classify the Licence
blocks.instruction: Match each software description to its licence type.

pairs.term: Free to use, source code public, must keep derivatives open
Free to use permanently, no source code, cannot modify
Free trial with limited features, pay for full version
Must pay, source code secret, cannot copy or share
Free, source code public, can use in commercial projects

blocks.text: Software Management

In any organisation, managing software is as important as managing hardware. IT departments are responsible for ensuring that software is properly installed, kept up-to-date, and tracked across all devices. Poor software management leads to security vulnerabilities, licensing breaches, and compatibility problems.
Software Installation
Installing software involves more than just clicking 'Next'. IT professionals consider compatibility, security, licensing, and system requirements before deploying software across an organisation.

items: Check system requirements — does the hardware meet the minimum specifications (RAM, CPU, storage, OS version)?
Verify the source — only download software from official vendor websites or trusted repositories to avoid malware.
Check licence availability — ensure the organisation holds enough licences before installing.
Test before deploying — pilot-test new software on one or two machines before rolling it out across an organisation.
Administrator rights — installation often requires administrator privileges; in organisations, this is typically restricted to IT staff.
Silent/remote installation — enterprise IT departments use tools like Microsoft SCCM or Intune to push software to hundreds of machines remotely without visiting each device.

blocks.text: Software Updates and Patching

Software updates are released to add features, fix bugs, and most critically — close security vulnerabilities. A vulnerability that is left unpatched is an open door for attackers. The WannaCry ransomware attack of 2017 exploited a Windows vulnerability for which a patch had already been released — organisations that had not applied the update were compromised.

items.definition: A small software update that fixes a specific bug or security vulnerability without changing the major version of the software.
A significant new release of software (e.g., Windows 10 → Windows 11, Photoshop 2024 → 2025). May introduce new features, change the interface, or require hardware upgrades.
A setting that allows software to download and install updates without user intervention. Keeps security patches current but can cause disruption if a bad update is released.
Reverting to a previous version of software after an update causes problems. Important to have in an enterprise environment to recover from faulty updates.

blocks.heading: Industry Standard
blocks.text: In a professional environment, updates are never applied to production systems immediately. IT departments test updates in a staging environment first, then schedule deployment during maintenance windows (usually overnight or on weekends) to minimise disruption.
Software Version Control
Version numbering helps users and administrators understand the maturity and compatibility of software. Most software uses a format like MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH (e.g., 3.2.1).

rows: Significant changes — may break backward compatibility

blocks.text: IT Asset Management

IT Asset Management (ITAM) is the process of tracking all hardware and software in an organisation. A proper ITAM system records what software is installed, on which device, under which licence, and when licences expire.

items: Licence compliance: Know exactly how many licences you own vs how many are installed. Over-deployment is a legal risk; under-deployment is wasted money.
Renewal tracking: Software subscriptions and licence agreements expire. ITAM systems alert IT managers before renewals are due.
End-of-life software: Vendors stop releasing security updates for old software versions (e.g., Windows 7 end-of-life in January 2020). ITAM helps identify and replace unsupported software.
Software audit readiness: Companies like Adobe and Microsoft conduct licence audits. Organisations with proper ITAM records can respond quickly and confidently.
Tools used: Microsoft SCCM, ManageEngine, Lansweeper, Snow Software, and Ivanti are common enterprise ITAM platforms.

blocks.heading: Workplace Application
blocks.text: Many small businesses fail to track their software licences properly. As an IT professional entering the workforce, creating and maintaining a simple software register (even in a spreadsheet) can save an organisation from costly compliance failures.

blocks.title: Software Audit Register
blocks.intro: Create a software asset register that an IT manager could use to track installed software across a small organisation of 10 computers.
steps: Open a new spreadsheet. Create the following column headers in row 1: Device Name, Software Name, Version, Licence Type, Licence Key (last 5 digits), Licences Owned, Licences Used, Licence Expiry, Vendor Contact, Notes.
Bold the header row and apply a fill colour to distinguish it from data rows.
Enter data for at least 8 software titles. Include a mix of proprietary (e.g., Microsoft Office 365), open source (e.g., GIMP), and freeware (e.g., Adobe Reader).
In the Licence Type column, create a drop-down validation list with the options: Proprietary, Open Source, Freeware, Shareware, SaaS Subscription.
In a column to the right of your data, use a formula to flag any software where Licences Used exceeds Licences Owned. Use an IF formula: =IF(G2>F2,"OVER-LICENSED","OK").
Add conditional formatting to that flag column: red fill for 'OVER-LICENSED', green fill for 'OK'.
Add a Licence Expiry column. Use conditional formatting to highlight any expiry date within the next 90 days in orange.
Freeze row 1 so headers remain visible when scrolling.
Save the file as 'Software_Asset_Register_[YourName].xlsx'.

blocks.text: Advanced Spreadsheet Design

A well-designed spreadsheet is not just functional — it communicates clearly, is easy to navigate, and looks professional. In Year 12, you are expected to go beyond entering data and formulas. You need to apply composition, layout, and design principles to produce spreadsheets that a client or employer could use without guidance.
Composition and Layout Principles for Spreadsheets
The same design principles you apply to posters and websites apply to spreadsheets. A spreadsheet is a visual product — poor layout makes data harder to read and increases errors.

rows: Most important information at the top; summaries prominent
Title in row 1, key totals in a shaded summary section at the top
Data consistently left-, right-, or centre-aligned by type
Numbers right-aligned; text left-aligned; headers centred
Same formatting throughout — fonts, colours, number formats
All currency cells formatted as $#,##0.00; all headings in the same font and colour
Row height and column width adjusted; empty rows between sections
Extra row between data table and summary; adequate column widths so data is not truncated
Headers and key cells clearly distinguished from data
Bold header row with dark background and white text
Minimal colours; only what aids comprehension
Two accent colours maximum; avoid decorative borders that add clutter
Related columns close together; summary section clearly separated

blocks.text: Professional Formatting Techniques

items: Use a clear, descriptive title in a merged cell at the top of the spreadsheet.
Apply a consistent colour palette — one accent colour for headers, one for totals/summaries.
Format numbers appropriately: currency as $#,##0.00; percentages as 0.00%; dates as DD/MM/YYYY.
Freeze the top row (and sometimes the leftmost column) so headers remain visible when scrolling.
Use named ranges to make formulas readable: =SUM(Q1_Sales) instead of =SUM(B2:B32).
Protect formula cells from accidental editing using cell protection (Review > Protect Sheet).
Use data validation to restrict user input to valid values (e.g., dropdown lists, number ranges).
Remove gridlines from the printed view and add your own borders to define the data clearly.
Include a date stamp, version number, and author name in a footer or dedicated info row.

blocks.text: Chart Design for Data Presentation

Charts must support the data they display — not just fill space. Every chart needs a title, labelled axes, and a legend where applicable. Chart type choice should match the message you are communicating.

rows: Showing proportions of a whole (use sparingly, max 6 segments)
Age vs income; temperature vs sales
Comparing parts of a whole across categories
Comparing two data series with different scales

blocks.heading: Industry Standard
blocks.text: Professional analysts avoid 3D charts and gratuitous special effects. 3D charts distort proportions and make values harder to read. Keep charts clean: remove unnecessary gridlines, use a white or neutral background, and let the data speak.
Advanced Formulas for Analysis

blocks.title: SUMIF — Sum values that meet a condition
blocks.defaultCode:
=SUMIF(range, criteria, sum_range)

-- Example: Total sales for the 'West' region only
=SUMIF(B2:B100, "West", C2:C100)

-- Example: Total sales greater than $500
=SUMIF(C2:C100, ">500", C2:C100)
blocks.challenge: Write a SUMIF formula that totals sales in column D only where the product category in column A is 'Software'.

blocks.title: COUNTIF — Count cells that meet a condition
blocks.defaultCode:
=COUNTIF(range, criteria)

-- Example: Count how many sales reps exceeded $10,000 in sales
=COUNTIF(D2:D50, ">10000")

-- Example: Count how many orders are 'Pending'
=COUNTIF(E2:E100, "Pending")
blocks.challenge: Write a COUNTIF formula that counts how many cells in B2:B200 contain the word 'Completed'.

blocks.title: AVERAGEIF — Average values that meet a condition
blocks.defaultCode:
=AVERAGEIF(range, criteria, average_range)

-- Example: Average score for students in 'Class A'
=AVERAGEIF(A2:A30, "Class A", C2:C30)
blocks.challenge: Write an AVERAGEIF formula that calculates the average sales amount (column C) for all orders from 'Perth' (column B).

blocks.text: Spreadsheet Design Checklist

items: Title clearly identifies the spreadsheet's purpose
Header row is bold and visually distinct from data
Numbers are formatted consistently (currency, percentage, dates)
Formulas are correct and update automatically when data changes
Charts have titles, labelled axes, and a legend where applicable
Colours are consistent and purposeful — not decorative
Row 1 is frozen for easy scrolling through long datasets
Data validation is applied where user input is expected
Summary totals are clearly separated and easy to locate
File is saved with a clear, descriptive filename

blocks.title: Professional Sales Dashboard
blocks.intro: Design and build a professional sales report spreadsheet for a fictional IT company. Apply all layout and design principles from this lesson.
steps: Open a new blank spreadsheet. In row 1, merge cells A1:H1 and type the title 'Coastal IT Solutions — Q3 Sales Report'. Apply a dark blue background with white bold text, font size 16.
In row 2, add a subtitle showing the reporting period: 'July – September 2025'. Apply a lighter blue background, font size 12, italic.
Leave row 3 blank for visual separation. In row 4, add these column headers: Sale ID, Sales Rep, Region, Product Category, Units Sold, Unit Price, Total Revenue, Commission (10%).
Bold the header row. Apply a medium blue fill and white text. Centre all headers.
Enter data for 20 fictional sales. Use three regions (North, South, West), three product categories (Hardware, Software, Services), and vary units and prices.
In column G (Total Revenue), write a formula: =E5*F5. Copy down for all 20 rows.
In column H (Commission), write a formula calculating 10% of revenue: =G5*0.1. Copy down for all rows.

Below the data, create a Summary section (leave a blank row). Calculate: Total Revenue (SUM), Total Commission (SUM), Average Sale Value (AVERAGE), Best Region (use SUMIF for each region), Top Product Category (use SUMIF).
Create a bar chart showing Total Revenue by Region. Give it the title 'Revenue by Region — Q3 2025'. Delete chart gridlines and apply a clean white background.
Create a pie chart showing revenue split by Product Category with a clear legend.
Format all revenue and commission cells as currency ($#,##0.00). Format 'Units Sold' as a whole number.
Freeze row 4 (the header row). Add alternating row shading (light grey / white) to the data rows for readability.
Set up print area to include only the data and charts. Add a footer with your name and the date.
Save as 'Sales_Dashboard_Q3_[YourName].xlsx'.

blocks.text: Sorting, Filtering and Data Analysis

Raw data is only useful when you can find patterns and make decisions from it. Sorting and filtering are the most fundamental data organisation tools in any spreadsheet application — and conditional formatting makes those patterns immediately visible. Together, these tools allow you to extract meaningful insights for clients and stakeholders.
Sorting Data
Sorting rearranges rows in your dataset based on the values in one or more columns. It is one of the first steps in organising data for analysis or presentation.

items.definition: Arranges data from smallest to largest (A–Z for text, 0–9 for numbers, earliest to latest for dates).
Arranges data from largest to smallest (Z–A for text, 9–0 for numbers, latest to earliest for dates).
Sorting by more than one column — e.g., first by Region (A–Z), then by Sales (highest to lowest) within each region.

blocks.text: Always select the entire dataset (all columns) before sorting. Sorting only one column while leaving others in place scrambles your data — names get matched to the wrong values.
Filtering Data

Filters hide rows that do not match your criteria, letting you focus on a subset of your data without deleting anything. AutoFilter is the most common tool, but advanced filters give much more control.

rows: Drop-down menus on each column header to filter by value, text, or number rules
Quick filtering of a single column — e.g., show only 'West' region sales
Filter by conditions: greater than, less than, between, top 10, above average
Filter by begins with, ends with, contains, does not contain
Finding all products with 'Pro' in the name
Filter by specific date, date range, this week/month/quarter
Viewing all sales from last quarter
Apply filters to more than one column simultaneously
Show only 'Software' sales in 'North' region above $1,000

blocks.text: Conditional Formatting

Conditional formatting automatically applies colour, icons, or data bars to cells based on their values. It turns a table of numbers into a visual dashboard — patterns and outliers become instantly visible.

items: Highlight cells greater than a value — e.g., all sales over $5,000 in green
Colour scale — gradient from red (low) to green (high) across a column
Data bars — small horizontal bars inside cells showing relative magnitude
Icon sets — traffic lights, arrows, stars applied to cells based on value ranges
Duplicate values — highlight any repeated entries in a column (useful for data cleaning)
Top/bottom rules — automatically highlight the top 10% or bottom 10 values in a range

blocks.heading: Workplace Application
blocks.text: Conditional formatting is used in business dashboards to make performance immediately readable. A sales manager can look at a red/amber/green report and instantly identify which regions need attention — without reading every number.
Data-Driven Decision Making

Sorting and filtering are not ends in themselves — they support decision making. When working with a client, you need to identify what question they are trying to answer, then select the right technique to surface that answer from the data.

rows: Who are our top 5 highest-value customers?
Sort by Total Purchase (descending); take first 5 rows
Which products have not sold in the past 30 days?
Filter by Date of Last Sale; apply 'before [date 30 days ago]' filter
Are any staff consistently missing their targets?
Multi-level sort by Department then by Sales (ascending)
Which regions are below average performance?
AVERAGEIF formula + conditional formatting (red below average)
COUNTIF with date criteria; filter to show overdue only

blocks.title: COUNTIF with date and text criteria
blocks.defaultCode:
-- Count orders placed after 1 July 2025
=COUNTIF(A2:A100, ">01/07/2025")

-- Count cells containing 'Overdue' in status column
=COUNTIF(D2:D100, "Overdue")

-- Count sales above the column average
=COUNTIF(C2:C100, ">"&AVERAGE(C2:C100))
blocks.challenge: Write a COUNTIF formula that counts how many values in column B are greater than $1,000.

blocks.title: Retail Sales Analysis for a Client
blocks.intro: A retail client has given you a spreadsheet of 50 sales transactions. Your job is to analyse the data and prepare a sorted, filtered report with conditional formatting to help them identify trends and make decisions.
steps: Open a new spreadsheet and set up the following columns: Transaction ID, Date, Customer Name, Region, Product Category, Units Sold, Unit Price, Total Sale, Payment Method, Status.

Enter 50 rows of realistic fictional data. Use three regions, three product categories (Electronics, Clothing, Homewares), three payment methods (Card, Cash, Online), and two status values (Completed, Pending).
Calculate Total Sale in column H using the formula =F2*G2. Copy down for all rows.
Apply AutoFilter to all columns (Data > Filter). Test your filters by showing only 'Completed' transactions from the 'North' region.
Sort the data by Total Sale, descending. Identify the top 5 sales transactions — add a note column and mark them 'High Value'.
Apply a colour scale conditional format to the Total Sale column (red for low values, green for high values).
Apply a conditional format to the Status column: 'Completed' rows in light green, 'Pending' rows in light orange.
Create a summary table below the data (separated by two blank rows). Use SUMIF to calculate total sales by Region and by Product Category.
Create a COUNTIF formula that counts how many transactions are 'Pending'.
Insert a bar chart showing Total Sales by Region. Title it 'Sales by Region — Client Report'.
Use a multi-level sort: first by Region (A–Z), then by Total Sale (descending) within each region.
Save as 'Client_Sales_Analysis_[YourName].xlsx' and export a PDF version of the data range and charts only.

question.text: A spreadsheet contains 500 rows of sales data. A manager wants to see only transactions from the 'East' region where the sale value is greater than $2,000. Which technique is most appropriate?
options: Sort the data by region descending
Apply multi-column AutoFilter: Region = 'East' AND Total Sale > 2000
Use conditional formatting to highlight East region rows
Delete all rows that do not match the criteria
question.explanation: Multi-column AutoFilter is the correct tool — it hides non-matching rows temporarily without deleting any data. Sorting alone would not filter out rows, conditional formatting only changes appearance, and deleting rows would permanently remove data.

question.text: A client asks: 'Which product category generates the most revenue?' Describe the steps you would take in a spreadsheet to answer this question.
question.explanation: Use SUMIF to calculate total revenue for each product category. Then either compare the results directly, sort the summary table by revenue (descending), or create a bar/pie chart showing revenue by category. The category with the highest SUMIF total is the answer.

blocks.text: Integrated Digital Production

Professional digital projects rarely use just one tool or one skill. A complete digital product might combine a professionally designed logo (vector graphics), photographs (raster graphics), background music or narration (audio), and a data-driven report (spreadsheet). In this project, you will combine the skills from all previous lessons to produce a complete digital product for a client brief.

blocks.heading: About This Lesson
blocks.text: This is a 50–60 minute project task. It is designed to assess your ability to select and apply appropriate file formats, software, and design principles across a complete digital product. Work through each stage in order and save your work regularly.
The Client Brief

Your client is Nexus Digital — a small IT services company. They have asked you to produce a digital media package to support a new service they are launching: a monthly IT Health Check subscription for small businesses.

items: A professional logo for the 'IT Health Check' service (vector graphic, SVG format)
A promotional banner image for the company website (raster graphic, PNG format, 1200 x 400 px)
A short audio introduction (15–30 seconds) for a podcast or website landing page
A pricing and service comparison spreadsheet that the client can provide to potential customers
A brief written report (within the spreadsheet or as a separate document) explaining your file format choices and software decisions

blocks.text: Stage 1: Logo Design (Vector Graphic)
Use Canva (free) or Adobe Illustrator if available. In Canva, use a Logo template and download as SVG. Alternatively, use Inkscape (free, open source vector editor).

blocks.title: IT Health Check Logo
blocks.intro: Design a professional logo for the 'IT Health Check' service. The logo must be clean, modern, and convey trust and technology.
steps: Open your chosen vector software. Create a new document sized 500 x 500px (square logos scale well).
Design a logo that includes: the text 'IT Health Check', a simple icon (e.g., a shield, tick, or monitor), and a consistent colour palette of no more than 3 colours.
Use clean sans-serif typography (e.g., Montserrat, Roboto, or Open Sans). Do not use more than two font styles.
Ensure the logo works on both a white background and a dark background (test both).
Export/download as SVG format. Also export as PNG at 500x500px for raster use.
In your report, explain: Why is SVG the preferred format for a logo? What would happen if you used JPG instead?

blocks.text: Stage 2: Promotional Banner (Raster Graphic)

blocks.title: Website Promotional Banner
blocks.intro: Create a website banner for Nexus Digital advertising the IT Health Check service. Dimensions: 1200 x 400 pixels.
steps: Create a new document at 1200 x 400px. This is a standard web banner size (also called a leaderboard banner).
Include: the company name 'Nexus Digital', the service name 'IT Health Check', a brief tagline (e.g., 'Keep your business running smoothly'), and a call-to-action button ('Get Started Today').
Use the same colour palette as your logo for brand consistency.
Use a stock photo or abstract background appropriate to an IT services company (avoid clip art).
Place your SVG logo on the banner — ensure it looks sharp at this resolution.
Export as PNG at 1200 x 400px. Explain in your report: Why PNG and not JPG for this banner? Under what circumstances would JPG be acceptable?

blocks.text: Stage 3: Audio Introduction
Use Audacity (free, open source) to record and edit your audio. Record in a quiet environment. Even a built-in laptop microphone works if background noise is minimised.

blocks.title: Podcast/Website Audio Introduction
blocks.intro: Record and edit a 15–30 second audio introduction for the Nexus Digital IT Health Check service. This will be used on the company website and podcast.
steps: Open Audacity. Set the recording sample rate to 44100 Hz (CD quality). Check your microphone input is selected.

Write a short script (3–5 sentences) introducing the IT Health Check service. Example: 'Welcome to Nexus Digital's IT Health Check — the smart way for small businesses to stay secure and running smoothly. Our monthly check covers security updates, software licences, backup verification, and more. Get started today at nexusdigital.com.au.'
Record your script. Aim for a confident, professional tone. Record 2–3 takes and select the best one.
