Edit the audio: trim silence at the start and end, remove any obvious errors or long pauses, and adjust volume levels so the audio is clear and consistent.
Export as WAV (for your working master file). Also export as MP3 at 128kbps for web distribution.
In your report, explain why you saved two versions: one WAV and one MP3. What would be lost if you only saved MP3 from the start?

blocks.text: Stage 4: Pricing and Service Comparison Spreadsheet

blocks.title: Service Pricing Spreadsheet
blocks.intro: Build a professional pricing and service comparison spreadsheet that Nexus Digital can provide to potential customers comparing their three subscription tiers.
steps: Create a new spreadsheet. Title: 'Nexus Digital — IT Health Check Pricing Guide'. Apply professional formatting consistent with the brand colours from your logo.
Create three subscription tiers: Basic ($49/month), Standard ($99/month), and Premium ($199/month). These are the column headers.

List the following services as row headers: Security Updates Check, Software Licence Audit, Backup Verification, Network Performance Report, Malware Scan, Priority Support, Monthly Report, Quarterly Review Meeting.
Mark each cell with a tick (✓) or cross (✗) to indicate which features are included at each tier. Use conditional formatting: green fill for ✓, light red fill for ✗.
Below the feature table, add a row for 'Annual Cost (12 months)' using a formula: =monthly_price * 12 for each tier.
Add a 'Savings vs Monthly' row that calculates how much the client saves if Nexus Digital offers a 10% discount for annual payment: =annual_cost * 0.1.
Format all dollar amounts as currency. Bold the pricing row and the savings row.
Add a callout row at the bottom: 'Most Popular: Standard Plan — best value for small businesses with 1–10 staff.' Merge the cell across all columns and apply a prominent background colour.
Export as PDF for client distribution. Save the source file as 'Nexus_Pricing_Guide_[YourName].xlsx'.

blocks.text: Stage 5: Reflection Report

blocks.title: Project Reflection
blocks.scenario: You have completed the Nexus Digital digital media package. Now reflect on your technical and design decisions.

questions.question: Why did you choose SVG for the logo rather than PNG or JPG? What would happen if the logo was sent to a sign company as a JPG file?
You recorded your audio in WAV format before exporting to MP3. Explain the difference between these formats and why working in WAV first is better practice.
What licence type applies to Audacity and GIMP? How does this affect whether your school or workplace can use them?
Describe two composition/design principles you applied to the pricing spreadsheet and explain how each improves the document for the client.

blocks.text: Submission Checklist

items: Logo: SVG file + PNG export
Banner: PNG file at 1200 x 400px
Audio: WAV master + MP3 distribution version
Spreadsheet: XLSX source file + PDF export
Reflection report: Answers to all four reflection questions (can be added as a second sheet in the spreadsheet)
All files named clearly: Nexus_[Asset]_[YourName].[ext]

question.text: A student saves their logo as a JPG file and delivers it to a printing company for use on a 2-metre display banner. What problem will occur?
options: The logo will display in the wrong colours
The JPG file will be too large to open
The logo will be pixelated and blurry when scaled up to banner size
JPG files are not supported by printing companies
question.explanation: JPG is a raster format — it stores the image as a fixed grid of pixels. When enlarged to banner size, those pixels become visible and the image looks blurry or blocky. A vector format (SVG, EPS, AI) is required for large-format printing because it scales without quality loss.

question.text: Audacity and GIMP are both used in this project. What type of software licence applies to both of them?
options: Proprietary — you need to purchase a licence
Shareware — free to trial, pay after 30 days
Freeware — free to use but cannot be modified
Open source — free to use, modify, and redistribute
question.explanation: Both Audacity and GIMP are open-source software released under the GNU General Public Licence (GPL). They are free to download, use, modify, and redistribute. This makes them excellent choices for educational and small business settings where software costs are a concern.

blocks.text: Understanding the Client Brief

In professional IT work, every project begins with a client brief — a document or conversation that defines what the client wants, why they want it, and what success looks like. Your ability to read and interpret a brief accurately determines whether the final product actually solves the client's problem.

blocks.heading: Career Path: Project Coordinator
blocks.text: Project coordinators spend a significant portion of their time clarifying requirements with clients. A misunderstood brief at the start of a project costs far more to fix at the end. In industry, this is called 'the cost of change' — the later a mistake is caught, the more expensive it is to fix.
What Is a Client Brief?

A client brief outlines the context and requirements for a project. It is the starting point for all planning decisions. In Applied IT, your brief may be provided by a teacher, a simulated client scenario, or — in industry placement — a real business client.

items.definition: A document or statement from the client describing the project purpose, requirements, constraints, and expected outcomes.
The boundaries of a project — what IS included and, importantly, what is NOT included.
The process of examining a brief to extract, clarify, and prioritise what the solution must do.
A limitation placed on the project — such as time, budget, software tools, or audience.

blocks.text: The Four Dimensions of a Brief
When you receive a brief, examine it through four lenses:

rows: What must be included? What is out of scope? How big is this project?
Prevents scope creep — the gradual expansion of project requirements beyond what was agreed.
When is it due? Are there milestones? How many work sessions do I have?
Drives your Gantt chart and task prioritisation.
What software, hardware, assets, or data do I have access to? What skills do I need?
Identifies gaps early — before they become problems.
Why does the client need this? Who will use it? What problem does it solve?
Ensures the solution is fit for purpose, not just technically complete.

blocks.text: Asking the Right Questions

Clients rarely provide every detail in a brief. Part of your job is to identify the gaps and ask targeted questions. Good questions are specific, open-ended, and focused on understanding — not on seeking approval.

items: Who specifically will be using the final product?
Are there existing brand guidelines, colours, or styles I need to follow?
What format should the final deliverable be in (print, web, video, app)?
Are there any content or features that are definitely NOT required?
What does a successful outcome look like to you?

blocks.heading: Project Manager Tip
blocks.text: Document every clarification. If a client answers a question verbally, follow up with a written summary: 'Just to confirm, you said the app does not need to work offline — is that correct?' This protects you if requirements change later.

blocks.title: Client Brief: Local Music Festival
blocks.scenario: You have been approached by the coordinator of a local community music festival. She says: 'We need something digital to promote the festival. We want people to know when it is, who is playing, and how to get tickets. Our audience is mainly 18–35 year olds and we use a lot of Instagram. Oh, and we need it done in three weeks — our graphic designer is busy so it's just you.' No other written documentation has been provided.

questions.question: Identify THREE pieces of information that are missing or unclear in this brief that you would need to clarify before starting work.
Write two targeted clarifying questions you would ask the client, explaining why each question is important for your planning.
Define the scope of this project in your own words, including at least one thing that is explicitly out of scope based on what the client has said.

blocks.text: Interpreting the Brief: Your Written Response

Once you have read and clarified the brief, you write an 'interpreted brief' — a restatement of the requirements in your own words. This shows the client (and your assessor) that you have understood the project, not just copied the brief.

items: State the purpose of the product (what it is and what problem it solves).
Describe the target audience (who will use it and what their needs are).
List the functional requirements (what it must do).
List the constraints (time, tools, format, content boundaries).
State your evaluation criteria (how you will know the solution is successful).

question.text: A client tells you verbally that the project does NOT need to include a mobile version. What is the most professionally appropriate next step?
options: Proceed with the project and leave mobile out, as instructed.
Send the client a written summary confirming that mobile is out of scope.
Include mobile anyway in case the client changes their mind.
Ask the client to rewrite the brief to include this detail.
question.explanation: Verbal agreements should always be confirmed in writing. Sending a summary protects both you and the client if requirements are disputed later — a standard practice in professional project management.

question.text: Explain the difference between scope and constraints in the context of a client brief. Give one example of each.

blocks.text: Planning Your Project

Professional project planning transforms a client brief into an actionable schedule. It forces you to break large, vague tasks into specific, time-bound actions — and it gives you an early warning when something is falling behind.

blocks.heading: Industry Standard
blocks.text: The Project Management Institute (PMI) reports that organisations with mature project planning practices waste 28 times less money on failed projects than those without. Planning is not busywork — it is the highest-leverage activity in any project.
Scope Creep: The Silent Project Killer

Scope creep occurs when the project gradually expands beyond what was originally agreed — one small addition at a time. Each individual addition seems reasonable, but together they blow out time and resources. Recognising and managing scope creep is one of the most important skills a project manager can have.

blocks.definition: The gradual, uncontrolled expansion of a project's scope beyond what was originally agreed, typically without corresponding adjustments to time or resources.

items: Signs of scope creep: 'Can we just add one more feature?', new requirements appearing after work has started, the brief being reinterpreted mid-project.
Prevention: Document the scope clearly at the start; get client sign-off on the interpreted brief.
Response: When new requirements arise, assess their impact on time and resources, then negotiate with the client — add time, remove another feature, or defer the addition to a future phase.

blocks.text: Time Management: Breaking Down the Work

Before you create a schedule, list every task required to complete the project. This is called a Work Breakdown Structure (WBS). A WBS turns 'create a website' into specific, estimable tasks like 'design homepage wireframe', 'source and edit hero image', 'write navigation labels'.

items.definition: A hierarchical decomposition of all the work required to complete a project, broken into manageable tasks.
A horizontal bar chart that plots project tasks on a timeline, showing start and end dates, duration, and task dependencies.
A significant checkpoint in a project — often a deliverable or decision point — that marks progress.
A relationship where one task cannot start (or finish) until another task is complete.

blocks.text: Reading a Gantt Chart

A Gantt chart has tasks listed vertically on the left and a calendar timeline across the top. Each task is shown as a horizontal bar spanning its start and end date. Milestones are often shown as diamonds. Dependency arrows show which tasks must finish before others can begin.

rows: Milestone — a key deliverable or checkpoint
Task dependency — B cannot start until A finishes
Build site cannot start until wireframes are approved
Task is delayed or at risk
Site build planned for Day 5 but started on Day 7

blocks.text: Resource Allocation

Resources are everything required to complete the project: time, people, software, hardware, stock images, fonts, data, and even physical space. Identifying resources early prevents being blocked mid-project because you lack access to a tool or asset.

rows: Adobe Illustrator, Figma, Microsoft Publisher, DaVinci Resolve
Confirm you have access and a valid licence before starting
Identify sources; check licence (royalty-free vs commercial)
School computers, drawing tablet, scanner, camera
Book equipment in advance if shared
Map to your Gantt chart; identify your critical path
Identify gaps early; plan learning time or seek support

blocks.heading: Project Manager Tip
blocks.text: When estimating how long tasks will take, use the '3-point estimation' technique: estimate the best case, the worst case, and the most likely time. Use the most likely estimate in your Gantt chart, but build buffer time before critical milestones.

blocks.title: Build Your Project Gantt Chart
blocks.software: Microsoft Excel / Google Sheets / Canva / GanttProject (free)
blocks.intro: Using a brief provided by your teacher (or the music festival scenario from Lesson 1), create a Gantt chart for the full project. Your chart must show at least 10 tasks, 2 milestones, and at least one dependency.
steps: List every task needed to complete the project. Aim for tasks that are 1–3 days in duration — if a task takes longer, break it down further.
Identify dependencies: which tasks cannot start until another is finished? Draw these relationships on paper first.
Estimate the time for each task using 3-point estimation (best/worst/likely). Record your estimates.
Identify your milestones: client sign-off on design, completion of first draft, final submission.
Plot all tasks and milestones on your Gantt chart, colour-coding by phase (plan / create / review / evaluate).
Add a 'resources' column to your task list, noting what you need for each task.
Share your chart with a classmate and ask them to identify any gaps, missing tasks, or unrealistic time estimates.
Annotate your chart with a brief note explaining two planning decisions you made (e.g., why you allowed 2 days for the wireframe review).

question.text: Halfway through a project, your client asks you to add three new pages to the website that were not in the original brief. What is the correct project management response?
options: Add the pages immediately — the client is always right.
Refuse to add anything that was not in the original brief.
Assess the impact on time and resources, then negotiate a scope change or timeline extension with the client.
Add the pages and work extra hours to keep the same deadline.
question.explanation: New requirements mid-project are a scope change. The professional response is to assess the impact, document it, and negotiate with the client — adjusting the deadline, removing other features, or agreeing to defer the additions to a future version.

question.text: A student has one week to create a promotional video. They spend the first three days filming without creating a Gantt chart. On day four they realise they have no time left for editing. Explain what project management technique would have prevented this problem, and describe how it works.

blocks.text: Design Representation Techniques

Before you build anything, you design it. Design representation techniques are the visual tools that let you plan, communicate, and test your design ideas before committing hours of build time. They are used by professional UX designers, web developers, video producers, and software engineers alike.

blocks.heading: Career Path: UX Designer
blocks.text: UX (User Experience) Designers spend most of their time in the design representation phase — sketching ideas, building wireframes, creating prototypes, and testing with users — before any final product is built. Mastering these techniques is a direct pathway to a career in UX, UI design, or digital product management.
Why Represent Your Design First?

items: Identify problems in the layout or user flow before they are built in — changes are free on paper, expensive in code.
Communicate your vision to clients and team members who may not read technical documentation.
Get client approval on the look and feel before investing build time.
Test usability: can a user find what they need without instruction?

blocks.text: Technique 1: Thumbnails

Thumbnails are rapid, small-scale sketches that explore multiple layout ideas quickly. Done by hand or digitally, they are intentionally rough — the goal is quantity of ideas, not quality of drawing. A designer might produce 10–20 thumbnails in 20 minutes before selecting 2–3 to develop further.

blocks.definition: A small, rapid, rough sketch used to explore multiple layout or design concepts quickly before committing to a direction.

blocks.heading: Industry Standard
blocks.text: Professional designers at companies like Google and Apple routinely fill pages with thumbnail sketches before opening any digital tool. Do not skip this step because you think your first idea is good — the best designs usually emerge from comparison, not inspiration.
Technique 2: Annotated Sketches and Diagrams

An annotated sketch is a more refined drawing with written notes (annotations) that explain design decisions. Annotations clarify things that the drawing cannot: colour choices, font sizes, interactive behaviour, content type, or the reasoning behind a layout decision.

blocks.definition: A hand-drawn or digital design diagram with written notes explaining design decisions, colour choices, content, and functionality.

items: Label all major elements: navigation, hero image, body text, call-to-action button.
Note colours using hex codes, colour names, or references to a brand guide.
Explain why a design decision was made: 'Navigation at top — standard web convention; users expect it here.'
For interactive elements, note what happens on click or hover.
Annotations can be handwritten or typed and added digitally to a scanned sketch.

blocks.text: Technique 3: Wireframes

A wireframe is a structural blueprint of a screen or page. It shows the layout and hierarchy of content — what goes where — without any visual styling (colour, fonts, final images). Wireframes use boxes for images, lines for text, and simple shapes to represent UI components.

blocks.definition: A low-fidelity structural layout of a screen or page that shows content placement and hierarchy without visual design styling.

items.label: Low fidelity (Lo-fi)
points: Hand-drawn or simple digital boxes
No colour or real content
Used in early planning stages
Fast to produce; easy to change
Good for testing layout concepts

items.label: High fidelity (Hi-fi)
points: Digital tool (Figma, Adobe XD, Balsamiq)
Actual colours, fonts, images, and content
Used for client presentations and developer handoff
Takes longer; reflects near-final design
Can be made interactive (clickable prototype)

apps.name: Figma
apps.note: Industry standard for UI/UX design. Browser-based with real-time collaboration. Used at Google, Microsoft, Airbnb. Excellent for both wireframing and hi-fi prototypes.

apps.name: Balsamiq
apps.note: Specialist wireframing tool with a 'sketchy' aesthetic that keeps focus on structure. Very fast for lo-fi wireframes. Popular in agile teams.

apps.name: Adobe XD
apps.note: Part of the Adobe Creative Cloud. Strong prototyping features and integration with Photoshop and Illustrator.

apps.name: Canva
apps.note: Accessible for beginners. Not a specialist wireframing tool but useful for simple layout mockups and poster/presentation design planning.

blocks.text: Technique 4: Storyboards

A storyboard is a sequence of frames that shows how a product will unfold over time — used for video, animation, interactive media, and user journeys through websites or apps. Each frame represents a moment, screen, or scene, with annotations describing the content, transitions, and timing.

blocks.definition: A sequence of annotated frames that represents the flow of a video, animation, interactive experience, or user journey through a digital product.

rows: Sketch or image of what is seen on screen or in frame
What is heard — music, narration, sound effects
How the scene changes — cut, fade, dissolve, swipe
Titles, captions, subtitles, or UI labels
What happens — button press, animation, camera move

blocks.title: Create a Wireframe for a Client Landing Page
blocks.software: Figma (free) or hand-drawn on A4 paper
blocks.intro: Using the music festival brief from Lesson 1, create a lo-fi wireframe for the festival's landing page. Your wireframe must clearly show content hierarchy and all major page sections.
steps: Start with 3 thumbnail sketches exploring different layouts for the landing page. Consider: Where is the event name? Where is the ticket link? What is the hero visual?
Choose your strongest thumbnail and develop it into a labelled wireframe on A4 (or in Figma).
Include these sections in your wireframe: navigation bar, hero section (event name, date, location), lineup preview, ticket purchase call-to-action, footer with social links.
Use boxes with an 'X' through them to represent images. Use horizontal lines to represent text blocks. Label each element.
Add annotations explaining at least four design decisions. For example: 'Ticket button placed above the fold — users should not need to scroll to find it.'
If using Figma: create your wireframe using only rectangles, lines, and text. Do not add colours or final fonts yet.
Present your wireframe to a partner. Ask: Can they identify where to buy tickets without you explaining? Record their feedback.

blocks.title: Create a Storyboard for a Promotional Video
blocks.intro: The music festival client wants a 30-second promotional video for Instagram. Create a storyboard planning the full video sequence.
steps: Plan the video structure: Introduction (0–5s), Key information (5–20s), Call to action (20–30s).
Create at least 8 storyboard frames covering the full 30 seconds.
For each frame, sketch the visual, note the audio/music, write any on-screen text, and note the transition to the next frame.
Include at least one text overlay (event name, date), one transition effect (fade or cut), and one call to action ('Get tickets at...').
Annotate two frames in detail, explaining why you chose that visual approach for that moment in the video.
Review your storyboard: does the sequence make sense without sound? If not, revise it.

question.text: What is the key difference between a wireframe and a high-fidelity mockup?
options: A wireframe is used for video projects; a mockup is used for web projects.
A wireframe shows structure without visual styling; a mockup includes colours, fonts, and near-final design.
A wireframe must be hand-drawn; a mockup must be created in a digital tool.
A wireframe is submitted to the client; a mockup is only for the developer.
question.explanation: A wireframe focuses on layout and content hierarchy without any visual design styling. A high-fidelity mockup adds colour, typography, imagery, and detailed styling to show what the final product will look like.

question.text: A student creates a detailed, colourful hi-fi mockup as their first design step, without producing any thumbnail sketches or lo-fi wireframes. Identify TWO problems with this approach and explain how the missing steps would have helped.

blocks.text: Target Audience and Presentation Medium

The most technically perfect product fails if it is designed for the wrong person or delivered in the wrong format. Understanding your target audience and choosing the right presentation medium are core skills in the design process — and are explicitly required in SCSA AIT Unit 3 project work.
Target Audience Analysis

Your target audience is the specific group of people who will use your product. Analysing the audience means going beyond 'young people' or 'adults' to understand their digital habits, preferences, technical ability, context of use, and what they actually need from the product.

items.definition: The specific group of people for whom a digital product is designed, defined by characteristics such as age, digital literacy, interests, and context of use.
A fictional but research-based profile of a typical user, used to guide design decisions and keep the team focused on user needs.
The environment and circumstances in which a user will interact with the product — e.g., on a phone while commuting, or at a desk in a quiet office.

blocks.text: Target Audience Characteristics
When describing your target audience in project documentation, address the following characteristics:

rows: Are they children, teens, adults, seniors?
Affects font size, language complexity, visual style, and assumed knowledge.
Are they confident tech users or occasional users?
Low literacy → simpler navigation, more guidance, fewer features. High literacy → richer functionality acceptable.
Desktop, mobile, tablet? iOS, Android, Windows?
Determines responsive design requirements and file format choices.
Where and when will they use it? Work, home, commute?
Affects content density, attention span assumptions, and accessibility needs.
What are they trying to accomplish?
Ensures design supports the user's task, not just the client's preferences.
Do any users have visual, motor, or cognitive considerations?
May require colour contrast compliance, large text, keyboard navigation, or simple language.

blocks.text: Creating a User Persona

A user persona is a one-page profile of a fictional but representative user. It keeps design decisions grounded in real human needs. Industry teams refer to personas throughout the entire project: 'Would Sarah find this confusing?' is a more productive design question than 'Does this look good?'

blocks.heading: Career Path: UX Designer / Project Coordinator
blocks.text: User personas are a foundational tool in UX design. Senior UX Designers at companies like Spotify, Canva, and the ABC conduct interviews and surveys to build research-backed personas. In your AIT project, your persona can be based on your own experience with the target audience — e.g., if designing for Year 9 students, think about what you knew and needed at that age.
Choosing the Right Presentation Medium

The presentation medium is the format in which the final product will be delivered and consumed. It is not just 'a website' or 'a video' — it includes the platform, the device, the context, and sometimes the software environment. The medium must match the audience's context and the product's purpose.

rows: Any audience with internet access; scalable; accessible on multiple devices
Promotional material, event awareness, community engagement
Audiences already active on the platform; short attention span; mobile-first
Office/academic audiences; needs to be readable offline or printable
Audience being presented to live or watching a recorded walkthrough
Audiences who prefer visual/audio content; commuters; social media users
Audiences who may not be online; physical location contexts
Tech-confident users; gamified or education contexts

blocks.heading: Project Manager Tip
blocks.text: When justifying your choice of medium in project documentation, don't just name it — explain why it suits this specific audience. Example: 'I chose Instagram Stories because the target audience of 18–25 year olds are active Instagram users who access content on mobile; Stories format is optimised for mobile screens and has high engagement rates with this demographic.'

blocks.title: Audience and Medium Match
blocks.scenario: Three clients each need a digital product. Client A: A retirement village wants to help residents aged 65–80 find the weekly activities schedule. Client B: A surf school wants to promote their summer holiday camps to parents of children aged 10–14. Client C: A tech startup wants to onboard new employees with a company policy guide that staff can reference on the job.

questions.question: For each client, identify the most appropriate presentation medium and justify your choice by referencing specific target audience characteristics.
For Client A, describe THREE specific design decisions (not just medium choice) that would make the product accessible and usable for the described audience.
Create a brief user persona for one of the three clients. Include: name, age, digital habits, context of use, primary goal, and one frustration with technology.

blocks.title: Write a Target Audience Analysis
blocks.intro: Write a one-page target audience analysis for your current project (or the music festival brief). This document will be part of your project folio.
steps: Write a paragraph describing the target audience using at least four characteristics from the table above (age, digital literacy, device, context, goals, accessibility).

Create a user persona using this structure: Name, Age, Occupation/Year level, Devices they use, Digital confidence (1–5), How they will access your product, Their primary goal, One frustration or barrier.
Write one paragraph justifying your choice of presentation medium, explicitly connecting it to the audience characteristics you identified.
List three design decisions you will make specifically because of this audience (e.g., 'I will use 18pt minimum body font because many users are over 65').
Peer-check: swap your analysis with a classmate. Can they identify who the audience is from your description alone? Revise based on their feedback.

question.text: A student is designing a resource for Year 7 students to learn about online safety. Which medium would LEAST suit this audience and context?
options: An animated explainer video on YouTube
A printed A3 poster for the classroom wall
A 40-page technical PDF report
An interactive quiz website
question.explanation: A 40-page technical PDF report is poorly suited for Year 7 students — the format, length, and likely language complexity do not match the age, attention span, or reading level of the target audience. Animated video, posters, and interactive quizzes are all more appropriate for this age group.

question.text: Define 'context of use' and explain how it should influence design decisions for a digital product. Give a specific example.

blocks.text: Evaluation and Iteration

In professional IT, a product is never truly 'finished' after one pass. Evaluation and iteration — the cycle of testing, gathering feedback, and improving — are what separate professional-quality work from a first attempt. The SCSA AIT assessment requires you to demonstrate this cycle explicitly.

blocks.heading: Industry Standard
blocks.text: Software teams at companies like Atlassian, Canva, and Atlassian run 'sprint reviews' at the end of every two-week development cycle — presenting work, gathering feedback, and adjusting the next sprint accordingly. This is the Agile methodology, and it is built on the principle that iteration produces better outcomes than trying to get everything right the first time.
What Are Evaluation Criteria?

Evaluation criteria are specific, measurable statements that define what a successful product looks like. They are written during the planning phase — before you build anything — so that evaluation at the end is objective, not subjective.

items.definition: Specific, measurable standards against which a completed digital product or solution is assessed. Defined at the planning stage.
Criteria related to what the product does — whether required features work correctly.
Criteria related to visual design — whether design principles are applied effectively and consistently.
Criteria related to user experience — whether the product is easy and intuitive to use for the target audience.
The process of making targeted improvements to a product based on evaluation findings, then re-evaluating.

blocks.text: Writing Strong Evaluation Criteria
Weak criteria are vague and unmeasurable. Strong criteria are specific, testable, and tied to the brief. Compare the examples below:

headers: Why the strong version is better
rows: The website uses a consistent colour palette of no more than three colours that align with the festival brand guide.
Specific, testable, and tied to a design principle (repetition/consistency).
The video is easy to understand.
A person with no prior knowledge of the festival can identify the event name, date, and ticket URL after watching the video once.
Can be tested with a real user; gives a clear pass/fail outcome.
All body text on the poster meets WCAG AA contrast ratio requirements (minimum 4.5:1 for normal text).
References an industry standard; can be verified with a contrast-checking tool.
The budget spreadsheet automatically calculates totals and highlights cells that exceed the budget in red.
Specific functionality; testable with sample data.

blocks.text: Methods of Evaluation

rows: Assess each criterion as met / partially met / not met, with evidence.
Structured, objective overview of how well the product meets the brief.
Present the product to the client; gather feedback using specific questions.
Whether the product meets the client's intent — often reveals mismatches between your interpretation and their vision.
A classmate evaluates the product against the criteria — they do not know your design decisions.
Fresh perspective; identifies blind spots and usability issues you cannot see in your own work.
A member of the target audience uses the product while you observe — without guidance from you.
Reveals real usability issues; where users get confused, what they miss, what they find intuitive.
Honest personal assessment of the process as well as the product.
Demonstrates critical thinking; identifies what you would do differently next time.

blocks.text: The Iteration Cycle

Iteration is not about fixing everything — it is about making targeted improvements based on evidence, then re-evaluating. A single iteration cycle: evaluate → identify the most critical issues → make specific changes → re-evaluate against the same criteria.

items: Evaluate the product against your criteria — document the results clearly.
Prioritise: which issues most significantly affect the product's ability to meet the brief?
Make targeted improvements — document what you changed and why.
Re-evaluate the specific criteria that were not met — did the change work?
Document the iteration: before/after screenshots, change log, and reflection on the outcome.

blocks.heading: Project Manager Tip
blocks.text: In your evaluation write-up, always provide evidence. Screenshots, contrast ratio checker results, user testing notes, and client email responses are all valid evidence. 'I think it meets the criterion' is an opinion. 'The user completed the task in 30 seconds without any help' is evidence.
Portfolio Presentation

In SCSA AIT, your project folio is the complete record of your project management process. It demonstrates that you did not just build a product — you planned, represented, evaluated, and iterated. A strong folio tells a story from brief to final product.

rows: Your restatement of the project requirements in your own words, including target audience and constraints.
Detailed description of your audience with design implications.
Thumbnail sketches, annotated wireframes or storyboards, with notes explaining decisions.
Your planning timeline with milestones and resource allocation.
Your pre-defined criteria — written before you built the product.
Criteria checklist with evidence; peer review feedback; client feedback.
Documentation of changes made based on evaluation, with before/after evidence.
Honest assessment of the process, what you learned, and what you would do differently.

blocks.title: Evaluating a Peer's Product
blocks.scenario: Your classmate has created a website homepage for a local bakery. The original brief required: (1) the bakery's name and opening hours visible without scrolling, (2) at least one high-quality food photo, (3) a contact form, (4) colour palette matching the bakery's existing brand (brown, cream, and terracotta), (5) usable on mobile. You are conducting a peer review.

questions.question: Write a peer review using a criteria checklist format. For each criterion, state whether it is met, partially met, or not met, and provide a specific observation as evidence for your judgement.
Based on your review, identify the TWO most critical issues to fix first and explain why these should be prioritised over other issues.
Write a constructive peer feedback comment (3–4 sentences) that would help the designer improve their product without being vague or discouraging.

blocks.title: Evaluate and Iterate Your Design Representation
blocks.intro: Using the wireframe or storyboard you created in Lesson 3, conduct a structured evaluation and complete one iteration cycle.
steps: Write your evaluation criteria: create a checklist of 5–6 specific criteria your design representation must meet (e.g., 'All page sections are labelled', 'The ticket call-to-action is visible above the fold', 'Annotations explain at least 4 design decisions').
Self-evaluate: go through each criterion and rate it met / partially met / not met. Write one sentence of evidence for each rating.
Peer review: swap your wireframe or storyboard with a partner. They complete the same criteria checklist independently. Compare your results — where do you disagree?
Identify the two most important improvements based on the combined self and peer review.
Make the improvements to your wireframe or storyboard. Document each change: what you changed, why you changed it, and which criterion it addresses.
Photograph or export your revised design representation and annotate it to show what changed.
Write a 150-word reflection: What did the evaluation process reveal that you had not noticed yourself? What would you do differently in the planning phase next time?

question.text: Which evaluation criterion is written most effectively?
options: The app looks professional and modern.
Users find the app easy to navigate.
A Year 10 student can find the homework submission page within 30 seconds without any instruction.
The app has good design principles applied.
question.explanation: Option C is specific (Year 10 student), measurable (30 seconds), and testable (without instruction). The other options are vague, subjective, or cannot be objectively verified — they describe impressions rather than measurable outcomes.

question.text: Why should evaluation criteria be written BEFORE the product is built, not after?
options: Because criteria are not needed once the product is finished.
To ensure the evaluation is objective and not influenced by what was actually built.
Because clients will not accept criteria written after the deadline.
To save time at the end of the project.
question.explanation: Writing criteria after the product is built introduces bias — you unconsciously write criteria that match what you made, rather than what was originally required. Pre-defined criteria ensure the evaluation measures the product against the brief, not against itself.

question.text: A student evaluates their project poster and writes: 'I think it looks good and I am happy with it.' Explain why this is an inadequate evaluation and describe what a professional evaluation response would include instead.

blocks.heading: Project Manager Tip — Final Portfolio Checklist
blocks.text: Before submitting your project folio, check: (1) Does your folio include evidence of ALL phases — plan, represent, create, evaluate, iterate? (2) Have you written an interpreted brief that shows you understood the client's needs? (3) Do your design representations include annotations, not just drawings? (4) Are your evaluation criteria specific and measurable? (5) Do you have evidence (screenshots, feedback, test results) for every criterion judgement? (6) Does your reflection show genuine critical thinking — not just what went well, but what you would do differently?
Why Compress Files?

Every file you create — a photo, a video, a Word document — takes up storage space and takes time to transfer across a network. File compression reduces file size, making files faster to upload, download, and share, while consuming less storage. In a professional environment, unoptimised files can slow down websites, fill up servers quickly, and cost businesses money in cloud storage and bandwidth fees.

items.definition: The process of encoding data using fewer bits than the original representation, reducing file size.
Compression that permanently removes some data to achieve smaller file sizes. Quality is reduced and the original cannot be perfectly restored.
Compression that reduces file size without losing any data. The original file can be perfectly reconstructed from the compressed version.
The ratio of the original file size to the compressed file size. A ratio of 4:1 means the file is one quarter of its original size.

blocks.text: Lossy vs Lossless Compression
The two main types of compression each suit different use cases. Understanding which to apply — and when — is a core professional skill in IT, design, and media roles.

items.label: Lossy Compression
points: Permanently discards data that the algorithm deems less noticeable
Achieves much smaller file sizes than lossless
Each time you save, quality degrades further (generational loss)
Best for images displayed on screen or audio for casual listening
Examples: JPEG (images), MP3 (audio), MP4/H.264 (video), AAC (audio)

items.label: Lossless Compression
points: Reduces file size by finding and encoding repeated patterns
Original data is perfectly restored when decompressed
No quality loss — what you compress is what you get back
Best for documents, software, archiving, and professional editing workflows
Examples: PNG (images), FLAC (audio), ZIP (any file type), PDF/A (documents)

blocks.heading: Generational Loss
blocks.text: If you open a JPEG, edit it, and save it again as JPEG, compression is applied a second time — degrading quality further. Always keep master copies in a lossless format (e.g. PSD, TIFF, or PNG) and export lossy versions only for delivery.
Real-World File Size Comparison
The numbers below show how dramatically compression affects file size. All examples start from the same source content captured at high quality.

rows: All data preserved; larger than JPEG
Good balance for web and print
Depends heavily on file types inside

blocks.text: Calculating File Size Savings

args.name: Original
args.description: The file size before compression (use consistent units — both in KB, or both in MB)

args.name: Compressed
args.description: The file size after compression in the same units

args.name: Result
args.description: The percentage of the original size that was saved. Example: a 24 MB RAW file compressed to 3 MB JPEG saves ((24 − 3) ÷ 24) × 100 = 87.5%

blocks.text: Common Compression Formats at a Glance

rows: Web display, email attachments, social media
Modern web images — smaller than JPEG/PNG with similar quality
Apple ecosystem streaming, iTunes, YouTube audio
Studio recording, video production audio tracks
High-resolution video (4K) with smaller files than H.264
Archiving and transferring collections of files

blocks.title: Compare File Sizes Hands-On
blocks.software: Any image editor (Photoshop, GIMP, Paint.NET, or Canva export settings)
blocks.intro: In this activity you will export the same image in multiple formats and measure the size difference to see compression in action.
steps: Find or take a high-resolution photograph (at least 2 MB). Download a royalty-free image from Unsplash.com if needed.
Open the image in your image editor. Note the original file size.
Export/Save As the image as PNG (lossless). Record the file size.
Export the same image as JPEG at quality 90%. Record the file size.
Export the same image as JPEG at quality 40%. Record the file size.
Create a table in a Word document with columns: Format, Quality Setting, File Size (KB), Size vs Original (%), Visual Quality Rating (1–5).
Fill in your table with your measurements. Which format gave the best balance of file size and quality?
Write a one-paragraph recommendation: for a client who needs to display this photo on their website, which format and quality setting would you recommend, and why?

blocks.heading: Data Analyst Career
blocks.text: Data analysts regularly work with large datasets stored in compressed formats like Parquet and CSV.GZ. Understanding compression helps you choose the right format for speed vs storage, a common consideration when working with cloud platforms like AWS S3 or Google BigQuery.
Check Your Understanding

question.text: A graphic designer saves a logo as a JPEG to reduce file size. After sending it to a client, they discover some pixels along the edges of text look blurry. What is the most likely cause?
options: The screen resolution is too low to display the image
JPEG lossy compression introduced compression artefacts
The file was corrupted during email transfer
JPEG does not support text-based images
question.explanation: JPEG uses lossy compression, which introduces artefacts — especially on sharp edges like text and logos. PNG (lossless) is always the correct choice for logos and graphics with sharp edges or transparency.

question.text: A music producer wants to archive a completed recording so it can be retrieved at exactly the same quality in the future. Which format should they choose?
options: MP3 at 320 kbps
AAC at 256 kbps
FLAC
OGG Vorbis
question.explanation: FLAC (Free Lossless Audio Codec) is lossless — the compressed file can be perfectly decompressed to recreate the original audio. MP3, AAC, and OGG are all lossy and permanently discard audio data.

question.text: An original photo is 18 MB. After JPEG compression it is 2.7 MB. What percentage of the file size was saved?
question.explanation: Saving % = ((18 − 2.7) ÷ 18) × 100 = (15.3 ÷ 18) × 100 = 85%. The compressed file is 85% smaller than the original.

question.text: Explain the difference between lossy and lossless compression. In your answer, give one example of each and describe a scenario where each type is the appropriate choice.
question.explanation: Lossy compression permanently removes data to achieve smaller file sizes (e.g. JPEG for photos, MP3 for music). It is appropriate when some quality loss is acceptable — such as displaying images on a website. Lossless compression reduces file size without any data loss, allowing perfect reconstruction (e.g. PNG for graphics, FLAC for audio). It is appropriate when quality must be fully preserved — such as archiving master files or storing logos.

question.text: Saving a JPEG file multiple times causes quality to degrade each time because lossy compression is applied repeatedly.
question.explanation: True. This is called generational loss. Each save cycle applies lossy compression again, discarding more data. Always maintain a lossless master copy and only export to JPEG for final delivery.

blocks.text: One File, Many Destinations

A single piece of content — a photograph, a logo, a video — will often need to be delivered across multiple platforms: a printed brochure, a website, a social media post. Each platform has different technical requirements. Exporting without understanding these requirements leads to images that look blurry in print, slow websites, or pixelated graphics on high-resolution screens.

items.definition: The number of pixels in an image, typically expressed as width × height (e.g. 1920 × 1080). Higher resolution means more detail.
A measure of print resolution — how many ink dots are printed per inch. Higher DPI = sharper printed output.
A measure of screen or digital image resolution — how many pixels appear per inch. Used for digital displays and image editing.
Changing the number of pixels in an image — either upsampling (adding pixels, reducing quality) or downsampling (removing pixels, reducing file size).
Removing unwanted areas from the edges of an image to change its composition or aspect ratio, reducing file size.
The colour system an image uses. RGB is used for screens; CMYK is used for professional printing.

blocks.text: Platform Requirements at a Glance

blocks.heading: Always Start Large
blocks.text: It is always better to start with a high-resolution original and scale down for digital use. You cannot add detail that was never there — scaling up a small image creates pixelation and blur. Maintain a high-resolution master and export appropriately sized copies for each destination.
File Size Minimisation Techniques
Beyond choosing a compressed format, two specific techniques are used to further reduce file size before exporting.
Cropping

Cropping removes pixels from the edges of an image. This reduces both the pixel dimensions and the file size, and improves composition by removing distracting elements. For web use, crop images to the exact dimensions needed — there is no benefit in serving a 4000-pixel-wide image if it will only ever be displayed at 800 pixels wide.
Resampling (Downsampling)

Resampling adjusts the number of pixels in an image. Downsampling reduces pixel count — useful when converting a 300 DPI print image for web use. Most image editors offer resampling algorithms (Bicubic, Lanczos) that reduce the image while preserving as much sharpness as possible.

blocks.title: Pixel Dimension from Physical Size and DPI

args.name: Physical Size
args.description: The intended print size in inches (e.g. an A4 page is approximately 8.27 × 11.69 inches)

args.name: DPI
args.description: The target print resolution (300 DPI for professional print, 72 PPI for web)

args.name: Result
args.description: Example: A4 at 300 DPI = 8.27 × 300 = 2481 px wide. The same A4 at 72 PPI for web preview = 8.27 × 72 = 595 px wide — a much smaller file.

blocks.text: Exporting for Purpose — A Practical Workflow

items: Start with the highest resolution original available (camera RAW, layered PSD, or vector file).
Determine each destination (print, website, social media) and its specific requirements (DPI, pixel dimensions, colour mode, format).
Crop to the appropriate aspect ratio for each destination.
Resample (downsample) to the required pixel dimensions.
Convert colour mode if necessary (RGB for screens, CMYK for professional print).
Export in the appropriate format with appropriate compression settings.
Name the exported file clearly to reflect its destination (e.g. logo_web_72ppi.png, logo_print_300dpi.pdf).
Test — view the web image in a browser; send the print file to a proof if possible.

blocks.title: Optimise One Image for Three Platforms
blocks.software: Photoshop, GIMP, or Canva (with download settings)
blocks.intro: You will take a single source image and correctly optimise and export it for three different purposes: professional print, website banner, and Instagram post.
steps: Download a high-resolution royalty-free photograph from Unsplash.com (choose a landscape or cityscape — at least 3000 px wide).
Note the original pixel dimensions and file size.
Export 1 — Print: Resample to 2480 × 1654 px (A4 landscape at 300 DPI). Save as JPEG at 95% quality. Record the file size.
Export 2 — Website Banner: Resample to 1920 × 600 px (16:5 crop). Save as JPEG at 80% quality. Record the file size.
Export 3 — Instagram Post: Crop and resample to 1080 × 1080 px. Save as JPEG at 85% quality. Record the file size.
Create a summary table: Destination | Dimensions | DPI/PPI | Format | File Size | Compression Quality.
Write a short paragraph explaining why each export used different settings.

blocks.heading: Industry Standard: Cloud-First Approach
blocks.text: Professional design agencies use digital asset management (DAM) systems to store master files in the cloud and automatically generate optimised versions for every platform. Tools like Adobe Experience Manager, Bynder, and Cloudinary apply rules-based optimisation — you upload once and the platform handles all exports. Understanding manual optimisation gives you the knowledge to configure these systems correctly.
Check Your Understanding

question.text: A designer creates a poster that needs to be printed professionally. What DPI should the image be?
options: 72 DPI
96 DPI
150 DPI
300 DPI
question.explanation: Professional print requires 300 DPI to produce sharp, detailed output. 72 DPI is the standard for digital/screen display and would result in a blurry, pixelated print.

question.text: Which colour mode should be used when preparing an image for display on a website?
options: CMYK
LAB
RGB
Grayscale
question.explanation: RGB (Red, Green, Blue) is the colour mode used by all digital screens. CMYK is used for professional printing. If you send a CMYK image to a website, colours may appear differently than intended.

question.text: A student takes a photo at 4032 × 3024 pixels (12 MP) on their phone and wants to use it as a website banner (display size: 1200 × 400 px). Describe two specific techniques they should apply to optimise the file for this purpose, and explain the benefit of each.
question.explanation: 1. Cropping — crop the image to a 3:1 aspect ratio to match the banner shape, removing unnecessary pixels and improving composition. This reduces file size and ensures the image fills the banner correctly without distortion. 2. Resampling (downsampling) — reduce pixel dimensions from 4032 × 3024 to 1200 × 400 (or up to 2× for retina displays). This dramatically reduces file size, meaning faster page load times for website visitors.

question.text: Upsampling an image (increasing its pixel dimensions beyond the original) improves its quality when printed.
question.explanation: False. Upsampling adds pixels by interpolation — the software guesses at values for new pixels. This does not add real detail and results in blurry or soft output. Always use the highest-resolution original source.

blocks.text: The Problem of Uncontrolled Files

Without a system for managing document versions, projects quickly become chaotic. Professionals working on documents, code, or design files often encounter situations where multiple people edit the same file, earlier versions are accidentally overwritten, and no one can determine which file is the most recent or correct. Document version control solves these problems.

blocks.heading: Real-World Problem
blocks.text: A marketing team is working on an annual report. Without version control, the folder ends up with files named: report_final.docx, report_FINAL2.docx, report_FINAL_v3_Johns edits.docx, report_USE THIS ONE.docx. Nobody is certain which contains the approved content. Deadlines are missed. This is a real scenario in many workplaces — and entirely avoidable.

items.definition: A system for tracking and managing changes to documents or files over time, allowing you to view, compare, or restore earlier versions.
A record of all changes made to a document, including who made them and when.
A storage location for files and their complete history of changes, used in version control systems.
A saved snapshot of changes in a version control system, with a description of what was changed and why.
Reverting a file or project to a previous version — useful when a change introduces errors.

blocks.text: File Naming Conventions
A consistent file naming convention is the simplest form of version control. It allows team members to identify the purpose and version of any file at a glance, without opening it.

rows: Identifies which project the file belongs to

blocks.text: A well-named file might look like: AnnualReport2025_Budget_v3_20251015_CG_review.xlsx. Anyone on the team can immediately understand what this file is, when it was saved, who saved it, and what stage it is at.

blocks.heading: Date Format Tip
blocks.text: Always use YYYYMMDD (year first, then month, then day). Files will then sort correctly in alphabetical order — oldest first. Using DDMMYYYY causes files from different months to sort incorrectly.
Folder Structure for Version Control
A logical folder structure complements naming conventions. A common approach in professional environments uses separate folders for working files, reviews, and final approved assets.

items: 01_briefs — client briefs and project requirements
02_working — active drafts and working files (versioned by filename)
03_review — files sent to client or team for review
04_approved — client-approved versions only
05_assets — source images, fonts, brand assets
06_delivery — final exported/delivered files

blocks.text: Built-In Version History — Office and Cloud Tools
Many modern applications automatically track document history without you needing to create multiple copies. These built-in tools are your first line of version control.

items: Microsoft Word / OneDrive — Version History panel shows every saved version. Right-click a file in OneDrive to see and restore previous versions.
Google Docs — File > Version History > See Version History shows all changes with timestamps and authors. Named versions can be created at key milestones.
SharePoint (Microsoft) — Enterprise document management with check-in/check-out to prevent two people editing simultaneously.
Adobe Creative Cloud — Version History is available for cloud documents in Photoshop, Illustrator, and InDesign.

blocks.text: Git — The Industry-Standard Version Control System

Git is the world's most widely used version control system, and it is the industry standard for software development. While it was designed for code, the concepts apply to any file-based project. Understanding Git at a conceptual level is important for anyone entering an IT role.

headers: What It Means in Plain English
rows: A folder where Git tracks all changes to every file
A saved checkpoint — like a named, timestamped version
A parallel version of the project — test changes without affecting the main version
Bring changes from one branch back into the main version
Download changes from a remote server (pull) or upload your changes (push)
Cloud hosting platforms for Git repositories — widely used in industry for team collaboration

blocks.heading: Data Analyst Career
blocks.text: Data analysts and IT professionals are expected to know Git. Version-controlling scripts, SQL queries, and data pipelines prevents accidental overwrites and allows teams to collaborate without conflicts. Most IT job advertisements mention Git as a required skill. Even if you don't write code, understanding Git gives you a significant advantage.
Business Document Management Systems

Larger organisations use formal Document Management Systems (DMS) to control how documents are created, reviewed, approved, stored, and archived. These systems go beyond file naming and version history to enforce business workflows.

items: Microsoft SharePoint — most common in Australian businesses; integrates with Microsoft 365
Google Workspace (Google Drive + Shared Drives) — widely used by SMEs and schools
Confluence (Atlassian) — used alongside Jira for software and IT project documentation
Notion — increasingly used by startups and creative agencies for collaborative documentation
DocuWare, M-Files, OpenText — enterprise-grade DMS used in regulated industries (healthcare, legal, government)

blocks.title: Set Up a Professional Folder Structure
blocks.intro: In this activity you will design and implement a professional folder structure and file naming convention for a mock project.
steps: Create a new folder called ClientProject_SchoolFete2025 on your desktop or OneDrive.
Inside it, create subfolders following the professional structure: 01_Briefs, 02_Working, 03_Review, 04_Approved, 05_Assets, 06_Delivery.
Create three Word documents and save them in 02_Working using a proper naming convention (e.g. SchoolFete2025_EventProgram_v1_20250901_draft.docx).
Make a small change to one document and save it as v2 (a new file — don't overwrite v1).
Move the v2 file to 03_Review to simulate sending it for approval.
Take a screenshot of your completed folder structure.
Write a short explanation (3–4 sentences) of why this folder structure and naming convention is more professional than saving everything to the desktop with names like 'program2.docx'.

blocks.text: Check Your Understanding

question.text: A team member opens a shared Word document and makes major changes, overwriting the previous version. Which version control approach would have best prevented this problem?
options: Saving the file with a new version number before making changes
Using a different font to indicate new edits
Emailing the document to a colleague for safekeeping
Saving the document as a PDF
question.explanation: Creating a new versioned copy (e.g. v2) before editing preserves the previous version. Alternatively, a system with check-in/check-out (like SharePoint) or automatic version history (like Google Docs or OneDrive) would also prevent this problem.

question.text: Which date format should be used in file names to ensure files sort correctly in chronological order?
options: DDMMYYYY (e.g. 15102025)
MMDDYYYY (e.g. 10152025)
YYYYMMDD (e.g. 20251015)
question.explanation: YYYYMMDD (year-month-day) is the ISO 8601 standard and ensures files sort alphabetically in the correct chronological order. Other date formats cause sorting errors — for example, files from October and November appear mixed with files from other months.

question.text: Explain what a 'commit' is in Git and why adding a descriptive message to each commit is considered best practice.
question.explanation: A commit is a saved snapshot of all changes made to files in a repository at a specific point in time. It records what changed, when, and who made the change. Adding a descriptive commit message (e.g. 'Updated homepage banner image and compressed for web') is best practice because it allows team members (and your future self) to understand what changed without having to open and compare the files. This makes it much faster to locate a specific version or understand the history of a project.

question.text: A small business has five employees who all share files through a single network drive. They have no version control system — they just save files and overwrite each other's work. Propose a practical document management solution for this business. Your answer should describe the system you recommend, how files would be named and organised, and what version control features it provides. (6 marks)
question.explanation: A strong response would: recommend a cloud-based solution (e.g. Microsoft OneDrive for Business with SharePoint, or Google Shared Drive) and explain why it suits a small team; describe a folder structure (e.g. by client or project with subfolders for working/review/approved); propose a file naming convention using project name, version number, date, and status; explain automatic version history features (e.g. OneDrive retains versions for 30+ days); mention the ability to restore previous versions if a file is accidentally overwritten; and potentially mention access permissions to control who can edit vs view files.

blocks.text: What is Cloud Computing?

Cloud computing is the delivery of computing services — including servers, storage, databases, networking, software, analytics, and intelligence — over the internet ("the cloud"). Instead of running software on your own computer or maintaining your own physical servers, you access these resources on-demand from a provider's data centres around the world.

The term 'the cloud' simply refers to servers, storage, and software that are accessed over the internet rather than stored locally on your device. You have already used cloud computing — every time you access Gmail, stream on YouTube, use Google Docs, or save a file to OneDrive, you are using the cloud.

items.definition: The delivery of computing services (servers, storage, software, databases) over the internet, accessed on demand.
A large facility housing thousands of servers and networking equipment. Cloud providers operate many data centres globally.
The ability to increase or decrease computing resources quickly to match demand, without purchasing physical hardware.
Storing data in multiple locations so that if one server or data centre fails, data remains available from another.
Software delivered over the internet and accessed via a browser, without local installation (e.g. Google Workspace, Microsoft 365, Canva).
Cloud-based computing infrastructure — virtual servers, storage, and networking — rented from a provider (e.g. AWS EC2, Azure Virtual Machines).

blocks.text: Advantages of Cloud Computing

items: Accessibility — access your files and applications from any device with an internet connection, anywhere in the world
Cost efficiency — no upfront hardware costs; pay only for what you use (pay-as-you-go pricing)
Automatic backups — cloud providers automatically replicate data across multiple locations, protecting against data loss
Scalability — instantly scale storage or computing power up or down as your needs change
Collaboration — multiple users can access and edit the same files simultaneously in real time
Automatic updates — software is maintained and updated by the provider, reducing IT maintenance burden
Disaster recovery — data stored in the cloud survives local disasters (fire, theft, hardware failure)

blocks.text: Risks and Disadvantages

items: Internet dependency — no internet connection means no access to cloud services or files
Security and privacy concerns — sensitive data is stored on third-party servers; a data breach at the provider could expose your data
Vendor lock-in — migrating large amounts of data from one provider to another can be costly and complex
Ongoing costs — subscription fees accumulate over time; costs can exceed owning local hardware for some use cases
Data sovereignty — data stored overseas may be subject to foreign laws (e.g. US data stored on AWS servers is subject to US law)
Outages — when a cloud provider experiences downtime, all services and data may be temporarily inaccessible

blocks.heading: Data Sovereignty in Australia
blocks.text: Australian businesses handling sensitive data (health records, financial data, government information) must consider where cloud data is physically stored. The Australian Privacy Act and sector-specific regulations may require data to be stored on Australian soil. Major providers offer Australian data centre regions (AWS Sydney, Azure Australia East) to address this.
Major Cloud Providers

rows: Market leader — largest global cloud provider (~32% market share)
Azure VMs, Azure SQL, Microsoft 365, Teams
Second largest — dominant in enterprise and Australian government
Google Compute Engine, BigQuery, Google Kubernetes Engine
Third largest — strong in data analytics and machine learning
Most widely used in Australian schools and businesses
Widely used in education and SMEs

blocks.heading: Industry Standard: Cloud-First Approach
blocks.text: Most organisations today operate a 'cloud-first' strategy — when evaluating new software or infrastructure, the cloud option is considered before any on-premises (local server) alternative. According to IBRS research, over 80% of Australian enterprises now run significant workloads in the cloud. Entry-level IT roles are expected to understand at least one cloud platform.
Business Applications of Cloud Computing

items.label: File Storage and Sharing
items.description: Store all business documents in OneDrive or Google Drive — accessible from any device, anywhere, with automatic version history and backup.

items.label: Communication and Collaboration
items.description: Microsoft Teams, Slack, and Google Meet are cloud-based platforms enabling video meetings, messaging, and real-time document collaboration.

items.label: Business Software (SaaS)
items.description: Accounting (Xero), CRM (Salesforce), HR (Employment Hero), and project management (Monday.com, Asana) are all delivered as cloud SaaS products.

items.label: Website and App Hosting
items.description: Websites and web applications run on cloud servers (AWS, Azure, GCP), enabling global availability and automatic scaling during traffic spikes.

items.label: Data Backup and Recovery
items.description: Automated cloud backups protect against ransomware, hardware failure, and natural disaster. Recovery is faster than restoring from local tape or disk.

items.label: Data Analytics
items.description: Cloud platforms provide powerful analytics tools (BigQuery, Azure Synapse, AWS Redshift) that process huge datasets without expensive local hardware.

blocks.title: Cloud Provider Research and Comparison
blocks.software: Web browser, Word or Google Docs
blocks.intro: Research the three major cloud providers (AWS, Azure, Google Cloud) and compare them for a specific business scenario.
steps: Scenario: A Perth-based accounting firm with 20 staff needs to move their file storage, email, and accounting software to the cloud. They handle sensitive client financial data.
Research each provider's Australian data centre locations. Does each provider offer Australian-region storage? (Search: 'AWS Australia regions', 'Azure Australia regions', 'Google Cloud Australia')
Compare storage pricing: How much does 1 TB of cloud storage cost per month from each provider? (Research OneDrive for Business, Google Workspace Business Starter, AWS S3 Standard)
Investigate their security certifications — look for ISO 27001, SOC 2, and Australian Government IRAP certifications.
Create a comparison table: Provider | Australian Data Centre | Price per TB/month | Key Security Certifications | Best Suited For.
Write a recommendation (one paragraph): which cloud provider would you recommend for this accounting firm and why? Consider data sovereignty, cost, and security.

blocks.text: Check Your Understanding

question.text: Which of the following best describes cloud computing?
options: Storing files on a USB drive that is kept in a physically secure location
Accessing computing services and storage over the internet from remote data centres
Using a powerful desktop computer with maximum RAM and storage
Backing up files to an external hard drive weekly
question.explanation: Cloud computing refers to delivering computing resources (storage, servers, software) over the internet from remote data centres, accessed on demand. Local storage devices like USBs and hard drives are on-premises solutions, not cloud.

question.text: Which of the following is a significant risk of relying entirely on cloud storage for business data?
options: Files can be accessed from too many devices
Cloud storage is always more expensive than local storage
If internet connectivity is lost, access to files and services is unavailable
Cloud providers do not offer any security measures
question.explanation: Internet dependency is a key risk of cloud computing. Without an internet connection, cloud-stored files and cloud-based applications cannot be accessed. Cloud providers generally do offer strong security, and pricing can be competitive with local storage at scale.

question.text: A startup needs email, video conferencing, and document editing tools for its team of 10. They want minimal IT maintenance overhead and pay-as-you-go pricing. Which cloud service model is most appropriate?
options: IaaS — they should build their own servers in the cloud
SaaS — they should use ready-made cloud applications like Google Workspace or Microsoft 365
They should purchase physical servers and install their own software
PaaS — they should build a custom platform to host their applications
question.explanation: SaaS (Software as a Service) delivers ready-to-use applications over the internet. Google Workspace and Microsoft 365 provide email, video conferencing, and document tools with no IT infrastructure to manage — perfect for a small team wanting minimal overhead.

question.text: An Australian medical clinic is considering moving patient records to a cloud service hosted in the United States. Identify two concerns they should consider and explain why each is significant.
question.explanation: 1. Data sovereignty — data stored in the US is subject to US laws, including the CLOUD Act, which may allow US authorities to access data without notifying Australian parties. Australian health data may be required to stay within Australia under the Privacy Act and Australian Digital Health Agency guidelines. 2. Security and privacy — medical records contain highly sensitive personal health information (PHI). If the cloud provider suffers a data breach, patient privacy is compromised. The clinic should verify the provider's security certifications (ISO 27001, SOC 2) and whether they offer Australian-region data storage.

question.text: Cloud computing allows businesses to scale their computing resources up or down without purchasing additional physical hardware.
question.explanation: True. Scalability is one of the primary advantages of cloud computing. A business can increase storage, computing power, or software licences instantly through the provider's portal, and scale back down when demand decreases — only paying for what is used.

blocks.text: Why System Maintenance Matters

Computers accumulate unnecessary files, fragmented data, and security threats over time. Without regular maintenance, systems slow down, storage fills up, and security vulnerabilities go unaddressed. In a business environment, a slow or compromised system costs money — downtime, reduced productivity, and potential data breaches all have real financial impacts.

System utility tools are software tools built into operating systems or provided as third-party applications to help maintain, optimise, and secure a computer system. Understanding these tools — and knowing when and why to use them — is a core IT skill.
Disk Clean-Up and Temporary Files

items.definition: Files created by the operating system or applications during normal operation. They are intended to be short-lived but often accumulate and consume significant storage.
Copies of web pages, images, and scripts stored locally by a browser to speed up repeat visits. Over time, the cache can consume gigabytes of storage.
A Windows utility that identifies and safely removes unnecessary files including temporary files, Recycle Bin contents, and cached Windows updates.

blocks.text: Common files that accumulate and can be safely removed include: Windows Update installation files (often several GB), browser cache and cookies, Recycle Bin contents, thumbnail cache, system error logs, and application temporary files (found in C:\Users\[Username]\AppData\Local\Temp).

blocks.heading: How Much Space Can You Recover?
blocks.text: On a computer that has never been cleaned, Disk Clean-Up on Windows can often recover 5–15 GB or more — including Windows Update files that are kept after installation 'just in case' a rollback is needed. Running Disk Clean-Up as Administrator reveals even more options including system files.
Disk Fragmentation

blocks.definition: A condition where files are broken into pieces (fragments) stored in different, non-contiguous locations on a hard disk drive (HDD). The read head must physically move between fragments, slowing performance.

blocks.text: When files are saved and deleted repeatedly, the disk gradually becomes fragmented — file pieces are scattered across the physical disk surface. Defragmentation (or 'defragging') reorganises these fragments, placing file pieces contiguously so the drive can read them faster.

blocks.heading: SSDs Do Not Need Defragmenting
blocks.text: Disk defragmentation applies only to traditional Hard Disk Drives (HDDs), which use a physical read/write head. Solid State Drives (SSDs) have no moving parts — they access data electronically and are equally fast regardless of where data is stored. Running a defragmenter on an SSD is unnecessary and actually shortens its lifespan by causing unnecessary write cycles. Windows 10 and 11 automatically detect drive type and apply the correct maintenance (Defragment for HDD, TRIM for SSD).
Anti-Malware and Antivirus

items.definition: Malicious software designed to damage, disrupt, or gain unauthorised access to a computer system. Includes viruses, ransomware, spyware, trojans, and worms.
Software that detects, quarantines, and removes known viruses and some other malware using a database of known threat signatures.
A broader category that detects a wider range of threats than traditional antivirus, including ransomware, spyware, adware, and zero-day exploits.
A feature that continuously monitors file activity and network traffic, blocking threats as they occur rather than only during scheduled scans.
Updates to the threat database used by antivirus/anti-malware software. Without current definitions, new threats may not be detected.

blocks.text: Modern security software goes far beyond scanning for viruses. Today's threats include: ransomware (encrypts your files and demands payment), spyware (secretly monitors your activity and sends data to attackers), keyloggers (record keystrokes to steal passwords), and rootkits (hide deep in the system to avoid detection). Multi-layered protection is the industry standard — antivirus alone is no longer sufficient.
Spam Filters

blocks.definition: Software that analyses incoming email messages and separates legitimate mail from unsolicited bulk email (spam) and potentially malicious phishing messages.

blocks.text: Email is the most common vector for cyberattacks. Spam filters use a combination of techniques to identify unwanted or dangerous email: rule-based filtering (blocking known spam domains), content analysis (detecting suspicious language patterns), reputation scoring (checking sender IP reputation), and machine learning (adapting to new spam patterns over time).

In business environments, email security gateways (such as Microsoft Defender for Office 365 or Proofpoint) scan all incoming and outgoing email for malware attachments, phishing links, and business email compromise (BEC) attacks.
Spyware

blocks.definition: A type of malware that secretly collects information about a user's activities — including browsing history, login credentials, and personal data — and transmits it to a third party without consent.

blocks.text: Spyware is often bundled with free software downloads or installed via malicious websites. It can be difficult to detect because it is designed to run silently in the background. Signs of spyware infection include: unexplained slow performance, browser homepage changing without permission, new toolbars or extensions appearing, and unusual network activity. Dedicated anti-spyware tools (or anti-malware suites that include anti-spyware) are the primary defence.
System Maintenance — Summary

rows: Remove temp files, cache, old update files
Reorganise fragmented file data for faster access
Weekly full scan; real-time always on
Check junk folder for false positives; update rules
Apply security patches and bug fixes
Free up space and fix browser performance issues

blocks.title: Run a System Maintenance Check
