blocks.intro: In this activity you will perform real system maintenance tasks on a Windows computer and document what you find.
steps: Open Disk Clean-Up: type 'Disk Clean-Up' in the Windows search bar. Select the C: drive. Wait for it to calculate.
Record: how many MB/GB of files are available to clean up? What categories have the most space (e.g. Windows Update files, Recycle Bin, Temporary files)?
Open Windows Security (type 'Windows Security' in search). Navigate to Virus and Threat Protection.
Check: When was the last scan run? Are threat definitions up to date? Take a screenshot.
Navigate to Device Performance and Health. Review the Health Report — are any issues flagged?
Open Settings > System > Storage. Review what is using the most space on your C: drive.
Open your web browser, go to Settings/History and find the option to clear cached data. Note how much space the cache is using.
Write a summary (half a page) of the current health of the system you examined. What maintenance tasks would you recommend be performed and why?

blocks.heading: Data Analyst Career
blocks.text: IT support and systems administration roles require regular maintenance tasks to keep business systems running efficiently. Even in analytical and development roles, understanding how to maintain a clean, secure system is expected. Knowledge of security tools is increasingly important as cyber threats to organisations grow — the Australian Cyber Security Centre (ACSC) reported a cyberattack on Australian businesses every 6 minutes in 2023.
Check Your Understanding

question.text: A computer running Windows 11 with a solid-state drive (SSD) is running slowly. A student suggests running Disk Defragmenter. Is this appropriate?
options: Yes — defragmenting an SSD always improves performance
No — SSDs do not use a physical read head and are not affected by fragmentation; defragmenting is unnecessary and reduces SSD lifespan
Yes — all storage devices benefit from defragmentation regardless of type
No — Windows 11 does not include a defragmenter
question.explanation: SSDs (Solid State Drives) access data electronically with no moving parts, so fragmentation does not affect their performance. Running a defragmenter on an SSD causes unnecessary write cycles, wearing out the drive faster. Windows automatically detects drive type and applies TRIM (for SSDs) instead of defragmentation.

question.text: Which of the following best describes spyware?
options: Software that displays unwanted advertisements on your screen
Malware that encrypts your files and demands a ransom for decryption
Malware that secretly collects user activity and personal data and sends it to a third party
Software that blocks legitimate programs from running
question.explanation: Spyware secretly monitors user activity and transmits data (browsing history, passwords, personal information) to unauthorised third parties. Adware (option A) displays ads; ransomware (option B) encrypts files for ransom.

question.text: Why is it important to keep antivirus definition files regularly updated?
options: Outdated definitions use more memory and slow down the computer
Antivirus software without current definitions cannot detect newly discovered threats
Definition updates add new features to the antivirus interface
Updated definitions reduce the file size of the antivirus program
question.explanation: Antivirus software identifies threats by comparing files against a database of known threat signatures (definitions). New malware is created constantly — without current definitions, the software does not know what the new threats look like and cannot detect or block them.

question.text: A business has not run any system maintenance for 12 months. List three specific maintenance tasks that should be performed immediately and explain what risk each addresses.
question.explanation: Responses should include three of: 1. Run antivirus/anti-malware scan with updated definitions — addresses risk of undetected malware, ransomware, or spyware that may have compromised the system. 2. Run Disk Clean-Up / delete temporary files — addresses risk of storage filling up, which can cause system instability and prevent new files from being saved. 3. Apply operating system updates and security patches — addresses risk of known vulnerabilities being exploited by attackers. 4. Clear browser cache — addresses performance issues and removes potentially sensitive cached data. 5. Defragment HDD (if applicable) — addresses slow file access speeds caused by fragmentation.

question.text: A spam filter only blocks unwanted advertising emails and has no role in cybersecurity.
question.explanation: False. Spam filters play an important cybersecurity role. The majority of phishing attacks, malware delivery, and business email compromise (BEC) scams arrive via email. Spam filters analyse incoming messages for malicious links, suspicious attachments, spoofed sender addresses, and known phishing patterns — blocking many attacks before they reach users.

blocks.text: Project Overview

In this project you will take on the role of a junior digital asset manager at a small creative agency. You have been handed a disorganised collection of digital assets for a client project and asked to clean them up, optimise them for their destinations, apply proper version control, and present a recommendation for how the business should manage its data going forward.

blocks.heading: Your Scenario
blocks.text: Client: Coastal Eats — a new café opening in Fremantle. You have received a USB drive from the client containing: a logo file (large TIFF, no folder structure), 12 high-resolution photos from a professional photographer (RAW files, mixed naming), a menu document (three versions with confusing names: menu_v2.docx, menu_NEW.docx, menu_USE THIS.docx), and a short promotional video (uncompressed MOV, 2.1 GB). The client needs all assets prepared for: (a) their printed menu, (b) their website, (c) their Instagram profile, and (d) internal file management going forward.
Project Tasks
Task 1 — Design a Folder Structure and Naming Convention

blocks.title: Task 1: Organise the Project Assets
blocks.intro: Before optimising anything, establish a professional folder structure and naming convention for all Coastal Eats assets.
steps: Design a folder structure for the Coastal Eats project. You must have at minimum: separate folders for raw/master files, working files, and delivery-ready exported files. Organise by asset type (images, documents, video) or by destination (print, web, social) — justify your choice.

Write a file naming convention document (one page) that specifies: how files should be named, what version numbering system to use, what date format to use, and what status labels to use (draft, review, approved).
Apply your naming convention: rename the three menu documents according to your convention and place them correctly in your folder structure. Show which is the 'current' version.
Take a screenshot of your complete folder structure. Annotate it (using Word or a drawing tool) to explain the purpose of each folder.

blocks.text: Task 2 — Optimise Images for Each Platform

blocks.title: Task 2: Export Images for Three Destinations
blocks.intro: Select two of the Coastal Eats photographs (use royalty-free images from Unsplash if you don't have access to real assets). Optimise and export each for three destinations.
steps: For each image, record the original pixel dimensions and file size.
Print Export: Resize/resample to 300 DPI at A4 size (2480 × 1654 px for landscape). Save as JPEG at 95% quality. Record the output file size.
Website Export: Crop and resize to 1920 × 1080 px (16:9). Save as JPEG at 80% quality. Record the output file size.
Instagram Export: Crop and resize to 1080 × 1080 px (square). Save as JPEG at 85% quality. Record the output file size.
Create a table comparing: Original vs Print vs Web vs Instagram — pixel dimensions, file size, and format.
Calculate the percentage file size saving for the web and Instagram exports compared to the original.
Name all files according to your naming convention from Task 1 and place them in the correct delivery folders.

args.name: Original
args.description: File size before compression/resampling (in KB or MB — use consistent units)

args.name: Compressed
args.description: File size of the exported/optimised file in the same units

args.name: Example
args.description: If the original photo is 22 MB and the web export is 1.8 MB: ((22 − 1.8) ÷ 22) × 100 = 91.8% saving

blocks.text: Task 3 — Cloud Storage Recommendation

blocks.title: Task 3: Recommend a Cloud Solution for Coastal Eats
blocks.intro: The café owner currently stores everything on a USB drive that has nearly been lost twice. They need a cloud storage and collaboration solution for their 4-person team (owner + 3 casual staff). Budget is limited.
steps: Research two cloud storage options suitable for a small business with 4 users and a limited budget (consider Google Workspace Business Starter, Microsoft 365 Business Basic, or Dropbox Business).
Compare them on: cost per user per month, storage per user, file version history, sharing/collaboration features, and mobile app quality.
Create a comparison table in your report document.

Write a recommendation (one paragraph, approximately 150 words) explaining which solution you recommend for Coastal Eats and why. Address: cost, ease of use for non-technical staff, version history, and file access from multiple devices.

blocks.text: Task 4 — System Maintenance Plan

blocks.title: Task 4: Create a Maintenance Schedule
blocks.intro: The café's office computer has not been maintained for over a year. Create a practical maintenance plan.
steps: Identify five specific maintenance tasks that should be performed on the café's Windows computer.
For each task, specify: what tool is used, how often it should be done, and what problem it prevents.
Format this as a professional maintenance schedule table that a non-technical café owner could follow.
Add a brief note explaining why keeping antivirus definitions updated is especially important for a business that processes customer payments.

blocks.text: Task 5 — Reflection

blocks.title: Project Reflection — Analysis Questions
blocks.scenario: You have completed the Coastal Eats digital asset management project. Reflect on what you have applied and why these professional practices matter.

questions.question: Explain why a business like Coastal Eats benefits from storing assets in the cloud rather than on a USB drive. In your answer, refer to at least two specific advantages of cloud storage. (3 marks)
You exported the same photograph at different quality levels for print, web, and Instagram. Explain why using a single high-resolution file for all three purposes would be a problem. (3 marks)

The client wants to know if they should use JPEG or PNG for their café logo on the website. Advise them, explaining the reason for your choice in terms of compression type and image characteristics. (2 marks)
Evaluate the importance of document version control for a small business. What could go wrong without it, and how does a naming convention and folder structure address those risks? (4 marks)

blocks.heading: Industry Standard: Cloud-First Approach
blocks.text: The skills you have applied in this project — asset optimisation, version control, cloud storage, and system maintenance — are daily tasks in digital marketing agencies, IT departments, graphic design studios, and data teams. Employers consistently identify these as fundamental digital literacy skills expected of all IT graduates, regardless of specialisation.
Project Review Questions

question.text: During the project, you needed to save the café logo for the website. The logo has a transparent background and sharp edges. Which format should you choose?
options: JPEG — smaller file size is important for websites
PNG — supports transparency and lossless compression preserves sharp edges
TIFF — the highest quality format available
GIF — the most widely supported web format
question.explanation: PNG is the correct choice for logos on websites. It supports transparency (so the logo can sit over any background colour) and uses lossless compression, preserving the sharp edges of text and vector-style graphics. JPEG does not support transparency and its lossy compression creates artefacts on sharp edges.

question.text: The client's promotional video is 2.1 GB in MOV format. They need to upload it to Instagram. What is the most appropriate action?
options: Upload the 2.1 GB MOV file directly — Instagram will handle compression
Convert to H.264 MP4 at 1080p using lossy video compression to reduce the file size significantly
Convert to PNG format to reduce the file size
Split the file into smaller pieces using a ZIP archiver
question.explanation: H.264 (MP4) lossy video compression will reduce the 2.1 GB file to roughly 200–500 MB at 1080p — a reduction of 75–90% — while maintaining sufficient quality for social media. Instagram also has a maximum file size limit (4 GB) and requires specific formats. MOV files may not upload correctly, and PNG is not a video format.

question.text: Write a short email (150–200 words) to the Coastal Eats café owner explaining the three most important data management practices you have implemented for their business and why each matters. Write as a junior IT professional communicating with a non-technical client.
question.explanation: A strong response will: use professional but accessible language (avoid unexplained jargon); clearly explain file compression/optimisation (faster website, correct quality for print); explain version control/naming conventions (prevents overwriting work, easy to find the right file); explain cloud storage benefits (data not lost if USB is lost, access from anywhere, team collaboration); be appropriately concise and structured for a business email; and demonstrate understanding of why each practice benefits the client specifically.

blocks.text: What is a Computer Network?

A computer network is two or more devices connected together so they can communicate and share resources. Networks underpin almost everything in modern business — from sending an email to processing a credit card payment. Understanding networking is foundational for any IT career.

items.definition: Two or more devices connected together to share resources, data, and services.
A network covering a single building or site — such as an office, school, or home.
A network spanning large geographic distances — connecting offices in different cities or countries.
A global WAN — a vast interconnection of millions of networks that allows worldwide communication.

blocks.text: Types of Networks

rows: Office computers connected to a shared server and printer
A bank's branch offices connected to head office
Billions of devices connected worldwide via ISPs

blocks.text: Advantages of Networking

items: Resource sharing — printers, scanners, and storage can be shared across many users, reducing costs
File sharing — staff can access and collaborate on the same documents from any connected device
Communication — email, video calls, and messaging work through the network
Centralised management — IT staff can update software, apply security patches, and manage user accounts from one location
Internet access — a single internet connection can be shared across all devices on the network
Data backup — files stored on a central server are easier to back up regularly

blocks.text: Disadvantages of Networking

items: Setup cost — cabling, switches, routers, and servers require significant upfront investment
Security risks — a connected network can be attacked; one infected device can spread malware to all
Dependence on infrastructure — if the network goes down, staff may be unable to work
Requires technical expertise — networks need skilled staff to set up, maintain, and troubleshoot
Privacy concerns — shared resources require careful access control to protect sensitive data

blocks.heading: Industry Insight: Why Networks Are Business-Critical
blocks.text: A 2023 Gartner study estimated that unplanned network downtime costs businesses an average of $5,600 per minute. For a large organisation, a one-hour outage can cost hundreds of thousands of dollars in lost productivity, sales, and reputation. Network reliability is not just an IT issue — it's a business continuity issue.
Business Networking Needs

Different businesses have different networking requirements. A small business with five staff has very different needs to a hospital or a university. When designing or evaluating a network, IT professionals consider: the number of users, the types of tasks performed, security requirements, budget, and physical layout of the building.

rows: Shared internet, shared printer, file server or cloud storage
Dedicated server, managed switches, VLAN segmentation, firewall
Multiple servers, redundant links, data centre, 24/7 monitoring

blocks.heading: Network Admin Career Path
blocks.text:
Networking skills open doors to high-demand roles:

• IT Support Technician — entry-level, $55,000–$70,000/yr; installs and troubleshoots network connections
• Network Administrator — manages and maintains organisation networks, $75,000–$95,000/yr
• Network Engineer — designs and builds complex networks, $90,000–$120,000/yr
• Cloud Network Architect — designs cloud-based network infrastructure, $130,000+/yr

Certifications such as CompTIA Network+, Cisco CCNA, and AWS Certified Advanced Networking are highly valued by employers in Australia.
Check Your Understanding

question.text: A business has three offices in different Australian cities. Each office has its own internal network. What type of network connects all three offices together?
options: LAN — Local Area Network
PAN — Personal Area Network
WAN — Wide Area Network
WLAN — Wireless Local Area Network
question.explanation: A WAN (Wide Area Network) spans large geographic areas, connecting networks in different cities or countries. The internet is the world's largest WAN. A LAN only covers a single building or site.

question.text: Which of the following is a DISADVANTAGE of computer networking?
options: Multiple users can share a single printer
Staff can access files from any device on the network
A virus on one device can spread to all networked devices
Software updates can be deployed centrally
question.explanation: One significant disadvantage of networking is that security threats can spread quickly. Malware, ransomware, or viruses on one machine can propagate to every device connected to the same network if not properly contained.

question.text: Explain TWO advantages and ONE disadvantage of networking computers in a small legal firm with eight employees. In your answer, refer to the specific needs of this type of business.
question.explanation: Strong answers will identify advantages relevant to a legal firm (e.g. shared access to case files/documents on a central server; shared printer/scanner for paperwork; easier data backup of confidential client files) and a relevant disadvantage (e.g. security risk — legal firms hold sensitive client data and a network breach could expose confidential information and breach professional obligations). Award 2 marks per well-explained point with business context.

blocks.text: How Data Travels Across a Network

Data must travel from one device to another through some form of transmission medium. The choice of medium affects speed, reliability, cost, and suitability for different environments. There are three main categories: optic fibre, copper wired, and wireless.

items.definition: The physical or wireless path through which data travels between network devices.
The speed at which data is transferred across a network, measured in bits per second (bps). Common units: Mbps (megabits per second) and Gbps (gigabits per second).
The maximum amount of data that can be transmitted over a network connection in a given time — like the width of a pipe.
The delay between sending and receiving data — measured in milliseconds (ms). Low latency is critical for video calls and gaming.

blocks.text: Optic Fibre

blocks.heading: How Optic Fibre Works
blocks.html:
Optic fibre transmits data as pulses of light through thin strands of glass or plastic. Light travels at close to 200,000 km/s through the fibre, making it the fastest transmission medium available.

Light source                       Light detector
   [LASER] ===~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~=== [RECEIVER]
             glass/plastic fibre
             (hair-thin strand)

Data encoded as light pulses:
  ON  = 1
  OFF = 0

Fibre is used in long-distance infrastructure (undersea cables, city backbones) and increasingly in homes and businesses via NBN fibre connections.

blocks.text: Copper Wired (Ethernet)

blocks.heading: How Ethernet Cable Works
blocks.html:
Ethernet cables (Cat5e, Cat6, Cat6a) use copper wire to transmit data as electrical signals. They are the standard for wired connections within office LANs.

Device A                           Device B
 [PC] ---[===copper cable===]--- [Switch]

Cat5e: up to 1 Gbps  (100m)
Cat6:  up to 10 Gbps (55m)
Cat6a: up to 10 Gbps (100m)

blocks.text: Wireless (Wi-Fi)

blocks.heading: How Wireless Transmission Works
blocks.html:
Wireless networks transmit data as radio waves through the air. A Wireless Access Point (WAP) sends and receives signals, allowing devices to connect without physical cables.

        )))  Wi-Fi signal  (((
[WAP] ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~  [Laptop]
        radio waves

Wi-Fi 5 (802.11ac): up to ~3.5 Gbps
Wi-Fi 6 (802.11ax): up to ~9.6 Gbps
(real-world speeds much lower due to walls/interference)

blocks.text: Comparing Transmission Media

rows: Good, but can drop in crowded areas
Less secure — signals can be intercepted
High — connect from anywhere in range

blocks.text: Transmission Rates

Transmission rate (also called data transfer rate or bandwidth) is the speed at which data moves across a network. It is measured in bits per second (bps). When choosing a network medium, higher transmission rates mean faster file transfers, better video streaming quality, and more users supported simultaneously.

rows: Undersea fibre cables, data centre links

blocks.heading: Bits vs Bytes
blocks.text: Network speeds are measured in bits (b), while file sizes are measured in bytes (B). There are 8 bits in a byte. A 100 Mbps connection downloads at approximately 12.5 MB/s. This is why internet marketing uses Mbps — it sounds faster than MB/s!
Business vs Home: Which Medium?

blocks.title: Home vs Business Network Media Choices

items.label: Home Network
points: Wireless (Wi-Fi) for phones, tablets, laptops — convenience is priority
NBN (optic fibre or copper to the premises) for internet connection
Some households use Ethernet for smart TVs or gaming consoles (better reliability)
Cost is a key factor — minimal cabling preferred

items.label: Business / Office Network
points: Ethernet (copper Cat6) for desktop PCs, servers, and printers — reliability and speed
Wi-Fi for laptops, phones, and meeting rooms — flexibility for mobile workers
Fibre backbone between floors or between buildings on a campus
High uptime requirements justify higher infrastructure costs

blocks.heading: Industry Insight: The NBN and Fibre Rollout
blocks.text: Australia's National Broadband Network (NBN) uses a mix of technologies — Fibre to the Premises (FTTP), Fibre to the Node (FTTN), and Hybrid Fibre Coaxial (HFC). Network engineers working for NBN Co, Telstra, and ISPs manage this infrastructure daily. Understanding transmission media is essential for roles in telecommunications and network installation.
Check Your Understanding

question.text: Which transmission medium transmits data as pulses of light through glass strands?
options: Copper Ethernet cable
Wireless (Wi-Fi)
Optic fibre
Coaxial cable
question.explanation: Optic fibre uses pulses of light — generated by a laser or LED — to transmit data through thin glass or plastic strands. This allows very high speeds over long distances without electrical interference.

question.text: A network technician is setting up a busy open-plan office with 40 desktop PCs that need fast, reliable connections. They need consistent speed for large file transfers. Which transmission medium is MOST appropriate for connecting the desktop PCs?
options: Wireless (Wi-Fi) — no cabling needed
Copper Ethernet (Cat6) — fast and reliable wired connection
Optic fibre to each desktop — fastest option
Bluetooth — easiest to set up
question.explanation: Copper Ethernet (Cat6) is the standard choice for office desktop PCs. It provides consistent Gigabit speeds, is not affected by wireless interference, and is cost-effective for a fixed installation. Optic fibre to every desktop would be unnecessarily expensive; Wi-Fi is less reliable under heavy load.

question.text: A file is 500 MB in size. Approximately how long would it take to transfer over a 100 Mbps network connection?
options: 5 seconds
40 seconds
500 seconds
4 seconds
question.explanation: 500 MB = 500 × 8 = 4,000 Mb. At 100 Mbps: 4,000 ÷ 100 = 40 seconds. Remember to convert bytes to bits (multiply by 8) before dividing by the connection speed in Mbps.

blocks.text: The Hardware That Makes a Network Work

Every network is built from a set of physical components, each with a specific role. Understanding what each component does — and why it is needed — allows IT professionals to set up, maintain, and troubleshoot networks effectively. This lesson uses a real office network scenario to bring the components to life.

blocks.heading: A Typical Small Business Office Network
blocks.html:
INTERNET
                           |
                        [MODEM]
                           |
                        [ROUTER]
                           |
                 ________[SWITCH]________
                |          |            |
            [Server]    [PC 1]      [PC 2]
            (files,
            backups)      |            |
                       [NIC]        [NIC]

         Wi-Fi signal from router:
              )))(((   )))(((   )))(((  
             [Laptop] [Phone] [Tablet]

Each component in this diagram has a specific purpose — removing any one of them would break part of the network.

blocks.text: Core Network Components

items.label: Server
items.description: A powerful computer that provides services to other devices (clients) on the network. Stores shared files, hosts applications, manages user accounts, and handles backups. In a business, the server is the central hub for data and services.

items.label: Router
items.description: Connects your network to the internet (and to other networks). Directs data packets to the correct destination — inside the network or out to the internet. Assigns IP addresses to devices via DHCP. Usually connects to the modem.

items.label: Network Interface Card (NIC)
items.description: Hardware inside every networked device (PC, server, printer) that allows it to connect to a network. Wired NICs have an Ethernet port; wireless NICs have a Wi-Fi antenna built in. Every NIC has a unique MAC address.

items.label: Switch
items.description: Connects multiple wired devices on the same LAN. Intelligently sends data only to the intended device (unlike an older hub which broadcast to all). An office might have one or more switches connecting dozens of PCs, printers, and servers.

items.label: Modem
items.description: Connects your network to the internet via your ISP (Internet Service Provider). Converts the ISP's signal (phone line, cable, or fibre) into a format your router can use. MOdulator-DEModulator — converts digital to analogue and back.

blocks.text: Component Function Comparison

rows: Converts ISP signal for local network
Routes packets between networks; assigns IP addresses
Devices cannot communicate with internet or other networks
Connects multiple wired devices on the same LAN
Only one device can connect wired to the router
Allows a device to connect to the network
That device cannot join the network
No centralised storage, authentication, or services

blocks.heading: Modem + Router Combo Units
blocks.text: In home and small office setups, the modem and router are often combined into one device (a 'gateway' or 'modem-router'). In larger businesses, they are kept separate so each can be configured and upgraded independently. Network engineers always distinguish between the two functions.
The Role of the Server
In a business network, the server is critical. It runs continuously (24/7) and provides services that all users depend on. Common server roles include:

items: File server — stores and manages shared files and folders that staff access from their own PCs
Print server — manages print jobs sent to shared printers from any device
Domain controller / authentication server — verifies usernames and passwords when staff log in
Web server — hosts internal websites or external customer-facing sites
Backup server — automatically backs up data from all devices on the network
Email server — manages all incoming and outgoing email for the organisation

blocks.heading: Network Admin Career Path: Server Skills
blocks.text: Server administration is a core competency for Network Administrators and Systems Administrators. In Australia, Microsoft Windows Server and Linux (Ubuntu Server, Red Hat) are the dominant server platforms. Certifications: Microsoft Certified: Azure Administrator, CompTIA Server+. Entry-level roles in server support typically start at $60,000–$75,000/yr.
Check Your Understanding

question.text: In a small business office, which device is responsible for directing data packets between the local network and the internet?
options: Switch
Server
NIC
Router
question.explanation: The router directs (routes) data packets between networks — including between your local network (LAN) and the internet. It uses IP addresses to determine where each packet should go. The switch connects devices within the LAN but does not route to the internet.

question.text: A PC in the office cannot connect to the network. The technician discovers the network port on the PC is not working. Which component is most likely faulty?
options: The router
The switch
The NIC (Network Interface Card)
The modem
question.explanation: The NIC (Network Interface Card) is the hardware inside the PC that provides the network port (Ethernet socket). If the port is faulty, the NIC needs to be replaced. The router and switch would still work for other devices — only this one PC is affected.

question.text: Match each network component to its primary function.

pairs.definition: Connects the local network to the internet via the ISP
Connects multiple wired devices on the same LAN
Provides shared files, applications, and services to client devices
Hardware that allows a device to physically connect to a network
Routes data packets between networks and assigns IP addresses

blocks.text: What is a Network Topology?

Network topology describes how devices are arranged and connected in a network — both the physical layout of cables and devices, and the logical flow of data. Choosing the right topology affects performance, reliability, cost, and ease of management. The Australian curriculum focuses on four topologies relevant to LANs.

items.definition: The arrangement of devices and connections in a network — how they are physically or logically structured.
Any device connected to a network (PC, printer, server, phone, etc.).
A network covering a single site — the context for all four topologies studied in this topic.

blocks.text: 1. Wired Star Topology

blocks.heading: Diagram: Wired Star
blocks.html:
[PC 1]
            |
[PC 4] ---[SWITCH]--- [PC 2]
            |
         [PC 3]
            |
         [Server]

All devices connect via cables to a central switch.
If one cable fails, only that device is affected.

blocks.text: In a wired star topology, every device connects to a central switch using Ethernet cables. The switch manages all communication between devices. This is the most common topology in modern business office networks.
2. Wireless Topology

blocks.heading: Diagram: Wireless
blocks.html:
[Laptop]   [Phone]   [Tablet]
         )))  ((( )))  (((
              [WAP]
               |
            [Router] --- [Internet]
               |
            [Switch]
               |
            [Server] (wired)

Mobile devices connect wirelessly via the WAP.
Fixed devices (server) remain wired for reliability.

blocks.text: A wireless topology uses a Wireless Access Point (WAP) — often built into the router — so devices connect using Wi-Fi rather than cables. In practice, most business networks are hybrid: fixed devices use Ethernet, while mobile devices use Wi-Fi.
3. Client-Server Topology

blocks.heading: Diagram: Client-Server
blocks.html:
[SERVER]
         /    |    \
        /     |     \
  [PC 1]  [PC 2]  [PC 3]
  client   client   client

The SERVER:
  - Stores all files centrally
  - Manages user accounts & login
  - Controls access permissions
  - Handles printing & email

CLIENTS request services from the server.

blocks.text: In a client-server network, one or more dedicated servers provide resources and services. Client computers (PCs, laptops) request these services. This is a logical arrangement that works on top of the physical topology (usually wired star). It is the standard model for business networks.
4. Peer-to-Peer Topology

blocks.heading: Diagram: Peer-to-Peer
blocks.html:
[PC 1] ------- [PC 2]
     |      \  /    |
     |       \/     |
     |       /\     |
     |      /  \    |
  [PC 3] ------- [PC 4]

Each PC can share its own files and resources.
No central server — all devices are equal (peers).

blocks.text: In a peer-to-peer (P2P) network, all devices are equal — any device can share its own files, printer, or internet connection with others. There is no central server. This is common in very small home or micro-business networks.
Client-Server vs Peer-to-Peer

items.label: Client-Server
points: Centralised control — IT staff manage one server, applying security policies and updates to all users
Easier to back up — all data stored on the server in one place
Better security — access permissions controlled by the server
Scales well — can support hundreds of users
Higher cost — requires a dedicated server and server operating system licence
Server = single point of failure — if the server goes down, all clients lose access
Used in: businesses, schools, hospitals, government organisations

items.label: Peer-to-Peer
points: Decentralised — each device manages its own files and sharing
Cheap to set up — no dedicated server hardware or licence needed
Easy for small groups — good for 2–5 computers in a home or tiny office
No central backup — each user responsible for their own files
Harder to secure — no central policy enforcement
Does not scale — becomes unmanageable with more than ~10 devices
Used in: home networks, very small offices, temporary setups

blocks.text: Topology Comparison Table

rows: All devices cabled to central switch
Fast and reliable; easy to add/remove devices; one cable fault doesn't affect others
Requires cabling to every device; switch is a single point of failure
Office with fixed desktop PCs needing reliable, high-speed connections
Devices connect via radio waves to WAP
No cabling needed; supports mobile devices; easy to add devices
Slower and less reliable than wired; security risks; interference from walls and devices
Laptops, phones, and tablets; areas where cabling is impractical
Logical arrangement over wired/wireless physical topology
Centralised control; easy to manage; scalable; better security and backup
Expensive server required; server failure disrupts all clients
Any business with more than ~5 users needing centralised data and security
Low cost; easy to set up; no server needed
No central management; poor security; does not scale; backup is each user's responsibility
Very small home networks or temporary setups with 2–5 devices

blocks.heading: Industry Insight: Real Networks Are Hybrid
blocks.text: In practice, modern business networks combine topologies. A typical office uses a wired star physical topology, client-server logical arrangement, AND wireless for mobile devices. Network Engineers need to understand all topologies to design networks that balance cost, reliability, security, and scalability for each client's specific needs.
Check Your Understanding

question.text: In a wired star topology, what happens if the central switch fails?
options: Only the device directly connected to the failed port loses connection
The entire network loses connectivity — all devices are affected
Devices automatically switch to wireless to maintain connection
Only the server loses connection; client PCs keep working
question.explanation: In a star topology, the central switch is a single point of failure. Every device connects through the switch, so if the switch fails, all devices lose network connectivity. This is a key disadvantage of the star topology — but it can be mitigated by using a redundant (backup) switch.

question.text: A small graphic design studio has 4 computers. The owner wants to share files between computers and connect to a shared printer, but cannot afford a dedicated server. Which network topology is most appropriate?
options: Client-server — best for all business networks
Peer-to-peer — suitable for small networks without a dedicated server
Wired star — requires too many cables for a small studio
Wireless only — most modern option
question.explanation: Peer-to-peer is appropriate for a very small network (4 devices) where cost is a concern and there is no need for centralised management. Each computer can share its files and the printer with the others. Client-server would be better for larger networks needing security and central management.

question.text: A medical clinic with 15 staff is setting up a new network. They need to store patient records securely, allow staff to log in from any PC in the clinic, and ensure files are backed up nightly. Explain which network topology is most suitable and justify your choice.
question.explanation: Strong answers will recommend a client-server topology (logical) over a wired star physical layout. Justification should include: centralised storage of patient records on a secure server; centralised user accounts allowing staff to log in from any PC; centralised nightly backup of all patient data; IT administrator can control access permissions (ensuring only authorised staff see certain records); scalability for future growth. A peer-to-peer network would be inappropriate because there is no central security control, no centralised backup, and it does not scale to 15 users. Award 2 marks for correct topology identified with brief reason, 2 marks for security/privacy justification, 2 marks for backup/scalability justification.

lessons.title: Designing a Network for a Client

blocks.text: From Theory to Practice: Network Design

Network Engineers and IT Consultants are regularly asked to design networks for clients — from a small office to a large enterprise. This requires applying knowledge of components, transmission media, and topologies to meet a client's specific business needs. In this lesson you will apply everything from this unit to design a real network.

blocks.heading: Network Admin Career Path: What IT Consultants Do
blocks.text: IT Consultants and Network Designers are paid to understand a client's business needs and translate them into a technical solution. They prepare network diagrams, write specifications, recommend hardware, and provide cost estimates. This role requires both technical knowledge AND communication skills — you must explain technical decisions to non-technical clients. Average salary in Australia: $90,000–$115,000/yr.
The Design Process

items: Understand the requirements — how many users? What tasks? What devices? What budget?
Choose a topology — wired star, wireless, client-server, peer-to-peer, or hybrid?
Select components — which server, switch, router, modem, NIC specifications are needed?
Choose transmission media — Ethernet cable, fibre, wireless, or a combination?
Draw the network diagram — show all devices and how they connect
Document the design — list all hardware with justification for each choice
Present to the client — explain the design in plain language with costs and benefits

blocks.text: Key Design Considerations

rows: How many staff? Will it grow?
Determines switch port count, server capacity, IP address range
Video calls? Large file transfers? Point-of-sale?
Affects bandwidth requirements and reliability needs
Is sensitive data involved? (medical, legal, financial)
May require client-server, firewall, VPN, encryption
What is the total IT budget?
Determines whether to use wired/wireless, dedicated server vs cloud
Open plan? Multiple floors? Remote workers?
Affects cable runs, need for wireless coverage, WAP placement
Design should be scalable — choose a managed switch, allow room for more ports

blocks.title: Scenario: The Gibbstown Travel Agency
blocks.scenario:
You have been hired as an IT consultant for Gibbstown Travel Agency, a new small business opening in a single-floor office. They have provided the following brief:

• 5 staff members, each with their own desktop PC
• 1 dedicated file server to store booking records and client files
• 1 shared laser printer used by all staff
• Staff also use laptops and tablets in the meeting room for client presentations
• The business handles client financial information and passport details — security is important
• Budget: moderate — willing to invest in good infrastructure
• The office is a single room, approximately 12m × 8m

questions.question: Recommend a network topology for Gibbstown Travel Agency. Explain why this topology is appropriate for their specific needs, referring to the number of staff, security requirements, and the presence of a file server.
Identify the network components that should be included in this network design. For each component, briefly explain its role in the Gibbstown network.

The 5 desktop PCs and the file server need to be connected to the network. The meeting room requires wireless access for laptops and tablets. Recommend the transmission media you would use for each situation and justify your choices.
Identify ONE potential disadvantage of your recommended topology for Gibbstown, and explain how you would address it in your design.

blocks.title: Task: Draw the Gibbstown Network Diagram
blocks.software: draw.io (diagrams.net) or Microsoft Visio — or pencil and paper
blocks.intro: Using your answers from the scenario challenge above, draw a network diagram for Gibbstown Travel Agency. Your diagram should be clear, labelled, and professional.
steps: Open draw.io (free at diagrams.net) or use pencil and paper for a hand-drawn diagram

Draw and label the following devices: 5 × desktop PC, 1 × file server, 1 × shared laser printer, 1 × switch, 1 × router, 1 × modem, 1 × wireless access point (or router with built-in WAP), 2 × laptop (meeting room), 1 × tablet (meeting room)
Use solid lines (——) to show wired Ethernet connections between the switch, server, PCs, and printer
Use dashed lines (- - -) or a Wi-Fi symbol ((())) to show wireless connections from the WAP to the laptops and tablet in the meeting room
Label the connection between the modem and the internet with the ISP/internet label
Add a legend explaining your symbols (wired connection, wireless connection, each device type)
Add a title: 'Gibbstown Travel Agency — Proposed LAN Design' and include your name and date
Review your diagram: does it show all required devices? Are all connections logical? Is the diagram clear enough for a non-technical client to understand?
Export or photograph your diagram and submit it with your scenario challenge written responses

blocks.text: Sample Network Diagram (Reference)

blocks.heading: A Well-Designed Small Business LAN
blocks.html:
INTERNET
    |
 [MODEM]
    |
 [ROUTER] )))Wi-Fi((( --- [Laptop] [Tablet]
    |        (meeting room wireless)
 [SWITCH]
  / | \ \ \
 /  |  \ \ \
[S] [P1][P2][P3][P4][P5]  [Printer]
(Server)

S   = File Server (wired — critical device)
P1-5 = Staff PCs (wired — reliability)
Printer = Shared laser printer (wired to switch)
Laptop/Tablet = Meeting room (wireless via Router WAP)

All wired connections: Cat6 Ethernet
Wireless: Wi-Fi 6 (802.11ax)

This hybrid design uses a wired star physical topology with client-server logical arrangement for staff PCs and the server, plus wireless access for mobile devices in the meeting room.

blocks.text: Review: Networks Unit Summary

items: A network connects devices to share resources — advantages include file sharing, resource sharing, and centralised management; disadvantages include cost, security risks, and dependency on infrastructure
LANs cover a single site; WANs connect multiple sites; the internet is the global WAN
Transmission media: optic fibre (fastest, most expensive, long distance), copper Ethernet (reliable, standard for office LANs), wireless (flexible, supports mobile devices but variable speed)
Transmission rate measures data transfer speed in bps (Kbps, Mbps, Gbps) — convert bytes to bits (×8) for calculations
Network components: modem (ISP connection), router (routes packets, assigns IPs), switch (connects LAN devices), NIC (device's network hardware), server (provides centralised services)
Wired star: all devices cable to a central switch — standard for office LANs; reliable but switch is single point of failure
Wireless: devices connect via WAP using radio waves — flexible but slower and less secure
Client-server: dedicated server manages resources centrally — scalable, secure, expensive; used in businesses
Peer-to-peer: all devices equal, no server — cheap, simple, not scalable; suitable for very small home/micro-business networks

question.text: Gibbstown Travel Agency stores client passport details and financial information. Which network arrangement BEST ensures this data is backed up securely and access is restricted to authorised staff only?
options: Peer-to-peer — cheapest to set up
Wireless only — most modern option
Client-server with a dedicated file server and access permissions
Peer-to-peer with a shared folder on one staff PC
question.explanation: A client-server arrangement with a dedicated file server allows the IT administrator to set access permissions (controlling who can view sensitive files), perform centralised nightly backups of all data, and enforce security policies. Peer-to-peer has no central security control, making it inappropriate for sensitive client data.

question.text: A new café with 3 staff and no server wants to share a printer between their 3 PCs and connect to the internet. They have a small budget. Describe the most appropriate network setup for this café, including topology type, whether they need a server, and what components are required.
question.explanation: Model answer: A peer-to-peer network is most suitable — 3 devices is small enough to manage without a server, and budget is limited. Components needed: modem (for internet via ISP), router (with built-in wireless for customer Wi-Fi if needed), switch (to connect the 3 PCs and shared printer), NIC in each PC, and a network-connected printer (or a print server adapter for a non-network printer). No dedicated server is required — one PC can share the printer to the others. A wired star physical topology using Cat6 Ethernet would provide reliable connections in a small space.

blocks.text: Privacy Act 1988

Australia's Privacy Act 1988 is federal legislation that governs how organisations collect, store, use, and disclose personal information. For Year 12 IT students heading into industry, understanding this law is not optional — it shapes how every Australian business handles customer and employee data.

blocks.definition: Australian federal legislation (Commonwealth) that regulates the handling of personal information by government agencies and private sector organisations through 13 Australian Privacy Principles (APPs).

blocks.text: What is Personal Information?

Personal information is any information or opinion about an identified individual, or an individual who is reasonably identifiable. This includes names, addresses, email addresses, phone numbers, financial details, health records, biometric data, and online identifiers such as IP addresses and device IDs.

blocks.heading: Legal Requirement
blocks.text: Organisations with an annual turnover over $3 million, plus all private health service providers, credit reporting bodies, and some other entities, are legally required to comply with the Privacy Act 1988. Non-compliance can result in penalties of up to $50 million for serious or repeated breaches.
The Australian Privacy Principles (APPs)
The 13 APPs form the backbone of Australian privacy law. They cover the entire lifecycle of personal information — from first collection through to final disposal.

rows: Organisations must have a clear, publicly available privacy policy explaining how they handle personal data
Individuals must be given the option to interact anonymously where lawful and practical
Only collect personal information that is reasonably necessary for the organisation's functions
If you receive personal data you didn't request and didn't need, destroy or de-identify it
Notify individuals at or before the time of collection — who you are, why you're collecting it, who you'll share it with
Use and disclosure of personal information
Use personal information only for the primary purpose it was collected, unless an exception applies
Personal information cannot be used for direct marketing unless the individual has consented or an opt-out is provided
Before sharing data overseas, take reasonable steps to ensure foreign recipients uphold equivalent privacy standards
Adoption, use or disclosure of government identifiers
Organisations cannot adopt government identifiers (e.g. Tax File Numbers) as their own
Take reasonable steps to ensure personal information is accurate, up-to-date, and complete
Protect personal information from misuse, interference, loss, and unauthorised access or disclosure
Individuals have the right to access their own personal information held by an organisation
Individuals have the right to request correction of inaccurate, outdated, or incomplete personal information

blocks.text: Collection of Personal Information

Under APP 3, organisations may only collect personal information that is directly necessary for a specific, legitimate purpose. This is the 'data minimisation' principle. For example, a video streaming service needs your email and payment details — it does not need your home address, date of birth, or health information to deliver its service.
Use of Personal Information

Under APP 6, once personal data is collected for a stated purpose, it cannot be used for a different purpose without the individual's consent. If you sign up to a hardware store loyalty program, that store cannot legally sell your purchase history to a competing retailer or a health insurance company without your knowledge and agreement.
Access to Personal Information

Under APP 12, Australians have a legal right to request access to any personal information an organisation holds about them. The organisation must respond within a reasonable timeframe (generally 30 days) and provide the information in the format requested where practical. Organisations can only refuse access in limited circumstances, such as when disclosure would threaten someone's safety.
Identity Theft and the Privacy Act

blocks.definition: The fraudulent acquisition and use of another person's personal information — such as name, date of birth, address, and financial details — to impersonate them for financial gain or to commit crimes.

blocks.text: The Privacy Act's security requirements (APP 11) are a key defence against identity theft. Organisations holding large volumes of personal data are attractive targets for cybercriminals seeking to commit identity fraud. When an organisation fails to adequately secure personal data, individuals can suffer financial loss, damage to credit ratings, and years of administrative burden to correct the damage.

blocks.title: Case Study: MediCare Portal Data Breach
blocks.scenario: In 2023, a major Australian health insurance provider suffered a data breach in which the personal information of 9.7 million current and former customers was accessed by cybercriminals. The stolen data included names, dates of birth, Medicare numbers, and sensitive health claim information. The attackers initially accessed the system using stolen employee credentials. The breach was not detected for several weeks. The Australian Information Commissioner launched an investigation into whether the company had complied with APP 11 (security of personal information).

questions.question: Identify TWO Australian Privacy Principles that may have been breached in this scenario. Justify your choices.
Explain how the affected individuals could exercise their rights under APP 12 (access to personal information) in the aftermath of this breach.
From an IT professional's perspective, describe TWO technical controls the company should have had in place to reduce the likelihood of this breach occurring.
Discuss the consequences — both legal and reputational — that the organisation could face as a result of this breach.

blocks.heading: Privacy Officer Career
blocks.text: Large Australian organisations are increasingly hiring Privacy Officers (also called Chief Privacy Officers or Data Protection Officers). These IT-adjacent roles are responsible for ensuring APP compliance, conducting privacy impact assessments, managing data breach response, and training staff. Strong knowledge of the Privacy Act is an in-demand skill in the Australian IT job market.

question.text: Under the Australian Privacy Principles, an organisation collects your email address to process an online purchase. According to APP 6, which of the following would be a breach?
options: Sending you a receipt for the purchase
Storing the email address securely in their database
Selling your email address to a marketing firm without your consent
Allowing you to request correction of your email address under APP 13
question.explanation: APP 6 requires that personal information is only used for the primary purpose for which it was collected. Selling the email to a marketing firm is a secondary use and requires consent. Options A and B are consistent with the original collection purpose.

question.text: Which Australian Privacy Principle specifically requires organisations to notify individuals about why their personal information is being collected?
options: APP 3 — Collection of solicited personal information
APP 5 — Notification of collection
APP 10 — Quality of personal information
APP 12 — Access to personal information
question.explanation: APP 5 requires organisations to notify individuals at or before the time of collection about who is collecting the data, why it is being collected, and who it may be shared with. This is typically delivered through a privacy notice or privacy policy.

lessons.title: Safe Disposal of Data and Devices

blocks.text: Safe Disposal of Data and Devices

Disposing of data and digital devices without following proper procedures is one of the most common — and most avoidable — causes of data breaches in Australia. The Privacy Act 1988 (APP 11) requires organisations to take reasonable steps to destroy or de-identify personal information once it is no longer needed. For IT professionals, understanding correct disposal procedures is a core workplace competency.
Why Simply Deleting Files is Not Enough

When you delete a file, the operating system removes the reference to it in the file system index — but the actual data remains on the storage media until new data overwrites that space. Forensic tools such as Recuva, FTK Imager, or commercial data recovery services can retrieve files deleted in this way, often with complete accuracy. This means devices that are resold, donated, or discarded with 'deleted' data can expose personal information to anyone with basic technical knowledge.

blocks.definition: The process of deliberately, permanently, and irreversibly removing or destroying data stored on a device so it cannot be recovered.

blocks.text: Methods of Secure Data Disposal

rows: Software writes random data over every sector multiple times (e.g. DoD 5220.22-M standard uses 3–7 passes)
Slower on large drives; not certified for SSDs in all cases
Firmware-level command that resets all storage cells — built into most modern drives
SSDs and modern HDDs before reuse
Must be supported by drive firmware; requires technical knowledge
Encrypt the drive fully, then delete the decryption key — data remains but is unreadable without the key
Cloud storage, SSDs where overwriting is impractical
Security depends on encryption algorithm strength
Exposes magnetic media to a powerful magnetic field, scrambling all data
HDDs and magnetic tape when device will not be reused
Does not work on SSDs or flash storage; device is destroyed
Device is fed through an industrial shredder that reduces it to small fragments
Any media requiring highest assurance — classified or sensitive business data
Device is permanently destroyed; must use certified shredding service

blocks.heading: IT Ethics
blocks.text: Passing on a device — whether donating an old school laptop or decommissioning company hardware — without sanitising it first is both an ethical failure and a potential legal breach. You are responsible for ensuring personal data does not end up in the wrong hands. Always document the disposal method used, especially in a professional environment.
Industry Standards for Data Disposal

Australian Government agencies and many private sector organisations follow the Australian Signals Directorate (ASD) guidelines for secure disposal, published in the Information Security Manual (ISM). These guidelines specify which disposal methods are approved for different data classifications. For TOP SECRET data, physical destruction is mandatory. For lower-classification data, approved overwriting methods may be sufficient.

items: NIST Special Publication 800-88: Guidelines for Media Sanitization — widely referenced international standard
ASD Information Security Manual (ISM) — Australian government standard
ISO 27001 — international information security management standard that includes asset disposal requirements
DoD 5220.22-M — US Department of Defense standard, commonly referenced in commercial contexts

blocks.text: BYOD Policies and Disposal

blocks.definition: A workplace policy that allows employees to use their personal smartphones, laptops, or tablets for work purposes, accessing company systems and data on personal hardware.

blocks.text: BYOD creates a significant data disposal challenge. When an employee leaves an organisation, company data — emails, files, app credentials, VPN configurations — may remain on their personal device. A robust BYOD policy must address data disposal by requiring employees to enrol devices in Mobile Device Management (MDM) software, which allows the organisation to remotely wipe company data (sometimes only the company partition) from the device when employment ends.

items: Enrolment in MDM (e.g. Microsoft Intune, Jamf) allows selective remote wipe of corporate data
Employees must agree to data wipe procedures in the BYOD policy before enrolment
Organisations must define what constitutes 'company data' on personal devices
BYOD policies must be consistent with the Privacy Act — employee personal data on the device must not be accessed or deleted without consent

blocks.title: Privacy Breach Recognition: Safe Disposal Scenarios
blocks.instruction: Review each scenario below. Identify whether a privacy breach has occurred and explain what should have been done differently.

emails.subject: Scenario A: Old Reception Computer Donated to Local School
emails.body: Our clinic upgraded our reception computer last month. The old PC still had patient appointment records, Medicare numbers, and contact details stored in the practice management software. The office manager deleted all visible files from the desktop, then donated the computer to a local primary school. The school's IT volunteer plugged it in and found all the patient records were still accessible.

redFlags.detail: Deleting files from the desktop does not remove data from the hard drive. A forensic tool or even a simple 'Recycle Bin' recovery could restore the files.

Medical records are sensitive information under the Privacy Act. APP 11 requires organisations to destroy or de-identify personal information that is no longer needed. Donation without sanitisation is a serious breach.

The clinic should have used certified data destruction software (overwrite all sectors) or physically destroyed the hard drive before donation. A certificate of data destruction should have been kept for audit purposes.

emails.subject: Scenario B: Decommissioning 200 Point-of-Sale Terminals
emails.body: Our IT department is replacing 200 POS terminals across all stores. Before decommissioning, we have: (1) run certified secure erase software on all internal SSDs, verifying completion with a checksum report; (2) removed and shredded all SIM cards and SD cards separately; (3) obtained a certificate of data destruction for our records; (4) engaged a certified e-waste recycler for physical disposal of the hardware. All documentation has been filed in our asset management system.

question.text: A school is donating 30 old laptops to a community organisation. Which of the following is the MOST appropriate method to ensure student data is not exposed?
options: Delete all files from each laptop's desktop and empty the Recycle Bin
Create new user accounts on each laptop so the old accounts are inaccessible
Use certified disk-wiping software to overwrite all data on each drive, then verify with a report
Format the C: drive on each laptop using Windows installation media
question.explanation: Certified disk-wiping software that overwrites all sectors (multiple passes) and produces a verification report is the only option here that reliably prevents data recovery. Simple deletion (A) and new user accounts (B) leave data intact and accessible. A standard format (D) is also insufficient as it does not overwrite data sectors.

question.text: A small accounting firm is closing down. They have 10 years of client tax records stored on a server hard drive and on three employees' personal laptops (used under a BYOD policy). Explain the steps the firm's IT consultant should take to ensure all client data is disposed of in a manner that complies with the Privacy Act 1988.
question.explanation: A strong answer should include: (1) Identifying all data locations — server HDD and BYOD devices; (2) Server HDD — use certified overwriting software or physical destruction (degaussing/shredding), obtain certificate of destruction; (3) BYOD laptops — use MDM remote wipe or require employees to use certified wipe tools under supervision; note that the firm cannot wipe personal data, only company data; (4) Reference to APP 11 requiring destruction of personal information no longer needed; (5) Document all disposal actions for audit/legal purposes; (6) Notify clients if required under the Notifiable Data Breaches scheme.

blocks.text: The Digital Divide

The digital divide refers to the gap between individuals and communities that have meaningful access to digital technologies — computers, smartphones, reliable internet — and those that do not. For IT professionals, understanding this divide is essential because the systems you design and build will either bridge or widen it.

blocks.definition: The inequality in access to, use of, or benefit from digital technologies — including devices, internet connectivity, digital skills, and web-based services — between different demographic groups, geographic regions, or nations.

blocks.text: Dimensions of the Digital Divide
The digital divide is not a single gap — it operates on multiple levels. Access alone does not guarantee meaningful participation in the digital economy.

rows: Physical infrastructure for internet connectivity does not exist or is inadequate
Remote Australian communities without NBN or mobile coverage; developing nations without fibre infrastructure
Devices and internet plans are too expensive relative to household income
Low-income families choosing between data plans and food; students sharing one device between multiple children
Lack of skills to use devices, software, and online services effectively
Older Australians unable to use online government portals; job seekers unable to complete online applications
Available digital content does not serve the language, culture, or needs of the community
Indigenous communities with limited content in language; communities with no local digital services
Connection speeds and reliability vary significantly
Students in regional WA on satellite internet (high latency) vs metropolitan fibre connections

blocks.text: The Digital Divide in Australia

Australia has a significant internal digital divide despite being a high-income country. Geographic isolation is a major factor — remote and regional Australians often rely on satellite internet (such as Starlink or the NBN Sky Muster service) which can have higher latency and lower data caps than metropolitan fibre connections. Socioeconomic factors also play a role, with low-income households, public housing residents, and recent migrants less likely to have reliable home internet.

items: Approximately 2.5 million Australians lack basic digital literacy skills (Australian Digital Inclusion Index)
Remote Indigenous communities have significantly lower rates of internet access than capital cities
Students without home internet are disadvantaged in completing homework, online assessments, and career research
Elderly Australians are disproportionately represented among those with low digital inclusion
The COVID-19 pandemic exposed the divide sharply — students without devices or internet could not access remote learning

blocks.text: The Global Digital Divide

Globally, the divide between high-income and low-income nations is stark. Sub-Saharan Africa has internet penetration rates below 40% in many countries, compared to over 90% in Australia, Japan, and northern Europe. This means billions of people are excluded from e-commerce, online education, telehealth, digital banking, and the broader digital economy — with profound implications for economic development and equality.
Societal Dependency on Digital Technology

Modern society has become deeply dependent on digital technology. Banking, healthcare, education, government services, employment, and communication are all increasingly delivered online. This dependency makes the digital divide more critical — being excluded from digital access now means being excluded from basic participation in society.
Government Initiatives to Bridge the Divide

items: National Broadband Network (NBN): Australian Government infrastructure program to deliver high-speed internet nationally, including satellite services to remote areas
Digital Literacy for Older Australians: Be Connected program providing free training and support for seniors
School laptops programs: State and federal initiatives providing devices to students from low-income families
Starlink and Low Earth Orbit satellites: Commercial solutions now providing high-speed, low-latency internet to remote areas previously underserved by NBN
First Nations Digital Inclusion roadmap: Australian Government commitment to closing the digital gap for Indigenous Australians

blocks.text: Implications for IT Professionals

When you design software, apps, or digital services, you make design choices that either include or exclude users. IT professionals have a responsibility to consider accessibility — including the digital divide — from the start of any project.

blocks.heading: IT Ethics
blocks.text: Designing for the digital divide means: building lightweight web apps that work on low-bandwidth connections; ensuring services work on older, low-cost devices; providing offline functionality where possible; designing for accessibility (screen readers, large text, high contrast); and offering non-digital alternatives for critical services. 'Digital by default' should never mean 'digital only'.

blocks.title: Case Study: Regional School — Digital Divide in Practice
blocks.scenario: Horizon Valley District High School serves students from a remote mining and agricultural community 450 km from Perth. The school has 180 students across Years 7–12. 40% of students live on stations or farms with unreliable satellite internet. The school introduced an online learning management system (LMS) that requires a stable internet connection to access assignments, submit work, and receive teacher feedback. During a teachers-only day, students at home were expected to complete online tasks. Approximately 70 students were unable to access the LMS due to connectivity issues. Several Year 12 students missed a major assignment submission, affecting their ATAR calculations.

questions.question: Identify and explain THREE factors contributing to the digital divide experienced by students at this school.
As the school's IT consultant, propose TWO technical solutions that would allow students with limited internet access to still participate in the online learning program. Justify each solution.
Discuss the broader societal implications if the digital divide in Australian education is not addressed. Consider both short-term and long-term consequences.

question.text: Which of the following BEST describes the concept of the digital divide?
options: The technical difference between analogue and digital signals
The gap in access to and meaningful use of digital technologies between different groups
The divide between social media platforms and professional networking sites
The security boundary between internal and external networks in an organisation
question.explanation: The digital divide refers to inequality in access to, use of, and benefit from digital technologies. It encompasses not just whether someone has a device or internet connection, but whether they can meaningfully participate in the digital world — including skills, affordability, and relevant content.

question.text: A government agency is redesigning its welfare payment system to be entirely online. Which of the following design considerations BEST addresses the digital divide?
options: Using the latest JavaScript framework to ensure the fastest possible user experience
Requiring users to create accounts with multi-factor authentication for maximum security
Ensuring the system works on low-bandwidth connections and maintaining phone and in-person access alternatives
Designing only for mobile devices since most people now use smartphones
question.explanation: Addressing the digital divide requires ensuring critical government services do not exclude those with limited connectivity, older devices, or low digital literacy. Maintaining non-digital alternatives (phone, in-person) is essential when digital access cannot be guaranteed for all citizens.

blocks.text: E-Commerce and 24/7 Business

Electronic commerce (e-commerce) refers to the buying and selling of goods and services over the internet. It has fundamentally changed how businesses operate, who can compete in the market, and how consumers make purchasing decisions. For Year 12 students considering IT careers, e-commerce represents one of the fastest-growing sectors in the digital economy.

blocks.definition: The buying and selling of goods, services, and information through electronic networks — primarily the internet. Includes B2C (business to consumer), B2B (business to business), C2C (consumer to consumer), and government e-services.

blocks.text: How E-Commerce Changed Business Models

Before e-commerce, small businesses were largely constrained by geography — a boutique clothing store in Fremantle could only sell to people who walked through the door. E-commerce demolished that barrier. A sole trader in regional WA can now sell products globally through platforms like Etsy, eBay, or Shopify, with customers processing orders while the business owner sleeps.

items.label: Traditional Retail
points: Fixed operating hours — typically 9am to 5pm weekdays
Geographic catchment — limited to customers who can physically visit
High overheads — rent, utilities, staff for floor coverage
Stock is visible and can be physically inspected
Personal service and immediate fulfilment for in-stock items
Limited reach for marketing — local newspaper, signage, word of mouth

items.label: E-Commerce
points: 24/7 availability — orders accepted any time, any day
Global reach — any customer with internet access can purchase
Lower overheads — no shopfront required; warehouse or dropshipping models possible
Products presented through photos, videos, and reviews
Fulfilment time adds shipping delay; returns process more complex
Digital marketing — SEO, social media, email campaigns, targeted advertising

blocks.text: Improved Digital Communications and E-Commerce

The growth of e-commerce has been driven by improvements in digital communication infrastructure. Faster internet (NBN, 5G), widespread smartphone adoption, secure payment gateways, and reliable logistics tracking have all made online shopping a mainstream activity. In Australia, e-commerce sales exceeded $64 billion in 2024, with continued growth across all retail categories.

items: Secure payment gateways (PayPal, Stripe, Afterpay) reduced consumer fear of online transactions
Mobile-responsive websites and apps enabled shopping from any device
Real-time inventory management systems keep stock information accurate across multiple channels
Automated order confirmation, shipping notifications, and tracking build consumer confidence
Live chat and AI chatbots enable 24/7 customer service without human staff

blocks.text: 24/7 Availability — Advantages and Challenges

rows: No lost sales from closed hours
A customer browsing at 11pm can complete a purchase immediately rather than 'deciding to come back tomorrow' (and often forgetting)
A business in Perth can sell to customers in New York, London, or Tokyo during their local business hours
Automated systems handle transactions without requiring customer service staff on duty overnight
Customers shop when it suits them — during lunch breaks, after children are in bed, or on weekends
Every transaction and page view generates data that businesses use to optimise pricing, layout, and marketing
A website outage during peak shopping periods (e.g. Click Frenzy, Black Friday) can cost thousands of dollars per minute
Handling payment data online requires PCI-DSS compliance and robust cybersecurity — 24/7 attack surface
Online purchases have higher return rates; managing disputes without face-to-face interaction is more complex
Small Australian businesses compete against global giants with massive price advantages and logistics networks
Business owners must understand SEO, digital advertising, analytics, and platform management — or hire someone who does

blocks.text: Advantages for Small Businesses

E-commerce has been particularly transformative for small and micro-businesses in Australia. The ability to operate without a physical shopfront dramatically lowers the barrier to starting a business. Platforms like Shopify, Etsy, and WooCommerce provide ready-built e-commerce infrastructure, so a sole trader can launch a professional online store without deep technical expertise.

blocks.heading: Privacy Officer Career
blocks.text: E-commerce businesses generate substantial personal data — customer names, addresses, purchase histories, browsing behaviour, and payment information. This creates significant demand for IT professionals with privacy and data security expertise. Roles include e-commerce security analyst, data privacy consultant, and digital marketing analyst (who must understand data collection consent requirements under the Privacy Act).
Career Paths in E-Commerce and Digital Marketing

blocks.title: IT and Digital Careers in E-Commerce

items.label: E-Commerce Developer
items.description: Builds and maintains online stores using platforms (Shopify, Magento, WooCommerce) and custom code. Skills: HTML/CSS, JavaScript, APIs, payment gateway integration.

items.label: Digital Marketing Analyst
items.description: Uses data from Google Analytics, Meta Ads, and email platforms to optimise campaigns. Skills: data analysis, A/B testing, SEO, SEM, copywriting.

items.label: UX/UI Designer
items.description: Designs the user interface and shopping experience to maximise conversion rates. Skills: wireframing, user research, Figma, accessibility design.

items.label: Cybersecurity Analyst
items.description: Protects e-commerce platforms from attacks, ensures PCI-DSS compliance, and manages incident response. Skills: penetration testing, SIEM, network security.

items.label: Data Analyst / Business Intelligence
items.description: Analyses sales, customer behaviour, and inventory data to drive business decisions. Skills: SQL, Power BI, Tableau, Python.

items.label: Logistics and Fulfilment Tech
items.description: Manages warehouse management systems (WMS), automated fulfilment, and delivery tracking integrations. Skills: ERP systems, API integration, process automation.

question.text: A small WA-based producer of handmade leather goods currently sells only at weekend markets. Which of the following is the MOST significant advantage e-commerce would provide to this business?
options: Eliminating the need to create and photograph their products
Removing the requirement to comply with Australian consumer law
Enabling sales to customers outside their local geographic area at any time of day
Guaranteeing lower prices than their competitors
question.explanation: The key advantage of e-commerce for a small, geographically limited business is breaking free of location and trading hour constraints. They can reach national and global customers 24/7 without the cost of a physical shopfront. Options A and B are incorrect (products still need presentation; consumer law still applies). Option D is not guaranteed — competition is fierce online.

question.text: Discuss how improved digital communications have contributed to the growth of e-commerce in Australia. In your answer, refer to at least TWO specific technological developments and explain their impact on both businesses and consumers.
question.explanation: Strong responses should identify specific technologies (e.g. NBN/5G — faster speeds enabling rich media, video product demonstrations; secure payment gateways — reducing consumer fear of online fraud; smartphones/mobile apps — enabling shopping anywhere; real-time logistics tracking — building post-purchase confidence). Each should be linked to a specific business benefit (lower barriers to entry, global reach, lower overheads) and consumer benefit (convenience, choice, price transparency). Avoid vague answers such as 'the internet got better'.

blocks.text: Society's Dependency on Digital Technology

Modern society is profoundly dependent on digital technology.
