Shade the cells corresponding to each task's start and end dates — use colour to distinguish task types (e.g. planning in blue, development in green, testing in orange, documentation in purple)
Mark all milestones with a distinct symbol (e.g. a bold border, a diamond cell, or a different fill colour)
Draw arrows or add a 'Depends on' column to show task dependencies
Identify and highlight the critical path — the sequence of tasks where any delay causes the whole project to be delayed
Screenshot or export the Gantt chart and include it in your plan of action document
Review the chart: is there any single week where you have more than you can realistically complete? Redistribute tasks now, not later.

question.text: On a Gantt chart, the 'critical path' refers to:
options: The tasks that are most interesting or technically challenging
The sequence of tasks that determines the minimum total project duration, where any delay causes the whole project to be delayed
The tasks that require the most expensive resources
The first and last task in the project only
question.explanation: The critical path is the longest sequence of dependent tasks in the project. Any task on the critical path that runs late directly delays the project completion date. Tasks not on the critical path have 'float' — they can be delayed slightly without affecting the end date.

question.text: Your capstone project has 5 weeks remaining. You have just missed your 'Wireframes complete' milestone because you spent too long on the homepage design. Describe the steps you would take to get the project back on track, and explain what planning strategies could have prevented this situation.
question.explanation: A strong response will include: immediate actions (reassess remaining tasks, identify what can be compressed or cut, communicate with the teacher, update the Gantt chart); and prevention strategies (setting sub-milestones within large tasks, using a priority matrix to avoid over-investing in one task, scheduling a weekly review of milestone status).

blocks.text: Resource Management in Practice

Identifying your resources in the plan of action is just the beginning. Managing those resources throughout the project — ensuring they are available when needed, used effectively, and not wasted — is an ongoing responsibility. In professional IT environments, poor resource management is among the most common reasons projects fail to deliver.

blocks.definition: The process of assigning specific resources (people, time, tools) to specific tasks. Effective allocation matches the right resource to the right task at the right time, without overloading any single resource.
A situation where the same resource is required by two tasks simultaneously, or where a resource is over-allocated (assigned more work than is physically possible in the available time).

rows: Your own skills and time; team members' skills
Skill gaps, unavailability, uneven workload distribution
Adobe suite, VS Code, Canva, Wix, WordPress
Licence limitations, version incompatibility, access from home vs school
Equipment unavailability, storage limitations, compatibility between devices
Missing assets blocking progress, copyright issues, wrong file format
Class periods, homework time, teacher consultation time
Competing demands from other subjects, unexpected absences

blocks.heading: Project Manager
blocks.text: If you are managing a team, resource management means actively checking on your team members — not assuming everything is on track. A weekly 5-minute check-in where each person reports their status prevents small problems from becoming project-ending crises.
Working in Teams

Most real IT projects involve teams. Even if your capstone project is individual, understanding team dynamics and professional collaboration skills is an assessed expectation in Year 12 General IT. These skills are also among the most frequently cited by employers as 'missing' in new IT graduates.

blocks.definition: A hierarchical decomposition of a project into smaller, manageable components. Each component is assigned to a team member, ensuring all work is accounted for and responsibilities are clear.

items: Define roles and responsibilities in writing before work begins — verbal agreements are forgotten
Assign tasks based on skills and interest, but ensure no team member carries a disproportionate load
Use a shared project management tool (Trello, Asana, shared Google Sheet) so all team members see the same current status
Set a regular team meeting schedule — even a 10-minute weekly check-in prevents misalignment
Document decisions made in team meetings — a brief set of notes keeps everyone accountable

blocks.text: Communication Protocols

Communication failures are the leading cause of team project problems in both schools and industry. A communication protocol is simply an agreed set of rules for how the team will communicate — which tools they will use, how quickly they will respond, and how decisions will be made.

rows: Each person updates their task card before the end of each work session
Group chat (Teams, Slack, or Google Chat)
Responses expected within 24 hours on school days
All client emails to be reviewed by the team before sending; keep copies on file
All project files saved to shared folder — never only on a personal device
All decisions recorded in writing with the date and who agreed

blocks.text: Version Control in Team Projects

blocks.definition: A system for tracking changes to files over time, enabling multiple team members to work on the same project simultaneously and revert to earlier versions if something goes wrong.

blocks.text: Every professional software development team uses version control. In IT courses, even if you are not writing code, version control principles apply to your design files, documents, and assets. The most common version control system is Git, hosted on platforms like GitHub or GitLab.

items: Never save over the previous version of a file without a backup — use filenames like 'homepage-v1', 'homepage-v2', or cloud version history
Commit your work regularly — frequent small saves are safer than one large save at the end
Write meaningful commit messages or file notes: 'Updated nav bar colours per client feedback 15 June' is more useful than 'changes'
Before any major change, create a saved checkpoint you can return to if the change does not work
If using Git: create a new branch for experimental changes, so the main project is always in a working state

blocks.heading: Industry Standard: Agile vs Waterfall
blocks.text: Agile teams use Git branching strategies (like GitFlow) to manage simultaneous work on multiple features. Each new feature gets its own branch; it is only merged into the main project once it is tested and approved. This keeps the main codebase always in a releasable state — a critical requirement for teams deploying live software.

blocks.title: Scenario: The Team in Trouble
blocks.scenario: You are three weeks into a group website project. Your team of three was assigned clear roles: you are the project manager and lead developer; Jamie is responsible for content and copywriting; Alex is responsible for visual design. At your week-3 check-in, you discover: Jamie has done none of the copywriting because 'I didn't know what style to write in'; Alex completed the design but saved all the files only on a personal USB drive that has now been lost; and your own development work is on track. The project is due in two weeks.

questions.question: Identify two project management failures that led to this situation. For each failure, explain what planning measure could have prevented it.

As project manager, write a brief action plan for the next two weeks that gives the project the best chance of delivering a complete product on deadline. Be specific about task reassignment and prioritisation.

After the project is completed, you hold a team retrospective (a review of what went well and what to improve). Identify two team collaboration protocols you would implement from the start of the next project, and explain why each would be effective.

blocks.title: Create a Team Resource and Collaboration Plan
blocks.intro: Whether your capstone project is individual or team-based, complete this resource management plan. Individual students should complete sections 1–4; team projects should complete all sections.
steps: Create a resource inventory table: list every tool, software application, and hardware item your project requires. For each, note: where it is accessed (school / home / cloud), whether access is confirmed, and who is responsible for it
For team projects: create a roles and responsibilities matrix. List each team member and their assigned tasks, skills they contribute, and the tasks they are accountable for completing
Write your team's communication protocol: which tools will be used for which purposes, expected response times, and how decisions will be documented
Design a file management system: choose a cloud storage platform, create a logical folder structure for your project files, and document the file naming convention your team will use
Identify your version control approach: describe how you will track changes to files and ensure previous versions can be recovered
Identify two resource risks (e.g. 'a team member is absent for a week') and write a contingency plan for each

question.text: A team member saves all their design files only on a personal USB drive and it is lost. Which project management practice would have prevented this problem?
options: A more detailed Gantt chart
A formal communication protocol requiring client approval before file changes
A file management protocol requiring all project files to be saved to a shared cloud storage folder
A more detailed scope statement
question.explanation: A file management protocol — part of resource management planning — specifies where project files must be stored. Requiring all files to be saved to a shared cloud folder (e.g. Google Drive, OneDrive) ensures that no single device failure can result in permanent data loss.

question.text: In project management, what is a 'Work Breakdown Structure'?
options: A chart showing how tasks are divided by the number of team members
A hierarchical decomposition of a project into smaller components, each assigned to a responsible team member
A list of the resources required for each task
A summary of the project's risk register
question.explanation: A Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) breaks the project down into its component parts in a hierarchical way — from the overall project to major phases, then to individual tasks. Each task is assigned a responsible owner, ensuring complete coverage of all project work.

blocks.text: What Does 'Done' Actually Mean?

One of the most important questions in any project is: how do we know when the product is finished? 'I think it looks good' is not a professional answer. Professional evaluation is criteria-based — you measure the product against specific, pre-agreed standards that were defined at the start of the project. This is why your evaluation criteria must appear in your plan of action, not be invented at the end.

blocks.definition: An assessment method in which a product or outcome is judged against a specific, pre-defined list of measurable criteria. Each criterion is either met, partially met, or not met — providing an objective basis for evaluation rather than subjective impressions.

blocks.text: Building an Evaluation Framework

Your evaluation framework should assess the product across multiple dimensions — not just whether it works, but whether it meets the client's needs, is usable, is professional in appearance, and was delivered on time and within constraints.

rows: All functional requirements from the specification are working
Test each requirement individually — pass/fail checklist
Target audience can navigate the product without instruction
Ask a real member of the target audience to complete 2–3 tasks and observe
Consistent colour scheme, typography, and spacing throughout
Compare against the approved design brief / style guide
Client confirms the product meets their expectations
Formal client sign-off or feedback form
Project was delivered on time; documentation is complete
Compare actual vs planned timeline; check all deliverables submitted
No broken links, no errors on load, appropriate file sizes

blocks.heading: Career Ready
blocks.text: In the IT industry, formal product evaluation is called Quality Assurance (QA) or User Acceptance Testing (UAT). QA testers are professional roles — IT companies pay specialists to ensure products meet their defined standards before release. Documenting your own evaluation process demonstrates this professional mindset to teachers and future employers.
Reflective Practice

blocks.definition: A professional habit of systematically reviewing your own performance and processes — identifying what worked, what did not, why, and what you would do differently next time. Used in teaching, healthcare, engineering, and IT.

blocks.text: Reflection is not self-criticism — it is a tool for professional growth. In your capstone evaluation, you are expected to evaluate both the product (how well does it meet the criteria?) and the process (how well did you manage the project?). Strong reflective evaluations acknowledge problems honestly and propose genuine improvements.

items: What went according to plan? Why did those things work?
What did not go as planned? What were the root causes?
Were there scope changes? How were they managed?
Were milestones met? If not, what caused the delays?
What would you do differently if starting the project again?
What skills did you develop through this project?

blocks.text: Client Presentation Skills

The final client presentation is the moment where all your work is translated into value for the client. No matter how technically excellent your product is, a poor presentation can undermine confidence in it. Conversely, a polished, confident presentation that walks the client through the product's features — relating each to their original requirements — demonstrates genuine professional maturity.

items: Open by revisiting the client's original need — show that you understood the brief from the start
Demonstrate the product live where possible — showing is more powerful than telling
Walk through each functional requirement and show it working
Acknowledge any limitations or constraints honestly — do not hide problems
Show your process documentation briefly — the plan of action, Gantt chart, and specification demonstrate professionalism
Invite questions and answer them directly — 'I don't know, but I'll find out' is an acceptable professional answer
End with clear next steps — what happens after today? (handover of files, training, future maintenance?)

blocks.heading: Career Ready
blocks.text: IT professionals regularly present technical work to non-technical stakeholders — clients, managers, board members. The ability to translate technical detail into plain language, and to tailor your communication to your audience, is one of the highest-value professional skills in the IT industry.
Portfolio Documentation

Your capstone project — the plan of action, the specification, the Gantt chart, the final product, and the evaluation — is evidence of your professional capability. Packaging this as a portfolio is a direct career asset. Year 12 graduates entering the workforce or further study with a documented portfolio are meaningfully ahead of those who cannot demonstrate their skills.

rows: You can scope, plan, and document a professional project
You can translate client needs into precise technical requirements
You can schedule complex, multi-task projects with dependencies and milestones
You have technical skills in design and/or development
You can assess your own work critically and professionally
You have worked with a real (or realistic) client successfully
You learn from experience and can articulate your growth — a key employability trait

blocks.text: Preparing for Industry Assessment Tasks

The Year 12 General IT Unit 4 assessment tests your ability to apply project management techniques under realistic conditions. It is a performance-based assessment — you will be judged on the quality of your documentation, your product, and your ability to explain your decisions. The following strategies will maximise your performance.

items: Start all documentation early — do not leave your plan of action until the project is nearly finished
Keep your process diary / progress log updated throughout the project — it is evidence and an assessment item
Review the marking criteria for each assessment task before you start — design your work to meet those criteria explicitly
Submit all components — an incomplete plan of action loses marks even if the final product is excellent
Practise your presentation to a peer before the real thing — timing, transitions, and clarity all improve with rehearsal
Save everything in multiple locations — losing your work is not an acceptable excuse for incomplete assessment

blocks.title: Scenario: Preparing for the Final Presentation
blocks.scenario: You have completed your capstone website project for the Riverside Cafe. The site has five pages: Home, Menu, About Us, Gallery, and Contact. You have a 10-minute presentation slot with Lisa (the client). Your plan of action, Gantt chart, and project specification are all complete. The contact form has a known bug — it does not send emails, but it does display a confirmation message to the user. You have tested the site on Chrome and Firefox, but not on Safari or mobile.

questions.question: Plan the structure of your 10-minute client presentation. List the sections you would include and the approximate time you would allocate to each. Justify your structure.

How would you handle the known contact form bug in the presentation? Write the exact words you would say to Lisa when you reach this feature. Your response should be honest, professional, and solution-focused.

Write three evaluation criteria for this project that could be used to formally assess whether the website is complete and successful. For each criterion, describe how you would measure whether it has been met.

blocks.title: Write Your Capstone Evaluation Report
blocks.intro: Complete a formal evaluation report for your capstone project. This report is a graded assessment component — it should be professional in language and rigorous in its analysis. A strong report is honest about shortcomings and proposes genuine improvements.
steps: Create an evaluation table: for each evaluation criterion from your plan of action, record whether it was met, partially met, or not met, and provide specific evidence (e.g. 'The menu page displays all 12 menu items and their prices as required by the specification')
Write a product evaluation section: discuss the finished product's strengths and weaknesses with reference to the client brief and specification
Write a process evaluation section: assess your project management — were milestones met? Was scope maintained? Were resources managed effectively?
Write a reflective section: identify the two most significant challenges you faced during the project and explain specifically what you would do differently next time
Include client feedback: if you presented to a client or conducted user testing, summarise the feedback received and note which items you addressed and which are 'future improvements'
Conclude with a 'Future Improvements' list: identify at least 3 specific improvements that would add value to the product in a future version, ranked by priority

question.text: Why should evaluation criteria be written at the start of a project, not at the end?
options: Because the teacher requires them to be in the plan of action
Because criteria written at the end tend to be designed to match what was built, rather than what was needed — removing their value as objective measures of success
Because it is easier to think of criteria before you start working
Because the client does not need to see the evaluation criteria
question.explanation: If you write evaluation criteria after the project is finished, you will unconsciously write criteria that match what you built — making the evaluation meaningless. Pre-defined criteria force you to measure the product against its original purpose and the client's actual needs.

question.text: During your capstone project evaluation, you discover that two of your five functional requirements were not fully met — the contact form has a bug, and one page is not displaying correctly on mobile. Write a professional evaluation entry for each issue. Your entries should acknowledge the problem clearly, provide evidence of testing, explain the likely cause, and describe either how you resolved it or what the next steps would be to resolve it.
question.explanation: A strong response will: acknowledge the issue without making excuses; describe specific testing evidence (e.g. tested on Chrome 124 on Windows 11, error appeared on iPhone 13 in portrait mode); identify a plausible technical cause; and propose a specific, actionable resolution (e.g. check PHP mail server configuration; use responsive CSS media queries to fix the mobile layout). Language should be formal and precise throughout.

blocks.heading: Project Manager
blocks.text: Congratulations on completing Unit 4 Project Management. The skills you have developed here — scoping projects, managing resources, communicating with clients, evaluating your own work honestly — are not just assessment skills. They are the professional habits of successful IT practitioners at every level, from junior roles to executive positions. Use this project as the foundation for your professional portfolio.
